card.name: RE: Meeting to discuss cytoskeleton analysis

card.desc:
Here is the information in preparation for the meeting.

Representative images can be found on OMERO under OK\_20x\_20260303. It is a dataset with 5 channels of some sagittal brain slices.

**What is the biological question you are trying to answer?**
Our lab is interested in autoimmune psychosis. As part of our clinical studies, we receive patient serum and CSF samples, which we screen for autoantibodies using a range of methods. One of our aims is to identify patterns in antibody binding across different patient groups and determine whether there are distinct antibody profiles associated with specific clinical cases.

**Briefly describe the samples you are imaging/have imaged.**
The images are from a tissue-based assay using mouse brain sections. Patient serum or CSF is used as the primary antibody source, followed by immunostaining and whole-slide scanning. We have been sending the slides to Donald for scanning.

**What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis?**
We quantify the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) in specific brain regions. Previously, this has been done by manually drawing ROIs in QuPath and extracting the MFI values. We are now trying to automate this process.

**With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?**
The images were acquired by Donald through the CALM STP as whole-slide scans.

**How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?**
Around 200 available training images from different scanning session.

**What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?**
The images contain the immunofluorescence channel used to detect patient antibody binding, along with the nuclear stain. The dataset includes both patient samples and control samples.

**Is there any reference paper you are following for the analysis?**
No specific analysis paper. Our current workflow has been developed in-house.

To give some additional context, I have already trained a QuPath pixel classifier that can accurately distinguish the hippocampus and surrounding regions. We found that analysing this area alone is sufficient to discriminate between cases and controls, and the MFI values extracted using the classifier correlate strongly with those obtained using manually drawn ROIs.

One problem I have had with the classifier is that both DAPI and AutoFL are necessary for distinguishing regions, but the AutoFL can be very variable between scanning sessions, how to overcome this?

The remaining challenge is that the classifier currently requires me to manually draw a polygon around the hippocampus before running the analysis. We were wondering whether QuPath has a way to automatically identify or generate this polygon so that the entire workflow could be fully automated.

@saras471 is going to present a poster on this at IMC21 in September: [https://www.imc21.org.uk/](https://www.imc21.org.uk/ "smartCard-inline")

We’ll starting pulling together some data soon. I’ve had a quick look at some of the “big errors” I was getting and it looks like a lot of these are down to the presence of transmitted light images in the training/test data. These can be difficult to filter out as the metadata on the IDR is not always complete. Right now, the IDR is undergoing maintenance, so probably not going to worry about it too much for now - might just flag these particular datasets in the data that we currently have, if possible. We should still have enough for a poster - aiming to get some “real” test data prior to IMC.
Haven’t heard anything further from Jose - putting this on hold.
Should probably put this one on hold, unless there’s been any further contact with Mireia since December?

Hi Minna,

Apologies, it looks like you were inadvertently left off the below thread!

To summarise, I think your instinct is correct - WebKnossos is for viewing/annotating data, not for long term archiving with a permanent identifier.

As Ken mentioned, for that sort of thing, we tend to point people toward BioImage Archive[https://www.ebi.ac.uk/bioimage-archive/](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/bioimage-archive/ "smartCard-inline") for most bio image data, but for this particular data, EMPIAR might be more appropriate: EMPIAR - Electron Microscopy Public Image Archive[https://www.ebi.ac.uk/empiar/](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/empiar/ "smartCard-inline")

Hope this helps. If you want to know more about WebKnossos specifically, I can put you in touch with the people behind it - there may be an easy way to get from WebKnossos to a permanent archive.

Can I ask who specifically you were in touch with in Andreas' lab?

Dave.

Hi Christine,

Following on from our conversation on Monday, please find attached a project agreement draft for a medium image analysis project to look at the HeLa datasets (#1-4).

From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 03 July 2026 10:52
To: Christine Dambietz <christine.dambietz@crick.ac.uk>; Rocco D'Antuono <rocco.dantuono@crick.ac.uk>; David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Christine BIA

Hi Christine,

Thanks again for your time yesterday. Just to recap, we've discussed the three main parts of your project as follows: 

  1.
Sample design - test different experimental conditions (passage, dosage) for two cell types (HeLa, neurons). These are multichannel 2D or 3D stacks.   2.
Sample design - test against lentivirus to evaluate infection dynamics. These are time series.   3.
Experimental output - evaluation of marker localisation in mitochondria and lysosomes. 

We also discussed that it might make sense to priorities the first part of the project for now, starting to work with a CellProfiler pipeline on the 2D dataset for HeLa cells. 

You also mentioned your plan for asking us to get one or multiple projects going to support you with the analysis. I'm attaching a template for the project work so that you can take a look at the information we'll need to get started. You can find the current project pricing at this link<https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1sMPvcBFGiFH3FI68NjZbcOOM439q6NJhBuGYSTCDTVU/edit?gid=1429924353#gid=1429924353>. 

Ahead of the meeting, please start thinking what are the questions you'd like to ask first, and what data we are going to use to answer! 

Talk on Monday!

Best wishes,
Stefania

data.text:
Hi Dave,


Thank you for your email. We are curious as to whether the Golgi fragmentation
we observe is due to direct effects of TDP-43 LoF on the Golgi or a more
indirect result of cytoskeletal disruption, especially the actin and tubulin
networks; as such we are trying to quantify whether either network is perturbed.
We tried looking at intensity, but we want to do more in-depth and perhaps more
appropriate analyses, such branching analyses.
In terms of our protocol, we’ve been using phalloidin and a-tubulin staining to
look at actin and tubulin. We’ve been using the LSM980 over at UCL for our
imaging.


I have attached one of our recent images to the email where a-tubulin was
stained in SK-N-BE(2) cells with doxycycline knocked down and imaged at 20x.  


Please let me know what other information you would like us to provide. Thank
you!


Best wishes,
Anson
Perfect, thanks for letting us know

data.text:
Hi Anson and Auri,

‌

Yes, this sound like something we can help with.

‌

Could you share your images with us and provide a bit more detail on exactly
what it is you want to do? For example, you say you’re looking at effects on the
cytoskeleton, but specifically what effects and what components of the
cytoskeleton? What stains have you been using in your IF and what system have
you been using to perform the imaging?

‌

Best,

‌

Dave.

**Analysis questions**

Hi Ajla,

Thanks for your time earlier. Please find below a recap of what we've discussed! 

Best wishes,
Stefania

---

  *
You can find a tutorial to create Fiji macros at this link<https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro>.   *
You can find more information about OnDemand on the HPC wiki<https://wiki.thecrick.org/display/HPCuserwiki/OnDemand>. They have a helpdesk on Mon, Wed and Fri morning in the café area, or you can slack them in the #hpc or #hpc-beginners channels to get set up. They offer a regular introductory course, available on Workday by searching "HPC Driving Licence"   *
To install software on OnDemand, you can follow the instructions on this page<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions>. Running the analysis on the virtual desktop will speed up computational time, especially when running many images (e.g., batch processing in ilastik)   *
Morphological filters (Process> Binary in Fiji) can be used to clean up the binary masks obtained from thresholding the ilastik probability masks. You can read more at this link<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/filter_morphological/index.html>.

**Follow up "FACS plots**

Hi Joyce,

A K-means clustering with 3 centres (one for each category) as detailed in this blog post<https://www.statology.org/how-to-perform-cluster-analysis-r/> should do the trick. I'm not a big expert in R, though, so let me know if you need further assistance, and I'll ask someone in BABS for helP! 

Best wishes,
Stefania

From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Friday, 3 July 2026 at 14:04
To: Joyce Man <joyce.man@crick.ac.uk>; Sara Salgueiro Torres <sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Follow up "FACS plots

Hi Joyce!

One way to decide your categories from this plot in an unbiased manner would be to run a cluster analysis<https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/data-analysis/cluster-analysis/>. You can do so in R or Python; let me know if you need more detailed directions. 

Alternatively, you can look back at the manual analysis you've run. When you considered a nucleus positive for a marker, what was its signal intensity? If you use those values as thresholds on your FACS plot, do you obtain a clear division between the 3 different categories? 

Finally, you could look at distributions of the green/magenta signal across all nuclei. If you were to set the threshold for positivity on the second or third quartile of the distribution, would that match what you had selected when analysing the data by eye? 

Whatever method you select, make sure to check whether you agree with the results by looking back at the images after you categorised each object. 

Best wishes,
Stefania

**Trajectory analysis**

Hi Julia,

Sorry for the delay in getting back to you!

I haven't managed to have a chat with Matt yet to enquire about the strategy he had in mind; I'll try again later today. 

I was recently contacted by Haonan in your lab, who told me he was working on an algorithm to analyse the same dataset, and it sounded like he was quite advanced in their approach for tracking single particles. Have you checked with them recently? 

A simple approach in Fiji could be to subtract the first frame from the last frame of your videos (Process> Image Calculator (subtract)) and measure the mean gray value of the output image (Analyze> Measure, you'll have to make sure "mean gray value" is selected in Analyze> Set Measurements). This can be used as a proxy for movement, as the resulting image will contain a higher signal the more the beads have moved. 

Cheers,
Stefania

**image analysis - meeting notes**

Hi Anais,

Thanks for your time yesterday. Please find below a recap of what we've discussed, let me know if you think I've forgotten anything! 

Best wishes,
Stefania

---

  *
Importing files in Fiji: please use Bio-Formats<https://www.openmicroscopy.org/bio-formats/> (File> Import> Bio-Formats) to open your raw files. This ensures that the metadata is read correctly.   *
You can run Fiji on OnDemand. You can find more information about OnDemand on the HPC wiki<https://wiki.thecrick.org/display/HPCuserwiki/OnDemand>, and you can find instructions on how to install and run Fiji on OnDemand at this link<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions/Fiji>.   *
You can take a look at this tutorial<https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro> if you're interested in automating the analysis using Fiji macros.   *
To analyse the green/red puncta in your images, we discussed the following pipeline:      *
Label the nuclei
     *
Label the puncta on both green and red channels
     *
Measure the puncta per nuclei
  *
To label any structure we follow these steps:
     *
(optional) pre-process the image using filters. We looked at Gaussian blur<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/filter_convolution/index.html> (Process> Filters> Gaussian Blur) for the DAPI channel. This aims at reducing the noise in the image to improve segmentation      *
Semantic segmentation. We are splitting the image histogram into two parts by using an algorithm to locate a threshold<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/binarization/index.html>. The resulting image is called a binary mask, and it's going to contain only two values, 0 for background and 255 for foreground. Choose an algorithm from the list (Image> Adjust> Threshold) that selects the regions of the image you want to select.      *
(optional) post-process the binary masks. You can use morphological filters<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/filter_morphological/index.html> to clean the binary masks (Process> Binary)      *
Instance segmentation. We can assign IDs to each object to analyse them separately. To do this, we exploit a plugin, MorphoLibJ<https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj>, which allows us to create a label mask<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/connected_components/> from the binary mask using connected component labelling (Plugins> MorphoLibJ> Binary Images> Connected Component Labeling).      *
(optional) post-process the label masks. MorphoLibJ has a few tools to clean labels based on area (Plugins> MorphoLibJ> Label Images> Label size filtering) and to clear the image border (Plugins> MorphoLibJ> Label Images> Remove Border Labels)   *
To measure the size and shape of puncta, we can use Plugins> MorphoLibJ> Analyze> Analyze Regions 3D. This also allows to obtain object centroid, which you'll need to compute any distances between puncta. All output from MorphoLibJ can be exported as csv for further analysis.   *
To measure whether puncta belong to the nuclei, we can use the approach detailed in this blog post<https://forum.image.sc/t/is-there-a-way-to-count-the-number-of-small-objects-inside-large-objects-in-a-3d-image-by-morpholibj/8857/2>, with Plugins> MorphoLibJ> Analyze> Intensity Measurements 2D/3D.   *
You can get assistance with statistics from the wonderful people in the BABS team<https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/our-crick/bioinformatics-biostatistics>.   *
We have an upcoming Fiji workshop<https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/seminar/image-analysis-introduction-fiji-image-analysis-2026-07-31t083000> on July 31st, if you're interested. 

The Francis Crick Institute Limited is a registered charity in England and Wales no. 1140062 and a company registered in England and Wales no. 06885462, with its registered office at 1 Midland Road London NW1 1AT

data.text:
**Christine BIA**

Hi Christine,

Thanks again for your time yesterday. Just to recap, we've discussed the three main parts of your project as follows: 

  1.
Sample design - test different experimental conditions (passage, dosage) for two cell types (HeLa, neurons). These are multichannel 2D or 3D stacks.   2.
Sample design - test against lentivirus to evaluate infection dynamics. These are time series.   3.
Experimental output - evaluation of marker localisation in mitochondria and lysosomes. 

We also discussed that it might make sense to priorities the first part of the project for now, starting to work with a CellProfiler pipeline on the 2D dataset for HeLa cells. 

You also mentioned your plan for asking us to get one or multiple projects going to support you with the analysis. I'm attaching a template for the project work so that you can take a look at the information we'll need to get started. You can find the current project pricing at this link<https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1sMPvcBFGiFH3FI68NjZbcOOM439q6NJhBuGYSTCDTVU/edit?gid=1429924353#gid=1429924353>. 

Ahead of the meeting, please start thinking what are the questions you'd like to ask first, and what data we are going to use to answer! 

Talk on Monday!

Best wishes,
Stefania

data.text:
### Meeting, following Edith/IAG/EHP/SEAI meeting, 30/6/26

Stefania, Sara, Joyce, Katherine Courtis. Email recap:

Hi Joyce and Katherine,

It was great to have a chat with you earlier today – looking forward to what comes out of this project! Here is the usual post-meeting recap. 

_**What to focus on**_

There is a lot that you could tackle, but whilst EHP helps you troubleshoot and check the potential effects of fixing on your samples, it’s worth continuing to analyse your new batch of images (i.e., GFP staining before mCherry). 

You can simultaneously explore other imaging modalities with Ale (Brainsaw/Lightsheet) to acquire a 3D dataset of your tissues. That will give you more confidence on whether the spatial distributions you are seeing are real. 

Based on all this information, that will inform you whether it's worth acquiring multiple sections of one tissue sample or not. Always start simple and build on what you see. 

**‘FACS’ plots**

Looking at the distribution of your data in a “FACS” type of manner will help you see in an unbiased way how your current pipeline results are clustering. In QuPath:

- Create a new project with all the images > run the same workflow with the object classifiers > extract detection measurements 
- This CSV file will have a row per cell, and columns for both channel intensities and object classification outcome (red/green positivity).
  - You can either plot a datapoint per cell, or per average region of tissue (the XY coordinates are also exported).
- It will be a huge file, so use either Python or R. We can help you with that if needed!

This will inform us a bit more on what normalisation types we can try for the dataset, if any. 

**FIJI macro to automate cell counting** (confocal dataset)

What you’ve done manually, can fairly easily be done in a more unbiased, automated way (and much faster!). You’ll need to have the [MorphoLibJ](https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj "‌") plugin installed, which you did already. 

As Stefania demoed during the meeting, the main steps would be to:

1. Open file with BioFormats (_Plugins > BioFormats > BioFormats importer_)
2. Create a Maximum Intensity Projection (MIP) for all channels (_Image > Stacks > Z Project_)
3. Separate the channels (_Image > Color > Split Channels_)
4. Apply a filter to your nuclear channel, DAPI (P_rocess > Filters > Gaussian Blur_). You can start with a sigma = 5. 
5. With the newly smoothed image, threshold to get binary mask (_Image > Adjust > Threshold_). Find an algorithm in the dropdown menu that works well for your images
6. Now you have a binary mask, and you need to distinguish between different objects – add a unique ID to each of the nuclei (_Plugins > MorphoLibJ > Binary Images > Connected Components Labeling_). 
7. You can “clean up” your resulting labelled mask to exclude things by size (_Plugins > MorphoLibJ  > Label Images > Label Size Filtering_) and remove objects touching the edge (_Plugins > MorphoLibJ  > Label Images > Remove Border Labels_).
8. Once you’re happy with the labelled mask, you can measure the green and/or red intensity inside those nuclear objects:

- _MorphoLibJ > Analyze > Intensity Measurements 2D/3D_)
- Input = the ORIGINAL UNTOUCHED fluorescent channel to measure (i.e., green or red)
- Labels = your final nuclear labelled mask
- We recommend you select “Number of Voxels” or “Volume” to measure to record the area of the objects that you’re measuring. That helps filter out from your dataset very small/big objects

1. You can save the resulting file as a txt file for downstream analysis (_File > Save As_).

   10. Repeat for your other channel of interest

 All of this can be recorded (_Plugins > Macro > Record,_ before you run any processing steps) and then transformed into a macro to batch analyse a folder of images. [Here’s](https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro "‌") the tutorial that Stefania mentioned that helps you transform the recorder output into a fully functional script. 

**Image Analysis projects**

We also work on a project-basis, where instead of supporting you doing the analysis, we do the work for you and teach you how to take over once the pipeline is set up. So if you see that you don’t have time to analyse it yourself before the end of your stay at the Crick in August, we can set up a paid project for it.

‌

I’m aware this is quite a lot of information, so if you get stuck at any point, feel free to reach out to us!

Best wishes, 

Sara

data.text:
**Cell segmentation**

Hi Julia,

Thanks for your time earlier today. As promised, here's a recap of what we've discussed during the meeting. If you need any further assistance, please don't hesitate to get in touch. 

Best wishes,
Stefania

---

  *
We've discussed that CellProfiler<https://cellprofiler.org/> might be the most suitable software for your analysis. To prepare the images for CellProfiler, we need to separate the channels and save them as tif files. We can use Fiji for this task: we open the files with Bio-Formats, perform a Z-projection (Image > Stack > Z-project > Max intensity), separate the channels (Image > Color > Split channels) and save each image as .tif.   *
You can wrap all of these operations in a macro to automate the task for all images in a folder. You can do so yourself, by following the tutorial at this link<https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro>, or you can have a go at the macro you used in the CellProfiler course<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/multi-dimensional-imaging-and-analysis/blob/main/Colocalization/SplitChannels.ijm> that you attended in the past.   *
In CellProfiler, you will first need to load the images you want to analyse. You will then need to check the filename patterns to associate each channel to the correct name (e.g., "C1" in the filename means that that channel contains DAPI). If you need help on how to do this, you can find tutorials on this page<https://cellprofiler.org/examples>.   *
I'm attaching both the project (.cpproj) and the pipeline (.cppipe) that we created together during the meeting. You can use those as guidance. Briefly, we first detected the primary objects (nuclei) and created a segmentation mask; then we detected the secondary objects (cells) by expanding the primary until the edge of the cytoplasmic signal is reached; we then subtracted the nuclei masks to the cell ones to obtain the regions of interest of the cytoplasm only (tertiary objects). These are the regions of interest where to quantify the YAP and P73 signal. Finally, we quantified both morphological (e.g., area) and intensity characteristics for each object and exported them in a csv file. The file contains one row per object, so it'll get quite big! Consider using a dedicated software such as R or Python to analyse this, instead of Excel, as you might hit the limit of what Excel can do quite quickly with such large files. The wonderful folks in BABS<https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/our-crick/bioinformatics-biostatistics> can assist if needed.   *
You can find information on how to run CellProfiler on the HPC at this link<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions/CellProfiler>.

Subject: Cell segmentation

Hi Stefania,

Hope you are well.

I was wondering if you had some time this week to meet with me briefly to help me with some image analysis. 

I want to segment cells to quantify the fluorescence intensity of a marker expressed both in the nucleus and cytoplasm and was wondering if you had a way of semi-automating this? I know how to do it manually in Fiji and sort of in QuPath but the cells are non-uniform so don’t always pick up the right shapes of the cells. 

Many thanks,
Julia

From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 09 June 2026 17:19
To: Ajla Dzanko <ajla.dzanko@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Imaging analysis questions

Hi Ajla,

Thanks again for your time earlier!

Please find below a short recap of what we've discussed earlier. 

  *
The pipeline you are currently using needs to take as input a Nikon file and create a binary mask, highlighting where the crypts are in the image. You have 3 channels available, one for nuclei, one for actin and one for cells at the base of the crypt. Segmenting on the actin channel might be a good solution, as it nicely highlights the structure. Alternatively, we could merge all of the channels, but this is a slightly more involved approach.   *
The first thing we discussed was how to retain the metadata from the acquisition. The easiest way is to open the raw file with Bio-Formats in Fiji (Import> Bio-Formats). You can then select the channel of interest, duplicate it, and save it as a tiff. This process can be automated for all series in an image and for all images in a folder using a Fiji macro. Please let me know if you'd need assistance with this. Here<https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro> you can find a protocol on how to use the macro recorder to create and adapt a macro for automation.   *
You then need to obtain a binary mask from the tiff image obtained above. This can be done either in Fiji with a simple thresholding, by selecting one of the default algorithms (Image> Adjust> Threshold) or with Ilastik. Binary masks can be cleaned by using morphological filters, like "Fill Holes" or erosion and dilation (Process> Binary), or by filtering out objects based on their shape or area (Analyze> Analyze Particles).   *
If you decide to create the binary in Ilastik, I would suggest running a pixel classification workflow on multiple images representative of different samples. I would work with a single channel (e.g., actin) and start by selecting all the features available (you might need to remove the ones which go beyond the image size in Z). You can add sparse annotations on all images in the project and use the live prediction to check the output. Once you're happy, you can set up the prediction export - it might be a good idea to work with h5 files (default, you need the ilastik plugin for import), but tiff files should also work. The output of Ilastik is a probability map with one channel per label. Setting a threshold on the probability (e.g., 0.6) will result in a binary mask, which can be fed into the MATLAB code.   *
It looks like the metadata is not retained from Ilastik, but that doesn't seem to be the issue, as MATLAB is reading the file as a matrix without expecting any metadata, which you're supposed to initialise at the start of the code.   *
I think the problem you're observing is related to the MATLAB code not using the initialised metadata correctly; however, I couldn't find the problem during the meeting. If you'd like to send me the code and an example binary mask, I can look into this. Just to clarify, I don't think there's anything wrong with the code itself. More likely, some details have changed in the way you acquire the data compared to your colleague, and this change has to be reflected somewhere in the code for it to work properly. 

Please let me know if you have any questions, and whether you'd like me to take a look at the code! We can also meet again if it would make things easier. 

Cheers,
Stefania

card.name: 2026-10-06 SPAOM (ext)
Using the Single Neurite Tracer plugin for two-channel timelapse
Using the Single Neurite Tracer plugin for two-channel timelapse

data.text:
Hi Magnus,

Thanks for your time just now! Below is a quick recap of what we've discussed. Please let me know if you think I've forgotten anything. 

Best wishes,
Stefania

  *
The most likely reason why your images are not successfully tracked by SNT is the low signal-to-noise ratio. It's possible that this can be improved by modifying sample preparation or image acquisition (e.g., light power, dwell time, resolution, objective, microscope, etc.). For image acquisition support, the best option is to come down next Monday to the CALM helpdesk where you can chat with the acquisition team (Cricknet event here<https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/seminar/calm-imaging-and-analysis-helpdesk-2026-01-12t100000>).   *
The official documentation for ilastik has a great pixel classifier tutorial<https://www.ilastik.org/documentation/pixelclassification/pixelclassification>. I might suggest trying this on your current data to see how far it gets you while you see whether improving the signal-to-noise ratio is an option. You will need to train the model on separate frames from different movies to then deploy it on all the unseen frames. Make sure to add annotations for training on frames from the start and later on in the movies so that the model learns to account for bleaching.   *
You can get around the problem of ilastik misclassifying specific regions by adding an additional class for those, or by cleaning the mask in Fiji afterwards with morphological operations on the binary.   *
There are bleach corrections<https://imagej.net/plugins/bleach-correction> you can apply before segmentation. They tend to affect the histogram, so make sure to quantify the original raw signal once you've located the regions of interest and segmented them.   *
You can use ilastik on OnDemand following the instructions here<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions/Ilastik>   *
SNT is probably the most suitable open-source package to analyse filamentous structures, so don't give up on that =) You can find the list of all computable metrics at this link<https://imagej.net/plugins/snt/metrics>. You can find some additional information on time series on this image.sc post<https://forum.image.sc/t/snt-time-lapse-utilites/47974>.

From: Magnus Jhaveri
Sent: 15 June 2026 13:51
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Using the Single Neurite Tracer plugin for two-channel timelapse 

Hi,

Hope this finds you well!
I'm Magnus Jhaveri, a PhD student in the Serio lab. I've collected some 2D timelapse footage of a photoconvertible fluorophore in growing axons and am hoping to segment the axons as they grow using SNT in FIJI. The aim would be to compare the ratio of photoconverted/unphotoconverted populations within the segmented timelapse of growing axons. I tried briefly in ilastik, but feel SNT may offer more consistent neurite segmentation across frames, particularly as my signal-to-noise is not great. 

The struggle I'm having is refining my paths on SNT, as axon width varies along the length. Would it be possible to book a slot with someone later this week to walkthrough SNT with some of my data as an example? 

All the best,
Magnus

card.desc:
**SUMMARY**

- **Biological question**: understanding how and why autosomal random
  monoallelic expression (aRME) occurs
- **Model**: mouse model, gene of interest (Bag3) is coupled to a cleavable, nuclear-localised GPF or mCherry on either allele.
- **Samples**: mouse brain and heart tissue samples, EHP IF-stained.
- **Acquisition**: Olympus slide scanner (Donald, CALM)
- **Image analysis output: count/analyse portion of nuclei showing**

1. **GFP/mCherry**
2. **GFP only**
3. **mCherry only**
   **in brain and heart tissue**

data.text:
### 1st meeting (19/6/26):

Email recap:

Hi Alexia,

Thank you for your time today. As I mentioned, here is a recap with some useful links on what we discussed.

**Visualising images in FIJI**

As mentioned previously, using the Crick’s HPC cluster is the best way forwards. Once you have access to OnDemand, the instruction on how to install FIJI on NEMO can be found [here](http://: "‌"). 

OnDemand is a useful resource to run other image analysis software, like _napari,_ or potentially even your lab’s python scripts. 

‌

**Other 3D visualisation & analysis software** – _napari_

[_Napari_](https://napari.org/stable/ "‌") is a great free, open-source tool built-in python that was made to visualise and analyse multi-dimensional images. 

- The best way to install it is through the terminal (instructions [here](https://napari.org/stable/getting_started/installation.html#install-python-package "‌")).
- You can also now install it as a standalone application, but it means some plugins won’t be available (instructions [here](https://napari.org/stable/getting_started/installation.html#installation-bundle-conda "‌")). 
- If you have nice nuclear segmentation from your lab’s pipeline, you can save the nuclear mask and import it into napari to overlay it onto your images.
- It also has some very nice animation [tools](https://napari.org/napari-animation/ "‌") to showcase your images and results in 3D

It can also be installed on OnDemand!

**3D foci counting**

[Spotiflow](https://github.com/weigertlab/spotiflow "‌") is worth trying. It has a plugin that is meant to work in 3D inside [napari](https://github.com/weigertlab/napari-spotiflow "‌"), which makes it much more user friendly than interacting with the terminal. 

‌

‌

**Radial intensity profile** **analysis in 3D**

I couldn’t find a standalone package that can make 3D ring ROIs for your nuclei. The way forwards could be doing it in python, via napari. A suggested workflow for the future:

1. Segment your nuclei (which your pipeline already does)
2. Compute the Euclidean Distance Transform from the nuclear surface inwards (to get a map of distances of a voxel to the edge). This can be done with SciPy in Python ([script.ndimage.distance\_transform\_edt](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.ndimage.distance_transform_edt.html "‌")) .
      - You’ll need to apply this in each nuclear binary mask in 3D
3. Normalise distance per nucleus (so it’s comparable across different nuclear sizes) and bin into different “rings”
4. Quantify signal of interest in each of those rings

I hope that makes sense. If there is anything else you need assistance with in the future, don’t hesitate to reach out to us again.

Have a great weekend, 

Sara

data.text:
**Elyanne Ratcliffe - Image Analysis discussion**

Oh, and you can find attached the QuPath project we played around with earlier! You will need to point the software at the path of the local copy of your data for it to work. It should be straightforward once you open the project in QuPath and follow the prompts, but please let me know if you need assistance with this. 

Cheers,
Stefania
________________________________
From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 18 June 2026 12:33
To: Elyanne Ratcliffe <ratcli@mcmaster.ca>
Subject: Re: Elyanne Ratcliffe - Image Analysis discussion

Hi Elyanne,

Thanks again for your time earlier!

As promised, please find below a recap of what we've discussed. If you think I've missed anything, please let me know, and feel free to get in touch with any further questions. 

Best of luck with your return to Canada! Please let us know if there are any opportunities for training over there. 

Best wishes,
Stefania

  *
The pipeline to analyse your data should have the following components:      *
Segment the objects of interest on the DAPI channel (Cell detection)      *
Classify each object based on its intensity or other features in channels other than the DAPI (Cell classification)      *
Export the results as tabular data for further data analysis
  *
We discussed two separate datasets. Both should be suitable for analysis in QuPath. If you wanted to explore further options, the second dataset can also be segmented in Ilastik and further analysed in Fiji or Python, like we did in the course.   *
QuPath should be sufficient for your needs. Make sure to have clear research questions to avoid getting lost in the very rich output you're going to obtain from this dataset! To set up a new project in QuPath, you can look at the documentation on this page<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/projects.html>. You will want to have all the images pertaining to an experiment in the same project if you want to analyse them simultaneously.   *
You can find a tutorial to perform a similar pipeline to the one we looked at in the meeting at this link<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html>. Both traditional and machine learning classifications are explained. The pipeline can run on specific regions of the image, called "Annotations" in QuPath. You can draw these manually, or you can use a Pixel Classifier to detect regions of the image which contain signal, or a specific anatomical feature (more details here<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/thresholding.html>).   *
You can automate the analysis to run on all the images in a project by using QuPath workflows and scripting. More information here<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/scripting/index.html>. QuPath developers are also very active on image.sc<https://forum.image.sc/tag/qupath/1025>, and often support users with scripting tasks.   *
You can export tabular data from QuPath after running the analysis on a single annotation, multiple annotations or multiple images, using the Measure>Export Measurement menu. More on measurements at this link<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/concepts/measurements.html>. Measurements can be exported by annotation or by detection; one raw per either will be created in the output file. I would advise using either R or Python to analyse the output tabular data, as the files tend to have a very high number of rows.   *
The QuPath extension InstanSeg<https://github.com/instanseg/instanseg> and associated publication might also be worth taking a look, if you find that standard Cell detection in QuPath fails in regions where cells are very packed (provided these regions are of interest for your biological question).   *
You can find instructions on how to set up QuPath on OnDemand on this page<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions/QuPath>. You might be able to follow similar instructions to set up locally back in your home institution, or to share these instructions with your IT department for support if you have local HPC resources.   *
For the second dataset, we discussed working with 2D images instead of 3D. This would be computationally cheaper, and there wouldn't be too much loss of information, as the z-stacks mostly contain one layer of nuclei (no overlapping cells in z). To go from 3D to 2D, you can use max intensity projections or select the middle slice of a z-stack. More information on projections here<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/projections/index.html>.

From: Elyanne Ratcliffe <ratcli@mcmaster.ca>
Sent: 17 June 2026 14:07
To: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Elyanne Ratcliffe - Image Analysis discussion

External Sender: Use caution.

Hello Stefania,

I am looking forward to working with you tomorrow and for using images from my lab group in Canada to consolidate concepts. 

I am attaching a link to a zip file with several images -  I hope this works. 

Western DR - is a cross section of rat intestine - with a focus on neurons in the myenteric plexus. The red is CB1 (cannabinoid receptor 1) and the green is HuC/D. -The challenge here was to quantify the CB1 staining - there is also staining in the epithelium (we’re still sorting if “real” vs artifact). It looks like all the HuC/D neurons are CB1 positive - how best to be convinced that’s true. We’ve also been challenged to determine how much of then CB1 is in the neurons vs in other cells in the region of the myenteric plexus. -Also - I realize it’s not a Z stack but just one image (the section was 10 um) - would it better to just and do this analysis on a Z stack even if a cross section? 

Whole mount Z stack (x10, x20, x80). Myenteric plexus of the mouse intestine. Stained with Hu (neurons), PGP9.5 (neuronal fibres), CB1 and DAPI. -The goal is to quantify the density of neurons (cell bodies and fibres) -Quantify the expression of CB1
-Resolve where the CB1 is expressed (neurons or other structures in the ganglia) 

I’ll keep preparing for tomorrow but wanted to send this before too late in the day. With appreciation, 

Elyanne

Hi,

Hope this finds you well!
I'm Magnus Jhaveri, a PhD student in the Serio lab. I've collected some 2D timelapse footage of a photoconvertible fluorophore in growing axons and am hoping to segment the axons as they grow using SNT in FIJI. The aim would be to compare the ratio of photoconverted/unphotoconverted populations within the segmented timelapse of growing axons. I tried briefly in ilastik, but feel SNT may offer more consistent neurite segmentation across frames, particularly as my signal-to-noise is not great. 

The struggle I'm having is refining my paths on SNT, as axon width varies along the length. Would it be possible to book a slot with someone later this week to walkthrough SNT with some of my data as an example? 

All the best,
Magnus

data.text:
### Initial meeting follow-up (17/6/26)

Hi Nina,

Thanks for your time today. As promised, here is a recap with useful links on what we discussed.

**Morphological analysis**

[SNT](https://imagej.net/plugins/snt/ "‌") (FIJI) is your best bet. It’s very actively maintained and has a very wide range of functionalities. Just a few notes:

- You will need to have the Latest version of FIJI for it. Worth having two copies in your machine (some plugins still work best with Stable).
- It also works in 3D.
- The[ soma detector](https://imagej.net/plugins/snt/auto-tracing#soma-detection "‌") function in particular might be very well suited to quantify those ‘mystery’ blobs along your Dendra2 signal.
- It also works in 3D.
- It does work nicely in manual mode, which we don’t necessarily recommend, but might become useful as a sanity check (especially for your new DiI experiment).

**Directionality analysis**

Two packages are worth trying here:

1. Dave’s [_TWOMBLI_](https://github.com/wershofe/TWOMBLI "‌")_:_

- It’s available as a FIJI plugin, and has a [tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCftZ5Ksy9vNcK8PiPq9XdnQ "‌") on how to use it too. 
- It was developed to look at extracellular matrix patterns, but it includes a broad range of descriptors that overlap nicely with neuronal morphology concepts.
- Further details in the original paper [here](https://www.life-science-alliance.org/content/4/3/e202000880 "‌").

  B. Stefania’s [AFT](https://github.com/OakesLab/AFT-Alignment_by_Fourier_Transform "‌") (_Alignment by Fourier Transform_):

- It doesn’t go via segmentation, as Stefania showed, but it might be useful to get a number on ‘directionality’ and apply it across your whole stack.
- It has implementations in both Python and MATLAB.
- More information in the original paper [here](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2021.745831/full "‌"). 

**Statistics**

Worth noting again that BABS has free statistics support that is incredibly useful once you get some numbers. You just need to reach out to their biostatistics email address. 

You have a lot of analysis work ahead of you, but if you need any further support, just reach out to us at any point.

Best of luck!

Sara

data.text:
Hi Minna,


The dataset that are being stored and made accessible through webknossos at the
Crick are being managed by Carles (Carles.Bosch@crick.ac.uk) who is attached to
Andreas Schaefer lab.
There data are pretty massive and hence they need to use a cloud based format
called OME-Zarr and with webknossos, they are able to collaborate with other
external collaborators and they can view and access the image data.  I believe
that their image data will be managed and financed by their project grant
and the lab funding.


We were interested in setting up webknossos as a visualisation tool for large
multi-dimensional image data that are stored in our OMERO image database.
However, we eventually believe that they do not satisfy our requirements, so we
have not use or pursue of setting up a webknossos client.


Within the STP CALM is concern, we do not want to compete with EMBL-EBI-BioImage
Archive (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/bioimage-archive/) a repository that is funded by
UKRI and other EU governments for the specific purpose of long-term availability
and open access. Therefore we recommend our users to submit their image data to
BioImage Archive for publication and for FAIR access.


At the moment we are actively working with our users and help them to submit
their image data that are stored in OMERO database to BioImage Archive to
support their publications.


Best regards
Ken

## PU attendee (Mahhum):

From supervisor, Simon Cleary (KCL):
Dear Sara,

Thank you for organising this.

I have registered with the hope that this could be on behalf of my student Mahhum Saqib who is using Qupath to develop a new method for quantifying tissue remodelling in fibrosis.

This is part of a project I recently started with Alessandro Ciccarelli funded through Bioimaging UK.

I am away the day the workshop is held. Would it be possible for you or a colleague to register Mahhum ([mahhum.saqib@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:mahhum.saqib@kcl.ac.uk "‌")) as a visitor and pick her up from security so she can enter and attend?

Super grateful for you considering this and organising this useful training. I understand if this is not possible. 

Best wishes,

Simon

old.desc:
Dear image analysis group, 


I hope this email finds you well!


I acquired .vsi files from the Olympus Inverted Sora from the microscopy
facility. However, I was struggling to open it on my FIJI through the VPN
because of their sizes (from 30GB to roughly 100GB for one single image). I
talked to the microscopy team, and they suggested me to contact you. They
mentioned the possibility to reach via VPN a FIJI installed on the computer
containing the data (data located on //data2.thecrick.org/lab-vermotj).


In addition, we are using a homemade interactive tool in the lab for several
quantifications and segmentations that runs locally with python. Do you think
there will be any way to 'download' the data in order to use our tool? The
microscopy team mentioned OMERO, if I remember well. 


Many thanks in advance for your help, 


Kind regards, 
Alexia PIGEOT

card.desc:
- We are imaging fixed and stained zebrafish embryonic hearts - either extracted from the fish or in the fish.
  This could lead to images of 4 to 6 tiles and, dependently of the sample, between 150 and 500 Z-stacks (but I used a very small stack of 0.1µM at 40X with SORA = 128X).
  I had 4 channels in total. 
- I'm looking at chromatin organisation into the nuclei. The biological question is then: how are chromatin and histone marks organised into the nuclei? I would like to quantify the global intensity of my signal over my sample, quantify intensity and number of foci I can observe depending on heart regions, signal localisation into the nuclei themselves - more central or accumulating at the nuclear periphery.

Shared images: '\data\STPs\light_microscopy\outputs\Alexia\_June\_2026'.

It's an example of an isolated zebrafish heart at 48hpf. It contains 4 channels (taken from far-red to blue to reduce photobleaching):

- NHS-ester-ATTO-647N: labelling all the proteins
- H3K9me3-Alexa-594: my signal of interest
- Fli1a:H2B-EGFP-Alexa-488: which labels my cell type of interest = endocardium
- DAPI

data.text:
Hi Sanja,

We can definitely help you with that! What’s your availability like next week? I have more availability Monday, Tuesday, and Friday, but it might have to be via Zoom if that’s okay. 

Before the meeting, could you provide some extra information on your project by answering the questions below? On top of that, it would be great to see some representative images so I can have a look at what’s ‘doable’. You can share some files by putting them in your lab’s NEMO space, in `stp/light-microscopy/outputs`​. If you create a folder there, I’ll be able to access the images. 

- _What is the biological question you are trying to answer?_ 
- _What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your RNAscope analysis? Any reference paper you are following?_
- _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?_ 
- _How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?_ 
- _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Dimensions (z-stack, time)? Conditions/Controls?_
- _Do you have any workflow/pipeline for your image analysis so far?_ 

Best wishes, 

Sara

card.name: Constantinos Savva (Downward lab) - course follow up
Constantinos Savva (Downward lab) - course follow up

data.text:
I met with Kamla today and we went through the imaging settings and things to improve on the acquisition side, to get cells steady and collect z-stack without floating cells. The goal of the experiments we have been doing is acquired data on one of the confocal and measure the total volume of red blood cells, to check whether there is a difference between healthy and diseased. We have explored two methods, get z-stacks of individual cells and measure the area above the threshold with FIJI, or use watershed 3D with napari. In today’s meeting I have helped Kamla to include one of the 3D rendering in the poster for a conference, but have told that we work in the same team with Stefania, so we are happy with whoever she want to get helped with. Stefania invited me to the follow up meeting so we can both get her advice and see how we can better help the user.

![RBC\_3DRendering.webp](https://trello.com/1/cards/6a267ba8bffd557a0fd7fabc/attachments/6a2997e464a214b770fd1809/previews/6a2997e464a214b770fd181a/download/RBC_3DRendering.webp)

From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Wednesday, June 10, 2026 5:01 PM
To: Constantinos Savva <costas.savva@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36ntm98wi3ne4nxbh4o+24ddkjcz8e@boards.trello.com <stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36ntm98wi3ne4nxbh4o+24ddkjcz8e@boards.trello.com> Subject: Re: image analysis follow up

Hi Costas,

Thanks again for your time earlier!

As promised, please find below a recap of what we've discussed. If you think I've missed anything, please let me know, and feel free to get in touch should you have any further questions. 

Best wishes,
Stefania

  *
The pipeline to analyse your data should have the following components:      *
Segment the objects of interest on the DAPI channel (Cell detection)      *
Classify each object based on its intensity or other features in channels other than the DAPI (Cell classification)      *
Export the results as tabular data for further data analysis
  *
QuPath should be sufficient for your needs. Make sure to have clear research questions to avoid getting lost in the very rich output you're going to obtain from this dataset!   *
You can find a tutorial to perform a similar pipeline to the one we looked at the meeting at this link<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html>. Both traditional and machine learning classifications are explained. The pipeline can run on specific regions of the image, called "Annotations" in QuPath. You can draw these manually, or you can use a Pixel Classifier to detect regions of the image which contain signal (more details here<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/thresholding.html>).   *
You can automate the analysis to run on all the images in a project by using QuPath workflows and scripting. More information here<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/scripting/index.html>. QuPath developers are also very active on image.sc<https://forum.image.sc/tag/qupath/1025>, and often support users with scripting tasks.   *
You can export tabular data from QuPath after running the analysis on a single annotation, multiple annotations or multiple images, using the Measure>Export Measurement menu. More on measurements at this link<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/concepts/measurements.html>. Measurements can be exported by annotation or by detection; one raw per either will be created in the output file. I would advise using either R or Python to analyse the output tabular data, as the files tends to have a very high number of rows.   *
There are pipelines out there (like MuSpAn<https://www.muspan.co.uk/>) which can help with setting up further spatial analysis. The QuPath extension InstanSeg<https://github.com/instanseg/instanseg> and associated publication might also be worth taking a look.   *
You can find instructions on how to setup QuPath on OnDemand on this page<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions/QuPath>.

Cc: stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36ntm98wi3ne4nxbh4o+24ddkjcz8e@boards.trello.com <stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36ntm98wi3ne4nxbh4o+24ddkjcz8e@boards.trello.com> Subject: Re: image analysis follow up

Dear Stefania,

I hope you are well and thank you for your email.

In my project, I treated obese and non-obese mice with breast cancer with immunotherapy, metformin, combination or no treatment and wanted to see if the addition of metformin improves immune cell infiltrates in the tumour. 

I have collected the tumours and performed multiplex IF for lymphocyte markers (CD8 and PD1) and macrophage markers (F4/80 and C11b) plus DAPI for nuclei. 

The spectal annotations are:
1 CD11b 520
2 F4/80. 780
3 PD-1 570
4 CD8 690

My aim primary aim is to quantify the immune cell densities of the lymphocytes (CD8+PD1+ and total CD8+) and macrophages (F4/80+CD11b+, F4/80+CD11b- and total F4/80+) in these samples. The secondary aim is to extract the coordinates to assess the distances of the immune cells from the tumour centre (I want to see if the immune cells move from edge to the centre of the tumour. 

The format of these images are in qptiff format.

I also copied a couple of images in the X:\data\STPs\light_microscopy\outputs\Costas 

My lab is lab-anastasioud.

Thank  you once again.

Looking forward to meeting you,

With kind regards

Costas

Hi Costas,

Thanks for booking your course follow up meeting for tomorrow! Ahead of the meeting, if you have time, could you please send me a short description of your project, what type of images you have or plan to acquire, and what kind of analysis you'd like to achieve? If you have any example images you'd like to send across, you can do so by email or by uploading them to the lm/output/ folder in your lab space on Nemo. 

I appreciate that there's not much time before the meeting, so if this is not possible, we can discuss directly tomorrow. 

Thank you!

Have a great day and week ahead,
Stefania

data.text:
Hi Sara! 

Many thanks for your reply and sorry for the delay! 

I will definitively contact the HPC team to get access to the remote desktop via OnDemand - which I never accessed before. 

Will keep you posted once I get access to it, in order to check together how we can install Fiji on it 🙂

Yet, I downloaded the files in the second biggest resolution from the pyramid onto my personal computer - after waiting for a long moment to open it on my personal Fiji through the VPN. Seems to work like this - and will work even better if I'm able to convert my .vsi images through the HPC! Will also keep you posted if I finally need access to OMERO.

To answer your questions: 

- We are imaging fixed and stained zebrafish embryonic hearts - either extracted from the fish or in the fish.
  This could lead to images of 4 to 6 tiles and, dependently of the sample, between 150 and 500 Z-stacks (but I used a very small stack of 0.1µM at 40X with SORA = 128X).
  I had 4 channels in total. 
- I'm looking at chromatin organisation into the nuclei. The biological question is then: how are chromatin and histone marks organised into the nuclei? I would like to quantify the global intensity of my signal over my sample, quantify intensity and number of foci I can observe depending on heart regions, signal localisation into the nuclei themselves - more central or accumulating at the nuclear periphery.

We can definitely have a chat together, that could be really useful!

However, could we please reach through a Zoom chat? I'm not often at the Crick since we are a satellite group mainly localised at Imperial College South Ken. 

Many thanks and best wishes, 

Alexia

data.text:
**Imaging analysis questions**

Hi Ajla,

Thanks again for your time earlier!

Please find below a short recap of what we've discussed earlier. 

  *
The pipeline you are currently using needs to take as input a Nikon file and create a binary mask, highlighting where the crypts are in the image. You have 3 channels available, one for nuclei, one for actin and one for cells at the base of the crypt. Segmenting on the actin channel might be a good solution, as it nicely highlights the structure. Alternatively, we could merge all of the channels, but this is a slightly more involved approach.   *
The first thing we discussed was how to retain the metadata from the acquisition. The easiest way is to open the raw file with Bio-Formats in Fiji (Import> Bio-Formats). You can then select the channel of interest, duplicate it, and save it as a tiff. This process can be automated for all series in an image and for all images in a folder using a Fiji macro. Please let me know if you'd need assistance with this. Here<https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro> you can find a protocol on how to use the macro recorder to create and adapt a macro for automation.   *
You then need to obtain a binary mask from the tiff image obtained above. This can be done either in Fiji with a simple thresholding, by selecting one of the default algorithms (Image> Adjust> Threshold) or with Ilastik. Binary masks can be cleaned by using morphological filters, like "Fill Holes" or erosion and dilation (Process> Binary), or by filtering out objects based on their shape or area (Analyze> Analyze Particles).   *
If you decide to create the binary in Ilastik, I would suggest running a pixel classification workflow on multiple images representative of different samples. I would work with a single channel (e.g., actin) and start by selecting all the features available (you might need to remove the ones which go beyond the image size in Z). You can add sparse annotations on all images in the project and use the live prediction to check the output. Once you're happy, you can set up the prediction export - it might be a good idea to work with h5 files (default, you need the ilastik plugin for import), but tiff files should also work. The output of Ilastik is a probability map with one channel per label. Setting a threshold on the probability (e.g., 0.6) will result in a binary mask, which can be fed into the MATLAB code.   *
It looks like the metadata is not retained from Ilastik, but that doesn't seem to be the issue, as MATLAB is reading the file as a matrix without expecting any metadata, which you're supposed to initialise at the start of the code.   *
I think the problem you're observing is related to the MATLAB code not using the initialised metadata correctly; however, I couldn't find the problem during the meeting. If you'd like to send me the code and an example binary mask, I can look into this. Just to clarify, I don't think there's anything wrong with the code itself. More likely, some details have changed in the way you acquire the data compared to your colleague, and this change has to be reflected somewhere in the code for it to work properly. 

Please let me know if you have any questions, and whether you'd like me to take a look at the code! We can also meet again if it would make things easier. 

Cheers,
Stefania

data.text:
### Initial enquiry response:

Hi Alexia,

Apologies for the delayed response – you caught us during a busy week!

Here are some notes on working with your large .vsi files:

**Viewing & analysis**

We have a High Performance Computing cluster available at the Crick. It's your best option for handling heavy image files. You can access these powerful compute nodes through a remote desktop via [OnDemand](https://ondemand001.nemo.thecrick.org/pun/sys/dashboard "‌") (’NEMO Desktop’). Have you used it before? It’s linked to your NEMO storage, where your lab images are stored, so no need to download your files, plus you get the power to process big files.

- If you haven’t, you can reach out to the HPC team (best way is via slack, #hpc) to get onboarded.
- We can then help you [install FIJI ](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/Image-Analysis-Group/software_instructions/Fiji/ "‌")on it.

**Getting your Python tool working**

[OMERO](https://omero-prod.camp.thecrick.org/ "‌") is what we would suggest using, it’s ideal for this problem. It’s an image server that stores your microscopy images.

- If you are not registered yet, I can put you in touch with our ‘OMERO guru’ Ken to create an account and upload your images.
- There is an OMERO Python API, which means you can pull tiles or specific Z-planes from the image server rather than having to download the whole file. We can have a look at your interactive tool to try implementing an option to import images directly from OMERO rather than locally. 

It would really help to have a quick conversation to understand your dataset better:

- _What does your image data look like – how many Z-slices, timepoints, channels… roughly?_ 
- _What samples are you imaging?_
- _What is the research question you are trying to answer? What measurement(s) are you aiming to get out of your image analysis pipeline?_ 

I’m happy to sit down with you next week to go through this together and map out the best way forwards. Let me know how that sounds.

Best wishes, 

Sara

data.text:
**Stitching brainsaw data**

Hi All,

Elisa, did you get a chance to have a look at the last output I produced?

Either way, I've now packaged everything I've done up and put it on Github: [https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/brainsaw-data-processing](https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/brainsaw-data-processing "smartCard-inline")

You can now try the stitching yourself on NEMO. There's a bit of setup involved (installing FIJI, setting up a python environment), but it should be pretty straightforward and you only need to set things up once. After that, running everything should be very straightforward.

The github repo contains a few test inputs and outputs that you can use to verify everything is working as it should be.

This is designed to replicate the section-by-section stitching that the brainsaw software has done successfully in the past, so the output will be a fused 3D image (a single TIF, but this can be modified if necessary) for each section. We could also add functionality to stitch together sections, so we end up with a single volume per sample - I think someone in the SE&AI team has been working on something to do this with EM data, so that should be applicable - I'll check. But the fused sections should definitely be enough for you to get started on segmentation and downstream analysis.

Let me know what you think,

Dave.

Yasemin booked a chat with me for next Tuesday before the below email exchange - I'll add you both to the calendar invite and you can move if it doesn't suit.

Dave.

From: Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 01 June 2026 16:19
To: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>; Gregory Mashanov <Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk>; David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi Yasemin,
You can put some data into your outputs folder for light microscopy, it'll be in your lab folder under outputs/lm and then if you let us know when you do that, we can take a look at it. 

Would it be possible to have a chat with you, and Greg? I have done some similar analysis in my life, but it was a bit ago. This type of analysis though is what Greg's been doing since before the Crick was the Crick, so he's the best suited to ask. He also has a wealth of knowledge on doing TIRF (which, again, I haven't done since 2017) so he's the one you want to talk to ASAP to make sure you get your experimental controls done properly so you can make proper measurements of fluorescence lifetime and stoichiometry estimates. 

Cheers,
Todd

________________________________
From: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk<mailto:yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 01 June 2026 15:02
To: Gregory Mashanov <Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk<mailto:Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk>>; David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>>; Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk<mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi David, all,

Thanks very much for your email.

To add a bit more detail on the work, I am using TIRF microscopy to image fluorescently labelled recombinant proteins in a reconstituted DNA replication system assembled from purified protein components on DNA, which is flow-stretched on the coverslip. The aim is to track individual protein molecules at the replication fork during DNA replication. 

I have already started acquiring data and would be happy to share representative movies if helpful - could you let me know the best way to share these? This paper (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6943181/) is very similar to my experimental setup and the type of data I acquire, as well as the type  of analysis I am aiming to implement. 

At present, the lab mainly uses Fiji/ImageJ for image handling and basic manual analysis, but there is not yet an established pipeline for this type of single-molecule tracking. 

The main outputs I am trying to extract from these movies are: tracking of labelled proteins, dwell/residence times at the replication fork, exchange dynamics between bound and unbound states, stoichiometry estimates intensity/photobleaching behaviour, and rates of DNA replication. 

I have not yet done any detailed image analysis of the datasets, as I have mainly focused on establishing the experimental system so far. My background is in biochemistry, so I am still building up experience on the image analysis side. 

Looking forward to hearing from you.

Best wishes,
Yasemin

From: Gregory Mashanov <Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk<mailto:Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Thursday, 28 May 2026 at 10:00
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>>, Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk<mailto:yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>>, Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk<mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hello everyone,

I can recommend you to visit this website www.mashanov.uk<http://www.mashanov.uk/> 

Where you can find free single molecule analysis software, very relevant references and single molecule imaging examples 

Yours

Greg

Gregory Mashanov

Senior Microscopist (Crick Advanced Light Microscopy (CALM)), SW312 

The Francis Crick Institute

1 Midland Road, London, NW1 1AT

(Postage: SW302, 1 Brill Place, London, NW1 1BF)

E: gregory.mashanov@crick.ac.uk<mailto:gregory.mashanov@crick.ac.uk> 

W: www.mashanov.uk<http://www.mashanov.uk/>

Y: https://www.youtube.com/@mashanov

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-70925-2.pdf

________________________________

From: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 28 May 2026 09:39
To: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk<mailto:yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>>; Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk<mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>>; Gregory Mashanov <Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk<mailto:Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi Yasemin,

I'm copying in @todd Fallesen<mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk> and @gregory Mashanov<mailto:Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk> who probably have more experience with this kind of analysis than me. 

Can you give us  a little bit more background and a bit more detail on what you've done so far and what you're planning to do? 

Have you already begun imaging and acquiring data? If so, could you possibly share some image data with us? 

Have you already tried to analyse the results in any way? If so, what software have you used? What approaches do other people in your lab use? Are there any previously published methods that you are trying to implement? 

There are some general approaches to tracking we use quite a lot, but the kind of trajectory analysis you're looking for is a bit more niche. The tracking bit is, in a sense, the "easy" bit and there are a few different options for that, but analysing the trajectories to extract "molecule tracking, stoichiometry, and dwell-time analysis" is definitely not "standard" - it's probably doable with a mix of different python packages (for example), but I doubt there's a single tool out there that can do all this. 

For example, there's PySTACHIO from Mark Leake's group in York: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2001037021002944. Mark's a very nice guy and he's well known in the UK microscopy community, so that might be a good place to start? But I haven't used it myself, so can't comment on the specifics of the software. It doesn't seem to be terribly well documented, but it does seem like it's being actively developed, which is a good sign. 

In the past, people would have generally written their own code and scripts to do this kind of analysis (as myself, Greg and probably Todd can attest to), but let's see if we can't reuse what's already out there before heading down that route (or at least keep the amount of new code to a minimum - no sense reinventing the wheel). 

Greg/Todd - anything to add?

Dave.

From: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk<mailto:yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 27 May 2026 16:06
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi David,

Many thanks for your email.

Yes, we haven't met yet. I am currently a visiting scientist and incoming postdoctoral fellow in the Yardimci Group. I have been working on single-molecule TIRF experiments and hope to extract and automate data analysis for molecule tracking, stoichiometry, and dwell-time analysis, which I understand are fairly standard approaches in single-molecule analysis. 

Since I am new to the data analysis side of things (my background is in biochemistry), I thought it would be very useful to have a chat with you. 

I have also been waitlisted for the Introduction to Image Analysis course next week and have not yet been enrolled. I was wondering whether there would be any update on the enrolment. 

Best wishes,
Yasemin

I don't believe we've met?

Happy to have a chat with you, but if you could give me some information on what you need assistance with prior to our meeting, that would be helpful. 

Best,

Dave.

Konstantina

**Konstantina Nikolatou (She/Her)**

Postdoctoral Research Associate

Cell and Tissue Mechanobiology Laboratory
The Francis Crick Institute/ King’s College London

London
NW1 1AT

**From:** Sara Salgueiro Torres <[sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Date:** Thursday, 28 May 2026 at 18:38
**To:** Konstantina Nikolatou <[konstantina.nikolatou@crick.ac.uk](mailto:konstantina.nikolatou@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Cc:** Crick Image Analysis Group <[bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk](mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Subject:** Re: Troubleshooting QuPath error

Hi Konstantina,

That’s all good – this is quite confusing to get your head around the first time!

I see you are indeed working inside your lab space, and that your model file is also in there. Based on your screenshots, try inputting this as your model path in the Stardist script now (line 21):

/data/home/users/nikolak/Histology/QuPath Directory/dsb2018\_heavy\_augment.pb

That should hopefully do the trick. If not, getting rid of the space in your directory folder might also help (e.g., \`QuPath_Directory\` instead of \`QuPath Directory\`).

If you are still getting stuck, or this is becoming too confusing, we can schedule a quick Zoom meeting tomorrow/next week to quickly go through it together. 

Best,

Sara 

**From:** Konstantina Nikolatou <[konstantina.nikolatou@crick.ac.uk](mailto:konstantina.nikolatou@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Date:** Thursday, 28 May 2026 at 18:13
**To:** Sara Salgueiro Torres <[sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Cc:** Crick Image Analysis Group <[bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk](mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Subject:** Re: Troubleshooting QuPath error

Hi Sara, 

Sorry I can not figure that out.

I do see that the QuPath user directory I had was set to 

 /data/home/users/nikolak/Histology/QuPath Directory which I assume is the “home” directory.  

 I thought I had previously selected a folder in my lab’s folder on NEMO (what I think is the “lab space”, correct? ) and there is actually a folder on NEMO set up for just that.  So what I thought I had set up was:

card.name: EC-mito colocalisation with vesicles (iSIM) – Gisela Tsoi
EC-mito colocalisation with vesicles (iSIM) – Gisela Tsoi
EC-mito colocalisation with vesicles (iSIM) – Gisela Tsoi

data.text:
### Initial meeting

Recap:

Hi Gisela and Matt,

As mentioned, here’s a recap of what we discussed today. I’m copying in [\@Matt Renshaw](mailto:Matt.Renshaw@crick.ac.uk "‌"), who helped get you set up on the iSIM, so he’s in the loop before you head back to the microscope. 

‌

**Main goal** 

To quantify how many free mitochondria are in close proximity to extracellular vesicles (EVs) released by astrocytes.

**Establishing “extracellularity”**

The big bottleneck with your current images is the lack of a good marker to identify the astrocyte cell body, i.e., a mask that determines what is “inside” vs “outside” the cell. Without this, you can’t confidently identify which mitochondria are truly extracellular.

A few routes forward on this:

- Ideally you optimise the Halotag method. If that is not feasible, trying to find a marker to target with immunostaining is a good workaround. I can see a few candidates when having a quick scan through Google.
- As a sanity check, your older construct (which labels the cell fill and secreted EVs) could be useful here. If the extracellular objects it labels overlap with what your new immunostained marker picks up, that gives you more confidence you’re capturing the right things. 
- Once you have a working astrocyte cell-body marker, we can make a mask out of it to exclude any mitochondria/EV objects that fall inside the cell.

‌

**Image acquisition!**

It’s very important to make sure you can resolve the structures you are trying to analyse before you acquire a big dataset. Loop someone in from the CALM acquisition team to check your resolution and settings once you have a promising test sample.

When you have a good image you are happy with, come back to us first – we can work through the analysis on that single image before you commit to imaging more. 

_**To 3D or not to 3D?**_

This is worth thinking about carefully. If a mitochondrion sits directly above or below an EV in Z, but isn’t actually touching it, a 2D MIP will make them look like they’re in contact when they’re not.

That said, given your Master's timeframe, simplicity matters. We can revisit this once we see what your data look like, but keep it in mind when you are deciding your imaging settings. 

Don’t hesitate to get in touch if anything is unclear, or come down to our biweekly helpdesks, where we have people that deal with image analysis, acquisition (+ other STPs). Next one is this Monday, 10-11am in the cafe area. 

Best of luck!

Sara

Hi Konstantina,

It looks like the issue now is that you’re missing a space between \`/data\` and \`/camp/apps/\`.

It’s much easier copy/pasting into the Terminal. You can change your permissions permanently in Chrome so your Nemo Desktop can access your local clipboard, where you copy the line to input in the Terminal. It’s a bit trickier with Safari, they’re much more aggressive with these type of permissions, but you can use OnDemand’s Clipboard:

1. Open the side menu by clicking the arrow on the left-hand side of the Desktop
2. Click on the Clipboard symbol
3. Paste here the Terminal line to open QuPath: singularity run --nv -B /nemo/lab/elosegui-artolaa:/data /camp/apps/containers/QuPath/0.7.0/qupath_0.7.0.sif
4. Close the side window by clicking on the arrow again
5. In the Terminal, right click and select “Paste"

![Screenshot 2026-05-28 at 10.24.05.png](cid:1651154268*1DDF21B9-C039-42D6-B4B3-1C4B58BC87AD)

Hopefully that helps with typo mistakes. Fingers crossed that lets you use QuPath smoothly.

Best wishes, 

Sara
