old.desc:
From: David Barry
Sent: 28 May 2026 09:40
To: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>; Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>; Gregory Mashanov <Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi Yasemin,

I'm copying in @todd Fallesen<mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk> and @gregory Mashanov<mailto:Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk> who probably have more experience with this kind of analysis than me. 

Can you give us  a little bit more background and a bit more detail on what you've done so far and what you're planning to do? 

Have you already begun imaging and acquiring data? If so, could you possibly share some image data with us? 

Have you already tried to analyse the results in any way? If so, what software have you used? What approaches do other people in your lab use? Are there any previously published methods that you are trying to implement? 

There are some general approaches to tracking we use quite a lot, but the kind of trajectory analysis you're looking for is a bit more niche. The tracking bit is, in a sense, the "easy" bit and there are a few different options for that, but analysing the trajectories to extract "molecule tracking, stoichiometry, and dwell-time analysis" is definitely not "standard" - it's probably doable with a mix of different python packages (for example), but I doubt there's a single tool out there that can do all this. 

For example, there's PySTACHIO from Mark Leake's group in York: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2001037021002944. Mark's a very nice guy and he's well known in the UK microscopy community, so that might be a good place to start? But I haven't used it myself, so can't comment on the specifics of the software. It doesn't seem to be terribly well documented, but it does seem like it's being actively developed, which is a good sign. 

In the past, people would have generally written their own code and scripts to do this kind of analysis (as myself, Greg and probably Todd can attest to), but let's see if we can't reuse what's already out there before heading down that route (or at least keep the amount of new code to a minimum - no sense reinventing the wheel). 

Greg/Todd - anything to add?

Dave.

card.desc:
Gisela: I am looking to automate the characterization of extracellular mitochondria for properties such as 1. percentage of colocalization with vesicle markers (CD9, integrin b1) and 2. the size of such vesicles. Immunofluorescent images are acquired from fixed primary mouse astrocytes and astrocytes + neurons co-culture, on iSiM at 150x.

---

I'm looking at extracellular mitochondria released by astrocytes, which seem to be predominantly enclosed within extracellular vesicles (EVs). The current problem with visualizing EVs is the lack of a universal marker, and in my pilot experiments CD9 and integrin beta-1 (ITGB1), compared to other common EV markers, seem to co-localize with mitochondria more readily. Therefore I am looking to quantify the extent of these two EV markers representing extracellular mitochondria (for other future experiments that involve these surface markers). 

I have uploaded a few examples of images that I have on hand currently, with the ones labeled "CC" being astrocyte + neuron co-culture and without "CC" being astrocyte-only mono-culture. Astrocytes are transduced with Halotag\_P2A\_mito-StayGold while neurons are transduced with mTagBFP2.

Images were acquired in 4 channels:

far red (cyan) — Halotag (astrocyte cell fill);

red — EV markers (CD9/ITGB1 by immunostaining);

green — mito-StayGold (astrocyte mitochondria)

blue — DAPI in mono-culture; mTagBFP2 (neuron cell fill) in co-culture

Initially I was also planning to distinguish non-enclosed mitochondria and EV-enclosed mitochondria using Halotag (because EVs tend to carry cytoplasmic content) but the Halotag protein expression is suboptimal and pretty much unusable in current state (my previous viral construct with good cytoplasmic expression unfortunately had non-specific expression for mitochondrial tag). Therefore at this stage I am just aiming to quantify the extend of co-localization of EV marker and extracellular mitochondria in general. As for the co-localization, the EV markers and mitochondria marker usually do not overlap completely (as seen in the examples), so the criteria would be the two being in proximity (in contact / partial overlapping). I also have EV marker secondary-antibody-only sample as control so these can be used for thresholding.

data.text:
Hi Konstantina,

Not running it from the container might explain the error. The cluster is missing some libraries that QuPath v0.6 and above need, so it only runs properly in the container that you access with our [instructions](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/Image-Analysis-Group/software_instructions/QuPath/#accessing-your-data-in-qupath "‌"). 

Let’s give that a go and see if it resolves the issue. From the screenshot you attached, I see that the line is throwing an error because there is a mistake in the path where the .sif file is located – you have \`nemo/apps\` instead of \`camp/apps\`. The correct line would be:

singularity run --nv -B /nemo/lab/elosegui-artolaa:/data /camp/apps/containers/QuPath/0.7.0/qupath_0.7.0.sif

Give that a go and let me know how it goes.

Best wishes,

Sara

Yes, please see attached screenshot

Yes

- _What QuPath version are you running on OnDemand? Did you follow _[_these instructions_](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/Image-Analysis-Group/software_instructions/QuPath/ "‌")_?_ 

 It’s v070. I could not get it to run with the singularity, could that be the issue? I tried several times and eventually I only managed to open it  If I remember correctly by running ml QuPath - enter- QuPath.

When I try to follow the instructions I get stuck on Step 4 (see second screenshot attached)

Apologies, I am not sure how to export the error (either that on QuPath or the one on the terminal) as txt. I can not copy + paste them on my word for instance and I can not seem to find some obvious way to export them as such.

Best wishes,

Konstantina

If not I’m happy to have a look at her specific project and see whether I can
replicate the issue.


Best,
Sara

data.text:
Hi Konstantina,

Ahead of our meeting next week, I’ve put together a Jupyter notebook for you to test different approaches: [https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Object-Midline-Detection](https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Object-Midline-Detection "smartCard-inline")

I don’t know how familiar with such things, but you can run it directly from your browser if you click on the “launch binder” button. I’ve added plenty of documentation around the code - you don’t need to understand all the code, but hopefully you can understand the accompanying explanations of what each section of code does.

The main parameters you should focus on playing around with are listed under the “Parameters” heading – these influence what the detected objects will look and, ultimately, this determines how successful the detection of midlines through each structure will be. So have a go at modifying the parameters in that section to see how they influence the results.

We can go through everything next week.

Best,

Dave.

From: David Barry <[David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Sent: Monday, May 11, 2026 5:03:40 PM
To: Konstantina Angoura <[konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures

Hi Konstantina,

I should stress that I just used pulled out the core function from the script and ran that with the default parameters – there are a whole bunch of parameters that can be tweaked which will influence the result.

But, there are many, many other approaches that could be used for this. However, it’s unlikely any of them are going to be perfect – they will all have their associated advantages and disadvantages.

With that in mind, it probably makes sense to try a few different approaches and see how this influences the overall result (of the downstream analysis). In other words, if two different approaches produce two slightly different midlines in the same section, how does this influence the classification of cells, niches, etc. in the subsequent analysis and, more importantly, does it change the overall conclusion.

This is probably the kind of thing you can experiment with yourself if you’re prepared to learn a little Python (with our support, of course)?

Dave.

Hi Dave!

Thank you for this! Hm, it works better on the DAPI but I am not sure it as accurate as we would want it.. we ideally want it to draw the line from the very tip of the structure to the other tip, like going from stem to edge of a banana.. hopefully, we can find a way to draw a better line so we can extract the coordinates and group cells based on position relative to the midline for James’ analysis… If there is a more accurate way of approaching this than the macro, then we are super keen to try. We want a nice smooth curved line going through the midline of each structure, if possible. Let me know if I can do anything to help! We can set up another meeting (and have Naomi and James join) if helpful! Thank you, again!

Best wishes,

Konstantina

card.name: Intro FIJI follow-up – Gisela Tsoi

data.text:
### 1st booking post-workshop

Hi Gisela,

I just received your booking for a meeting with me on the 28th May. At some point before then, could you share some information and context on your project?

- _What is the biological question you are trying to answer?_
- _What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis? Any reference paper you are following?_
  - I see you mentioned “% colocalisastion of extracellular mitochondria with vesicle markers”. What exactly do you mean by that? From the mitochondria outside of cells, which ones share the same space with vesicles?
  - And are we looking for absolute overlap, or just proximity (e.g., side-by-side)?
- _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?_
- _How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?_
- _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Dimensions (z-stack, time)? Conditions/Controls?_
  - It’s particularly important to have negative controls, i.e. images where you  expect zero true colocalisation. It will help us set the threshold for what sounds as “real” overlap vs random background noise.
- _Do you have any workflow/pipeline for your image analysis so far?_

‌

Could you then also share some representative images you are interested in analysing, so I can have a quick look ahead of our meeting to check the quality and what is doable for your image analysis. You can share files through your lab’s space NEMO storage. You should have a folder called stp, and inside it go to light-microscopy/outputs. You can then make a folder in there, with a representative name (e.g., gisela\_for\_sara). Just remember computers don’t like weird characters or spaces! Anything you put in that directory I’ll be able to see.

Thanks!

Best wishes,

Sara

‌

_———_

_This meeting was scheduled from the bookings page of Sara Salgueiro Torres._

Use the following link to reschedule or cancel this meeting:
[Manage meeting](https://bookings.cloud.microsoft/bookwithme/user/d4b915488d774ba3bb17791e0c9e1b93@crick.ac.uk/booking/4Z9ClsV_1UajBge2FZrYVg2?anonymous "‌")
\-----Note added from booking page on Friday, 22 May 2026 11:23-----
I am looking to automate the characterization of extracellular mitochondria for properties such as 1. percentage of colocalization with vesicle markers (CD9, integrin b1) and 2. the size of such vesicles. Immunofluorescent images are acquired from fixed primary mouse astrocytes and astrocytes + neurons co-culture, on iSiM at 150x.

### Follow-up 21/5/26:

Sara, recap:

Hi Lies,

Sending you the usual post-meeting recap, although it’ll be very brief this time (a great sign!).

**Sphericity filtering of 3D labels in napari**

Your colocalization results are looking very promising. The variability between samples is expected, but to put your mind at ease, it’s always good to visualise your resulting masks that you are measuring. As mentioned, napari is great for this, but filtering out 3D labels is not an option in the usual '[Filter labels by properties](https://napari-hub.org/plugins/napari-filter-labels-by-prop.html "‌")’. Two options to try:

1. [Segmentation toolbox](https://napari-hub.org/plugins/napari-segmentation-toolbox.html "‌") plugin – I haven’t tried it myself, but it seems to measure sphericity and do label filtering, so sounds promising. Worth a try.
2. Doing it through the napari console with a little Python script.

- If option 1 doesn’t work, let me know and we can try putting together a bit of code for it. 
- Then we also ensure it’s the same mathematical formula used for your FIJI macro results. The MorphoLibJ 3D sphericity formula is on their [documentation](https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj "‌").

**Controls**

Another important way to make your results more robust, is by including “biology-driven” controls (I can’t think of a better name for them right now). Having a condition where you are certain the phenotype of interest changes in a specific direction, gives you confidence on the reliability of your analysis pipeline. On top of that, it puts the biological effects you see into perspective.

- Example 1 – to assess how “real” the change in neuronal volume is you could try adding a neurotoxic compound that somehow inhibits neuronal growth. You would also have to test the effect of this drug on your samples via a non-imaging way, like a quick assay/flow/PCR... If not, one could argue your compound is expired or has the wrong effect on your tissue.
- Example 2 – including a much older timepoint where you are certain inflammation is over.

I am aware it is not as easy as “going back to the lab and setting up another sample”. But worth thinking of a simple treatment, or just collect a sample from that same mouse much later on, to put both your pipeline and results into perspective. 

Whenever you have advanced a bit more in your image analysis project, or if you want to tackle your other 2D project, reach out to us again and we can reconvene.

Good luck!

Sara

data.text:
**Stitching brainsaw data**

Hi All,

I think I've managed to resolve the metadata issue. The solution is a bit clunky and involves resaving the data, but it does seem to work. @juan Carlos Boffi[http://mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk "‌"), I'll share the script I've been using to do this so it can be forwarded to Rob so they can see exactly what the issues are.

I've also started trying to fuse together multiple sections - I've put an example here: proj-miguel-aliaga-brainsaw/working/barryd/fused\_tp\_0\_ch\_0.tif

The registration is not perfect (I think things are moving around in Z a little too much, but we can constrain that), but all the tiles at least look like they are in more or less the correct location.

So, it should be possible to run this on the entire dataset, but that would probably take quite a long time to run. So it probably makes sense to just register the tiles with relevant information (i.e. exclude empty tiles). For now, I'll specify an ROI containing a fly and try to get a slightly better registration of just those tiles - that should run a bit quicker. Going forward, it should be possible to automate this.

@elisa de Launoit[http://mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk "‌"), could you first take a look at the file above and make sure it looks somewhat sensible - everything is in approximately the correct location in x, y and z, right?

Dave.

Hi Dave,

Sorry I didn't answer your email sooner; I was at a conference in the US all last week. Thanks for the update, and no worries about the delay,  glad to hear NEMO maintenance is behind you.

Your hypothesis about two-channel acquisition corrupting the metadata makes a lot of sense. My single-channel scan two weeks ago stitched without issues, which lines up with what you're describing, and it would also explain why other groups haven't run into the same problems.

Hopefully Rob's input via @juan [http://mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk "‌") will help move things along too.

Looking forward to your results!
Best,
Elisa

Hi David,
Thank you for the update. I have forwarded the metadata concerns you voiced to Rob Campbell (the maker of the Berainsaw platform). Cheers,
Juan
Verification/Validation of metadata on IDR, some of which is clearly incorrect

### Merging spots in proximity, query + fix:

Hi Jenna,

I played around with the settings, and I think I found a workaround. At least in the last MIPs you shared with me. I’m attaching the updated CellProfiler pipeline.

I essentially added quite an aggressive smoothing onto the masked foci image to merge spots that are close together. Then I changed the parameters in the following IdentifyPrimaryObjects module so it fits the smoothed image better. An example of what I could achieve:

**Pre-smoothing**

![before\_smoothing\_jenna.png](cid:2337131564*A9499CEF-AE6D-49A8-9637-8167D92B19DA)

**After smoothing:**

Hi Sara, 

Yes I have an example here attached- the cell at the top left I would count as 2 dots. Then the one next to it I would also count as 2, even though there are 2 small dots next to each other. Same with the cell at the bottom. Do you have any other thoughts now that you have seen an example?

**From:** Sara Salgueiro Torres <[sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>

**Subject:** Re: Thank you! – Introduction to Fiji for image analysis

‌

Hi Jenna,

Just so I make sure I understand correctly – you want to ensure that when two spots are very close to each other, they’re counted as one instead of being split as two objects?

If that is the case, an easy way around worth trying would be to add a blur to the image just before you segment the spots. Like we did in the FIJI course, applying a filter to blur out the noise enough that the borders of the two spots merge. In CellProfiler that can be done by adding the ’Smooth’ module in between ‘MaskImage’ and ‘IdentifyPrimaryObjects’. 

      Just make sure that in IdentifyPrimaryObjects you change the input to be the smoothed image instead of the masked!

Give it a go and let me know. If you’re not successful, you can share an example image where you see this (the processed MIP will do), and I’ll have a go myself.

Best wishes,

Sara

**From:** Jenna Hare <[jenna.hare@crick.ac.uk](mailto:jenna.hare@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Date:** Tuesday, 12 May 2026 at 11:58

**Subject:** Re: Thank you! – Introduction to Fiji for image analysis

Hi Sara,

I have been having so much fun playing around with Cell Profiler! I'm pretty happy with the results so far, but when I compare them to manual counting I notice that CP counts a lot more triploid cells than me. I think it's because when 2 spots are very close together, counting manually~~ we assume they're undergoing mitosis and so it is actually one spot. I was wondering if there is a setting about "distance" of spots so they're not counted if they are too close to eachother. It's unlikely X chromosomes are directly on top of eachother so I think that would be a good parameter to put in.

I have been changing the Thresholding, I think Sauvola is the best and also the smoothing scale and threshold options for that.

BW

Jenna

Hi Konstantina,

Would you mind sending us the whole error in a txt file, for instance. 

A few more questions to understand what you’re running:

- _Did you install the Stardist extension and run it directly?_
- _Is the model that’s being referred to in line 21 in that folder?_
- _Are you running it on a GPU or CPU session?_
- _Is the image you are trying to run this in have a channel named ‘DAPI’?_
- _What QuPath version are you running on OnDemand? Did you follow _[_these instructions_](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/Image-Analysis-Group/software_instructions/QuPath/ "‌")_?_ 

Thank you,

Sara

data.text:
**Stitching brainsaw data**

From: David Barry
Sent: 12 May 2026 21:50
To: Elisa de Launoit <elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>; Juan Carlos Boffi <juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk>; Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: Stitching brainsaw data

Hi All,

Sorry for the radio silence - NEMO maintenance over the last couple of weeks has slowed progress a bit. 

So, I'm still grappling with the metadata issues a bit (I can't get BigStitcher to position tiles correctly across multiple sections), but I think I'm getting close to a solution (which essentially involves writing all the necessary metadata into the files). 

I'll hopefully be in a position to share some positive results shortly, although I am going to be away quite a lot over the next week, so could be the end next week before I have an update. 

The good news is that everything I'm doing is being done in a automated fashion, so all this should be doable automatically on NEMO (and @elisa de Launoit<mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>, I'll be happy to share everything with you, if it's successful, so you can run everything yourself). 

Best,

Dave.

From: Elisa de Launoit <elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk<mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 28 April 2026 14:39
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>>; Juan Carlos Boffi <juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk<mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk>>; Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk<mailto:stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: Stitching brainsaw data

Hi Dave,

Thank you so much for sharing these!

I've opened both files and everything looks good, the stitching in Z appears well done. Looking forward to seeing the results with the additional sections! Also, if you're able to share the script, I'd be happy to try running it on the whole block myself. 

Best,
Elisa

From: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Tuesday, 28 April 2026 at 13:16
To: Juan Carlos Boffi <juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk<mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk>>; Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk<mailto:stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>>; Elisa de Launoit <elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk<mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: Stitching brainsaw data
Thanks Juan,

Another issue I've encountered is the pixel values are stored as signed integers, which is weird for microscopy data. So instead of values ranging from 0 to 65,535, they range from -32,768 to 32,767. 

Anyway, I've worked around all these issues for now and had a go at properly registering/fusing all the tiles from the single section mentioned below - the paths to the results are as follows: /nemo/project/proj-miguel-aliaga-brainsaw/working/barryd/reg_fused_tp_0_ch_0.tif /nemo/project/proj-miguel-aliaga-brainsaw/working/barryd/reg_fused_tp_0_ch_1.tif 

@elisa de Launoit<mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>, could you take a look at these and let me know what you think? They look sensible to me, but you should double-check. Each of the files is big (26GB), but you should still be able to open them with FIJI, especially on OnDemand. 

The next step would be to do the same thing with a few more sections and then try to fuse together the results, so we end up with a single registered volume representing multiple sections. If that works, we run it on the entire dataset. 

In other words, it would be a two step process:

  1.  Fuse together tiles in single sections
  2.  Fuse together the results from multiple sections

Best,

Dave.

From: Juan Carlos Boffi <juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk<mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 24 April 2026 11:01
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>>; Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk<mailto:stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>>; Elisa de Launoit <elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk<mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: Stitching brainsaw data

Hi Stefania and Dave,
I will definitely bring up the metadata improvement to Rob Campbell. They are getting ready to make a brainsaw publication so any input on using brainsaw raw files for batch processing is much appreciated. Cheers,
Juan

From: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Thursday, 23 April 2026 at 14:14
To: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk<mailto:stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>>, Elisa de Launoit <elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk<mailto:elisa.delaunoit@crick.ac.uk>>, Juan Carlos Boffi <juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk<mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: Stitching brainsaw data
Hi All,

I've had a go at assembling the tiles in the following folder, to ensure the positional information is correct: /nemo/project/proj-miguel-aliaga-brainsaw/data/rawData/CrickSaw_260326_hml_old_young_females_hml-0001 

You can see the result here:
/nemo/project/proj-miguel-aliaga-brainsaw/working/barryd/CrickSaw_260326_hml_old_young_females_hml-0001_unfused_montage.ome.tiff 

Figuring out which tile goes where wasn't massively difficult, but reading the dimensionality of the individual tiles correctly was a bit more of a challenge (the Z and C axis were both being read by Bio-Formats as T). In the end I had to load and resave each of them to include the correct metadata, which is obviously not ideal. 

So, @juan Carlos Boffi<mailto:juancarlos.boffi@crick.ac.uk>, more metadata in the raw files would make this a whole lot easier! Not sure how this could be achieved, but maybe we could feed back to the developers? 

Note that the result I've shared above is just an assembly of the raw data on a regular grid pattern - I haven't tried to properly register and fuse the tiles in any way. Registration is the next step, but I just wanted to confirm that the initial positioning of tiles was accurate before doing anything else - looks sensible to me (I can definitely see flies!), but just wanted to double-check! 

Best,

Dave.

Best wishes,
Stefania

From: David Barry <[David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Sent: Monday, May 11, 2026 5:03:40 PM
To: Konstantina Angoura <[konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures

Hi Konstantina,

I should stress that I just used pulled out the core function from the script and ran that with the default parameters – there are a whole bunch of parameters that can be tweaked which will influence the result.

But, there are many, many other approaches that could be used for this. However, it’s unlikely any of them are going to be perfect – they will all have their associated advantages and disadvantages.

With that in mind, it probably makes sense to try a few different approaches and see how this influences the overall result (of the downstream analysis). In other words, if two different approaches produce two slightly different midlines in the same section, how does this influence the classification of cells, niches, etc. in the subsequent analysis and, more importantly, does it change the overall conclusion.

This is probably the kind of thing you can experiment with yourself if you’re prepared to learn a little Python (with our support, of course)?

Dave.

From: Konstantina Angoura <[konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Sent: 08 May 2026 12:08

Hi Dave!

Thank you for this! Hm, it works better on the DAPI but I am not sure it as accurate as we would want it.. we ideally want it to draw the line from the very tip of the structure to the other tip, like going from stem to edge of a banana.. hopefully, we can find a way to draw a better line so we can extract the coordinates and group cells based on position relative to the midline for James’ analysis… If there is a more accurate way of approaching this than the macro, then we are super keen to try. We want a nice smooth curved line going through the midline of each structure, if possible. Let me know if I can do anything to help! We can set up another meeting (and have Naomi and James join) if helpful! Thank you, again!

Best wishes,

Konstantina

---

From: David Barry <[David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Sent: Friday, May 8, 2026 11:00:52 AM
To: Konstantina Angoura <[konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures

It’s probably worth chatting to someone in CALM about the brightfield imaging – there’s probably something that can be done to enhance the quality. And no, changing the display contrast won’t help – it’s the underlying lack of contrast between sample and background that is causing the problem.

But as for the DAPI image (which has LOTS of contrast!), that is more straightforward (see attached) – I think the script you sent me is probably overkill for this.

Dave.

Hi Dave!

Thank you for having a go, looks promising! Sadly, the images never really look as nice as the demo, we take full 96 well plates still in media so the focus is a bit off sometimes and there's debris etc in the wells. Would it be helpful to adjust the contrast of the images manually before running the macro?

The red line outlining the structure looks pretty good! Is the contrast going to help with the actual midline drawing? Because from your results image it looks like it doesn’t measure the longest length of the structure..

Any luck with the DAPI image? That one is bigger priority for us since James is waiting for the coordinates of the lines for the analysis of the spatial..

Let me know if I can help! Thank you, again!

Best wishes,

Konstantina

From: David Barry <[David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Date: Thursday, 7 May 2026 at 14:40

Hi Konstantina,

I’ve had a go at running the script – it sort of works on your images (see attached example).

The script is a bit cumbersome for what it does (it could almost certainly be streamlined), but I think the bigger problem is that your images are relatively low contrast.

Compare your image below with the “demo” image the script was originally designed to handle:

[cid:image001.png@01DCE258.1D60E100][cid:image002.png@01DCE258.1D60E100]

The image on the right has a much more uniform background and a much darker object in the foreground, meaning segmentation is much more straightforward.

How did you acquire the images of your organoids? Have you discussed with any of the microscopists in CALM?

Best,

Dave.

Really nice to meet you just now and thank you for taking on the task. I have popped the script and the two images in the outputs folder (light_microscopy/outputs/Konstantina/). The two images are:

- 250402\_MultiR1\_188h\_BrightField\_10x: 3 channel image of gastruloids in culture, we want the first channel (BF) and to exclude samples touching the image borders   *   250402\_MultiR1\_188h\_BrightField\_10x: DAPI image from Xenium. (Xenium file did not have scale, so I converted pixel to um using the area and the pixel ratio of the DAPI image)

Also, I let James know what we discussed and he said he can work with both Python and R and that he mostly uses Python for Xenium data. Thank you, again! Excited to see how this goes. Let me know if you need anything else in the meantime!

Best wishes,

Konstantina

Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures

It’s probably worth chatting to someone in CALM about the brightfield imaging – there’s probably something that can be done to enhance the quality. And no, changing the display contrast won’t help – it’s the underlying lack of contrast between sample and background that is causing the problem.

But as for the DAPI image (which has LOTS of contrast!), that is more straightforward (see attached) – I think the script you sent me is probably overkill for this.

Dave.

Hi Dave!

Thank you for having a go, looks promising! Sadly, the images never really look as nice as the demo, we take full 96 well plates still in media so the focus is a bit off sometimes and there's debris etc in the wells. Would it be helpful to adjust the contrast of the images manually before running the macro?

The red line outlining the structure looks pretty good! Is the contrast going to help with the actual midline drawing? Because from your results image it looks like it doesn’t measure the longest length of the structure..

Any luck with the DAPI image? That one is bigger priority for us since James is waiting for the coordinates of the lines for the analysis of the spatial..

Let me know if I can help! Thank you, again!

Best wishes,
Konstantina

data.text:
## 1st meeting – 7/5/26:

Zoom, Stefania & Sara. Recap email:

‌

Hi Jenna,

I’m sending you a recap of what we chatted about today, with some useful reminders and attachments – beware, long email incoming!

**Image acquisition**

The histogram of your images show that you have squished your foci signal to ~200-300/ out of 65,536 grey values (0.4% of what’s available). Thankfully your signal-to-noise ratio is very good and works in your favour.

However, going forwards try increasing the dynamic range of your histogram when you’re sat at the microscope – it’ll guide you way better than just looking at the image! You can also reach out to someone in the CALM acquisition team to give you a hand.

‌

‌

**CellProfiler pipeline**

CellProfiler is a free, open-source software you can download [here](https://cellprofiler.org/releases "‌"). It was made with high-throughput screens in mind, so it’s very good to batch analyse images that look similar. I find it much easier to use when establishing these parent-child relationships between objects rather than loops within loops in FIJI macros.

- Before you start, give the [tutorials](https://cellprofiler.org/tutorials "‌") a go to familiarise yourself with it.
- I’ve attached a pipeline I made with the image you shared with me. You can use it as a base (spot\_count\_nuclei\_saraforjenna\_20260506.cpproj).
  - **It’s currently only for “green” spots,** but you can easily add modules to do the same thing with as many channels as you’d like.
  - You can drag & drop it into CellProfiler to open it.
  - Also note the naming of the files will change, so the _NamesAndTypes_ modules will need to be adapted.
- Test-mode is your friend! – add a few different-looking images and conditions to play around with the parameters until you find something that works well enough. Then batch analyse the images.
- It’s a widely used software, so there are a lot of posts about it in the [image.sc](https://forum.image.sc/ "‌") forum. It’s very likely that if you have a question, someone has already asked it there.

‌

- **FIJI macro** – prepping your images to input into CellProfiler

You are going to have to input images that are already Maximum Intensity Projections (3Ds squished into 2Ds) and separated by channels. For that, I’ve adapted a FIJI macro to do exactly that (CellProfiler\_saveCh\_MIPs.ijm).

- When run, it opens a dialogue:
  - Input directory: folder with your images.
  - Output directory: folder where your new images will be saved. I recommend making a folder _just_ for this, so you can drag and drop this into CellProfiler.
  - File suffix: the image' file format (nd2 in your case).
- If you have z-stacks, it makes MIPs and saves those. If you have 2D images, it just splits the channels.
- Output you’ll get from it – from a file like experiment01.nd2 with 3 channels and a Z-stack, you’ll get in your output directory:
  - MAX_C1-experiment01.tif
  - MAX_C2-experiment01.tif
  - MAX_C3-experiment01.tif

The C1/C2/C3 in the filename gives you the reference you need to set up the channel matching rules in CellProfiler’s _NamesAndTypes_ module.

‌

**FIJI pipeline**

As I mentioned, this analysis is doable in FIJI too. If you are still interested in trying with a macro, there are scripts people have put together for nuclear spot counting, for example this [one](https://github.com/MontpellierRessourcesImagerie/imagej_macros_and_scripts/wiki/Foci-Per-Nucleus-Tool "‌").

‌

‌

I hope that all makes sense. A reminder we have helpdesks every other Monday, 10-11am in the cafe (next one: 18th May), but feel free to reach out to us if you run into any hurdles (email/slack/somewhere in the building).

Best of luck!

Sara

Hi Konstantina,

I’ve had a go at running the script – it sort of works on your images (see attached example). 

The script is a bit cumbersome for what it does (it could almost certainly be streamlined), but I think the bigger problem is that your images are relatively low contrast. 

Compare your image below with the “demo” image the script was originally designed to handle: 

[cid:image002.png@01DCDE2F.4033AD70][cid:image003.png@01DCDE2F.4033AD70] 

The image on the right has a much more uniform background and a much darker object in the foreground, meaning segmentation is much more straightforward. 

How did you acquire the images of your organoids? Have you discussed with any of the microscopists in CALM? 

Best,

Dave.

Hi Dave,

Thank you for this! I booked a slot but just to mention the overall goal :) . These images are of the samples we did spatial transcriptomics (Xenium) on. We have 6 images in total. Our goal is (a) to get some morphometric measurements of the samples including the longest axis length to then group samples by morphology for the analysis of the sequencing. (b) We also want the coordinates of the longest axis to then position each cell of each sample we sequenced along the long axis for each sample. We know from imaging that cell types in our samples are dependent on their position along the axis, and we want to prove that by our transcriptomics and group cells sequenced by axis position. So, it is quite important that the axis is accurate, if possible. Naomi wanted this macro/method for consistency with our collaborators but if we have a way that works better then we are happy the change it! I hope that makes sense! Happy to explain further when we meet. Thank you! 

Best wishes,

Konstantina

data.text:
**Ollie Platt - Image Analysis Support**

Hi Ollie,

I've put together a script<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/cell-blob-detector/blob/main/blob_detector.py> to act as a starting point for this. 

I can make it a bit more user-friendly in due course, but maybe first take a look at the output it produces (you should see a folder called Ollie/spot-detection-outputs in your lab's inputs from light microscopy. Each TIF image represents either nuclei or spot ("blob") detections in each of the input image channels. 

All of the results are collated in batch_nucleus_blob_analysis.csv, which shows how many spots were detected for each nucleus in each image and the average distance between spots in different channels. There are a whole bunch of different ways this kind of data can be analysed and visualised, but I thought it best to start simple. There are also various different parameters that can be varied for the detection of nuclei and spots. 

Best,

Dave.

From: Ollie Platt <olivia.platt@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 21 April 2026 10:59
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Ollie Platt - Image Analysis Support

Hi Dave,

That's totally fine with me! I've attached the A/Z protocol for analysis below (they select cells by eye, which I think is a bit eh, but the rest of it might be of use). 

FISH Spot analysis:

Spot detection and co-occurrence analysis

Cytoplasmic viral RNA foci were detected with Fiji (Ref: https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.2019) using custom-built macros. First, the cytoplasm of individual cells was segmented by drawing the outlines of the whole cell and the nucleus with the freehand selection tool. The region-of-interest (ROI) was cut out by blurring the edges with a Gaussian filter (radius = 10 pixel). For detection of the RNA spots, Laplacian images (smoothing scale 1.5 pixel) were generated via the FeatureJ plugin after applying a Gaussian filter (radius = 2 pixel) to the raw image. Typically, thresholding was carried out using the 'IsoData' threshold. However, when the signal-to-noise ratio of foci was low so that detections were not correct by visual control (in particular for the A/NC/1999 HA:PA mutant strain), other threshold settings were used as indicated in Table XXX. Lists of foci centroid positions for each colour channel were generated by the 'Analyse particle' function applied to the thresholded Laplacian images (no cut-off for size or circularity). To determine the spatial density of foci in the host cell cytoplasm, the area of the ROI was measured as well. The 488-colour channel was used as reference. The image was blurred with a Gaussian filter (radius = 15 pixel), thresholded via the 'Otsu' threshold and the area measured using the 'Analyse particle' function. Spatial density of foci was calculated by dividing the number of foci by the ROI area. 

A custom Matlab script was applied to measure the spatial co-occurrence between foci of different colour channels. With the implemented algorithm, for each segmented spot, the distance from its nearest neighbour in the other colour channels was determined. If any spots were found within a range of 300 nm (diffraction limit of the microscope) from the spots in the original channel, these were labelled as 'co-occurred'. From each ROI, the percentage of spots co-occurring with the ones in each of the other colour channels was calculated and this data for all ROIs accumulated in a data sheet for each sample separately. 

Thanks again,
Ollie

Ollie Platt
She/her
PhD student
RNA Virus Replication Lab

The Francis Crick Institute
1 Midland Road
London NW1 1AT

### Catch-up meeting, 6/5/26:

Zoom, Stefania & Sara. Recap email:

Hi Joyce,

Sending you the usual post-meeting recap to keep track of what we’ve been talking about (plus successfully tested the spatial maps on your images!).

**High background in new set of images**

As you mentioned, the mistake in secondary antibody would explain why the images look so different, so the object classifier will also be very confused when exposed to them. 

- Don’t spend too much time fiddling around with training the classifier. Wait to see the newly properly stained images from that same block EHP is preparing.
- You will still encounter batch to batch variability, but we’d expect it to be much lower. Add annotations on a few different images to take into account that variability.
  - That means it might not perform so well in your first images, but hopefully you reach a sensible middle ground.
  - No need to go crazy on the annotations. Very few annotations per image might do the trick.

**Negative control**

This one is a very important one. If the high background is still an issue, we can try preprocessing your images to subtract that background fluorescence.

**If it’s still unsatisfactory**,

We can try different approaches (InstanSeg, _ilastik_…) if you see that stretching QuPath to its limit is still not enough.

**Different classifiers**

It is very valid to have classifiers trained on different independent conditions (e.g., stages), as long as you are sticking to the same analysis within that batch. 

**Spatial analysis** 

Decoding where in the heart tissue you see your phenotype is a question for the future, once you can happily classify your cells.

However, you can get a glimpse into spatial relationships by using the[ _Density Maps_](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/density_maps.html "‌") in QuPath that I mentioned during the meeting.  They are a very quick way of getting some visual feedback on where a certain class is clustering in the tissue, and even creating annotations based on those hotspots.

- Note – you’ll need to have cells already classified for this! 

A very quick example on one of your images:

![joyce\_density\_map\_gfpPOS\_example.png](cid:841557130*AD048CC2-5B4A-4F7F-B66C-DA3ADB4270FB)

**Measurement maps**

You can get a similar density map, but based on cell measurements by going to _Measure > Show measurement maps_. In the window that opens you can select certain measurements to show, e.g. _Cy3 mean_:

![Screenshot 2026-05-06 at 12.50.02.png](cid:841557130*71A89123-3A69-4EC2-90E7-003E5E577E6A)

**Follow up meeting**

Reach out to us whenever you have the new batch of images from EHP and have tested the above suggestions. If anything pops up in the time being, you know where to find us!

Best wishes,

Sara

card.name: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local)

data.text:
Hi Jing,

Thanks for your time just now. Please see below a recap of what we've discussed. If you have any further questions, or if I've forgotten anything, don't hesitate to get in touch! 

  *
There are two approaches you can use to quantify your data. One relies on quantifying your markers of interest by field of view, normalising by the total area of the image covered by cells, and another one is to quantify them by cell. In the first case, you'll need a binary mask of your green channel; in the second case, a label mask (each cell is given a unique integer identifier). More details on the difference between a binary and a label mask at this link<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/connected_components/index.html>.   *
To obtain a binary mask of your green channel, there are different approaches:      *
A simple thresholding in Fiji choosing one of the default algorithms, followed by some cleaning of the obtained binary mask to remove small objects or fill holes (see Analyze Particles and Process>Binary menu). You might want to apply some filters to the image before segmenting (mean or Gaussian) to smooth out some of the noise and improve the quality of the thresholding.      *
Using ilastik to train a shallow learning model based on a few annotations (pixel classifier). You can find a tutorial on this page<https://www.ilastik.org/documentation/pixelclassification/pixelclassification>. This will give as output a probability map in .h5 format, that you can open in Fiji with the ilastik plugin<https://www.ilastik.org/documentation/fiji_export/plugin> and then threshold to obtain a binary mask.   *
To obtain a label mask of your green channel, you can either:      *
Start from the binary mask obtained in the previous option and a segmentation of the nuclear channel to run a seeded watershed using MorphoLibJ. You can find more information at this link<https://imagej.net/plugins/marker-controlled-watershed>.      *
Run an object classification in ilastik, more information here<https://www.ilastik.org/documentation/objects/objects>.   *
Everything you run in Fiji for both methods can be wrapped into a macro. You can find on this page<https://imagej.net/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro> a tutorial on how to record the steps and wrap them in a script.   *
Information on how to install and use ilastik on OnDemand is available on our GitHub pages<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/tree/master/software_instructions/Ilastik>. 

If you are interested, we run regular image analysis training workshops<https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/group/514/events>. We have one coming up in June that goes from the basics all the way to advanced machine learning techniques, more information here<https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/seminar/image-analysis-introduction-image-analysis-2026-06-03t080000>. 

Best wishes,
Stefania

________________________________

I managed to obtain similar results to the website by downscaling the image and changing the radius accordingly, or by using a large Gaussian blur (about 10 px) and increasing the radius to a larger number. I worked off a maximum intensity projection on the green channel. 

I'm happy to have a chat! I have some time on Wednesday afternoon after 3 pm or on Friday afternoon. Would you have any preference?

Hi Stephania,

Thank you so much for your assistance again! Yeah I had the same thought over the weekend and had readjusted the image size to 224x224 pixels… but somehow it still did not resolve the issue. I’ll try a few different gaussian filters too and see if it does anything. May I please ask if the pixel adjustment method has worked for you on the images I sent? Is it possible to meet briefly with you and show you what I did anytime this week when you are available? 

Thank you very much again.

From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Monday, 27 April 2026 at 13:21
To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: stefaniam27+apz14vt0mjvcs1ztyzfj@boards.trello.com <stefaniam27+apz14vt0mjvcs1ztyzfj@boards.trello.com>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing,

I think I know what the problem is! The Cellpose website works by down-sampling the image, reducing its size to 224x224 pixels. It therefore smooths out a lot of the noise/ punctuated behaviour displayed on the green channel, resulting in a better segmentation. 

You can manually down-sample the images (in Fiji, Image>Adjust>Size) before running the segmentation locally in CellPose to obtain the same result, provided you then up-sample the masks you obtain before proceeding to perform the rest of the workflow. 

Alternatively, you can apply a filter (like a Gaussian blur or mean filter in Fiji, Process>Filter) that should get a similar “smoothing effect” before segmentation. You might need to play around a little with filter size to obtain an analogous result to the Cellpose webpage. 

Please let me know if you have any questions or require further assistance! 

Best wishes,
Stefania

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Date: Friday, 24 April 2026 at 14:38
To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing!

I believe the problem might be related to your input file format. From the screenshots you are sending, it looks like the local version of Cellpose is picking up information from the wrong channel, or from a mixture of channels. 

Have you tried using a .tif file as input for only the green channel? You can obtain this by importing the .czi file into Fiji with Bio-Formats and saving the green channel separately as .tif. You can then test feeding this as input to the local version of Cellpose. 

Please let me know if it works!

Best wishes,
Stefania

----------------------------------

From: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Thursday, 23 April 2026 at 17:43
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Dear Image Analysis Group,

Hope you’re doing well.

This is Jing and I’m a first-year PhD student at Gandhi Lab. I’m currently running into an issue with Cellpose segmentation and was hoping to get your advice. I’m working with max-projected composite PNG images (derived from .czi files), and I’ve noticed a discrepancy between the Cellpose website output and my local terminal GUI runs. 

Specifically:

  *   The exact same input PNG gives very good segmentation on the Cellpose website (Cellpose-SAM). (Please see attached Image_Cellposeweb). 

  *   However, when I run Cellpose locally (also using Cellpose-SAM), the segmentation appears distorted. (Pleasee see attached Image_GUI) 

I’ve tried to control for the main variables:

  *   Using the same input image

  *   Matching the model (Cellpose-SAM)

  *   Adjusting different channel settings

  *   Trying auto and manual diameters

I also confirmed that the issue is not due to preprocessing differences, as I’m using the same PNG exported. Please also find my cellpose version below: 

cellpose version: 4.1.1

platform:       darwin

python version: 3.14.2

torch version:  2.9.1

May I please ask if you may have encountered the same issue or have any suggestions? I’m happy to discuss or meet at any time you are convenient if needed. 

Thanks very much for your time and help in advance!

Best regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student
Gandhi Lab, The Francis Crick Institute

CD34 localisation to blood vessels in liver tissue – Quadri Omotesho
CD34 localisation to blood vessels in liver tissue – Quadri Omotesho

data.text:
## Initial meeting – 29/4/26:

Recap email:
Hi Quadri,

I am sending you a recap of what we’ve discussed yesterday, with quite a few useful links to help you get started on your image analysis journey – beware, long email incoming!

———————————————— 

**OMERO**

You should have received the email yesterday I sent to Ken and Donald. They’ll create an account for you. 

- As Ken mentioned, if you’re going to continue using the same slide scanner in EHP, there are some guidelines you can follow on how to name and structure your files when imaging, so it helps upload them into OMERO in the future – instructions [here](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/crick-omeroguide/instruments.html#experimental-histo-pathology-ehp-stp "‌").
- Once you are set-up, Ken can give you a quick onboarding for [OMERO](https://omero-prod.camp.thecrick.org/ "‌").
- _#omero-users_ is a very useful Slack channel to join to stay up to date with any announcements.

‌

**QuPath on OnDemand (Nemo Desktop)**

Using the powerful computers we have on the 8th floor will speed up your analysis massively. 

- If you’ve used OnDemand before, you should still have access. The HPC team have launched a new one, so ensure you save [this link](https://ondemand001.nemo.thecrick.org/pun/sys/dashboard "‌").
- Another useful Slack channel to reach out to the HPC team, which are in charge of this cluster, is _#hpc_. They also have a very useful Mon/Wed/Fri 10-11.30am drop-in helpdesk.
- Be mindful of the resources you ask for! If you only need a couple of hours to work on QuPath, you can always save the project and open it at another time with a different session.
  - Once the session ends and runs out of time**, any unsaved changes will be lost**. It’s a fantastic habit to keep saving your QuPath project as you go (_File > Save_). Unfortunately talking from experience :)
- More info on OnDemand in the [HPC Wiki](https://wiki.thecrick.org/display/HPCuserwiki/High+Performance+Computing+%28HPC%29+user+wiki "‌").

- To access QuPath on OnDemand you can follow the instructions [here](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/Image-Analysis-Group/software_instructions/QuPath/ "‌") – no need to install it yourself. These are brand new instructions for the newest version (v0.7), so any feedback is welcome! Let me know if you are struggling to open it.

**QuPath documentation**

The [official QuPath documentation](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/index.html "‌") is fantastic, and has very useful tutorials, tips, and even videos that are nicely organised and easy to follow along. I’d recommend you have a look at the basics to get familiarised with QuPath first.

‌

**Vessel detection in QuPath**

- Try using a [pixel classifier ](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/pixel_classification.html "‌")with the channel of interest (e.g., CK19 for bile ducts). From it, you can then click “Create objects” to segment the vessels. It will open a new window where you can filter objects by size, so you can remove the smaller hepatocyte structures that also get stained with CK19.

![Screenshot 2026-04-30 at 13.55.16.png](cid:3107493098*1C1B139D-B20F-4473-AC8B-D9AE59A9A0DA)

- [This](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zAs9e_Tl86E "‌") video tutorial does a great job at explaining the workflow!
- I’d recommend you make annotations in a few different images so you can account for sample-to-sample variability. Some more info on how to do so with classifiers [here](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/pixel_classification.html#handling-variation "‌").

‌

**Overall workflow for the future**

Very roughly, if you are interested in comparing the number of red-positive cells inside vessels versus in the whole tissue, you could follow this workflow in QuPath:

1. Create a QuPath project folder in your lab-space (as you have _much_ more storage than in your home space).

- Or open one if you already have one created.

1. Import images through the OMERO extension.

- There is an OMERO extension you can install through _Extensions > Manage extensions_. Instructions [here](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/crick-omeroguide/qupath/docs/installation.html "‌").

1.  [Detect](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/thresholding.html "‌") the whole tissue you are interested in. Start with a basic threshold. If that same threshold doesn’t work well with other images, you can try with a [pixel classifier](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/pixel_classification.html "‌").
2. Detect vessels (see “vessel detection” section above).
3. Inside all your annotations (_i.e._, tissue and vessels) run [cell detection](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/cell_detection.html "‌").

- Adjusting parameters in this inbuilt QuPath function generally works very well with DAPI stains.
- If you have very clustered cells and your segmentation isn’t satisfactory, there is a potential plan B.

1. Once you have cells inside your annotation, you can classify them based on red ‘positivity’ in several different ways. Give them a go until you find something that works consistently for a few different images:

1. _Analyze > Cell detection > Run _[_positive cell detection_](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/cell_detection.html#run-positive-cell-detection "‌") – what I demoed during our meeting.
2. _Classify > _[_Object classification_ ](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html#train-save-classifiers "‌")_\> Train object classifier_ – similar to a pixel classifier, you will have to add annotations to train a model on a few different images. It will help if you have a lot of variability between images.

1. Extract measurements:

- You can view results per annotation very easily through _Measure > Show annotation measurements._
- To export a file with all your results in one or several images at a time, go to _Measure > _[_Export measurements_](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/exporting_measurements.html#via-the-measurement-exporter "‌"). 
  - Select “Annotations” as export type – each row will correspond to an annotation, which will include Num detections, Num red+/green+, Num red-/green+, etc.
    - If your annotations are classed into ’tissue’ and ‘vessel’, you’ll be able to also see what the annotations correspond to in the results file.

‌

‌

**Measurement maps** – a great addition for lab meetings!

As I mentioned yesterday, these maps are very visual ways of seeing measurements coming from your cells (e.g., mean AF555 in the nucleus). You can start seeing spatial patterns very easily before going down a complicated spatial analysis pipeline (and great images for lab meetings)!

- _Measure > Show measurement maps_. 
- You will need to have cells detected beforehand!

There are some similar maps called [Density maps](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/density_maps.html#density-maps "‌"), where you can find hotspots and even create annotations from them. You will need classified objects for this one (_Analyze > Density maps > Create density maps_).

**Future discussion**

I’m aware this is a lot of information. I would start with getting your images onto OMERO and get QuPath working on OnDemand. Once you are more familiar with QuPath, feel free to reach out to me again (email/Slack) and we can tackle your image analysis workflow in a bit more depth. 

**Image analysis resources**

- We run regular image analysis trainings, including QuPath, which will be worth attending if you can. You can check our upcoming events in our IntraNet page [here](https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/our-crick/image-analysis "‌").
- We have joint helpdesks in the CALM STP with HPC, BABS, and SEAI every other Monday (10-11am) in the cafe. They are good opportunities to go down to ask us, or any other relevant STP, any burning questions you might have. 
  - The next one got postponed to Tuesday, 5th May (10-11am) because of the Monday bank holiday.

I hope your lab meeting presentation went well!

If you have any other questions in the time being, feel free to email/Slack me. 

Best wishes,

Sara

data.text:
## QuPath workflow assistance meeting – 29/4/26

Stefania & Sara, zoom.

Recap email:

---

Hi Zuzanna,

I’m sending you a recap of what we discussed yesterday with some useful links. Beware, long email incoming!

#### **OMERO**

As discussed, the big advantage of having your images in OMERO is that you don’t have to download them every time you want to work in QuPath.

- There is an OMERO extension you can download from the _Extensions manager_ in QuPath (versions 0.6+). [Here](https://franciscrickinstitute.github.io/crick-omeroguide/qupath/docs/installation.html "‌") are some guidelines on how to do so. 
- To import images, you’ll then have to go to the _Extensions_ tab > _OMERO > Browse server_. Connect to [https://omero-prod.camp.thecrick.org](https://omero-prod.camp.thecrick.org/ "‌") and log in. 
- Note you’ll have to be connected to the VPN or the Crick WiFi if you are working with a local QuPath (i.e., on your laptop).

All of this can be done via OnDemand too.

_**A reminder OMERO will be down 1st-5th May**_

‌

#### **QuPath workflow**

A suggested workflow to answer your research question:

1. Import an image via OMERO
2. Detect the tissue of interest (i.e., an annotation) with a pixel classifier
3. Detect cells with the DAPI channel inside the annotations
4. Classify cells with thresholds for the red and green channels
5. Export measurements

**Detecting tissue of interest**

From what you showed us yesterday, the exact region of interest (ROI) you’re interested in doesn’t show enough differences with the surrounding tissue to automatically segment it. It might be worth trying to have a rough estimate of the region anyway and see whether you still see the phenotype.

- For automatic tissue detection using a pixel classifier (_Classify > Pixel classifier > Train pixel classifier)_ you can check the tutorial [here](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/pixel_classification.html#pixel-classification "‌").

![](cid:669670CD-2005-4FB3-BA50-B750A22DEBD8)

- In your case you need to ensure that under “Features” you only select the channels that are relevant to detect your ROI (i.e., DAPI and red). 
  - In that same “Select features” window, ensure you also select a few different scales and features.
- Training it with annotations on a few different images to capture sample-to-sample variability will give you much more robust results.
  - You can do so by annotating different images in the project and then load the annotations into the pixel classifier with “Load training”. Just select the images where you’ve added those annotations.

**Cell detection**

QuPath’s inbuilt cell detection algorithm (_Analyze > Cell detection_) is very efficient with DAPI stains, and adjusting the parameters available gives great results most of the time.

However, if you feel you have a lot of clustered cells that you would like separated and quantified, there is an easy-to-use pre-trained model available as an extension called _InstanSeg_. More info [here](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/deep/instanseg.html "‌").

‌

**Red/Green positivity**

If classifying cells based on a threshold works for your images, that’s great news – the simpler the better. However, if you see that image variability makes thresholding impossible, you can [train an object classifier](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/cell_classification.html#train-a-cell-classifier-based-on-annotations "‌") for the red channel and another one for the green channel. You can then [combine both](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html#combine-the-classifiers "‌") and apply to the whole project.

**Exporting measurements**

- You can view results per annotation very easily through _Measure > Show annotation measurements._
- To export a file with all your results in one or several images at a time, go to _Measure > _[_Export measurements_](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/exporting_measurements.html#via-the-measurement-exporter "‌"). 
  - Select “Annotations” as export type – each row will correspond to an annotation, which will include Num detections, Num red+/green+, Num red-/green+, etc.

#### **QuPath scripting**

The [official tutorials](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/scripting/index.html "‌") on scripting are very helpful to get started. Like FIJI macros, once you have a workflow that you’re happy with, you can go to the _Workflows_ tab and click “Create script”. 

- You’ll have to do some cleaning on that initial script, but fortunately all the functions have very clear names that tell you what they do.
- Clicking “Run” will analyse the image you have open. You can run the script for all the images in the folder by going to _Run > Run for project_. 
- Near the beginning of the script, you will need to add a line so it processes all the annotations in the image:

selectAnnotations()

#### **Statistics**

It’s worth reaching out to the BABS STP once you have some results. They have free statistical support available.

‌

‌

#### **Follow-up meeting(s)**

Feel free to reach out to us again once you have made more progress if you still need some additional advice on your image analysis project. Both Stefania and I are also available via Slack. Or come down again to our bi-weekly helpdesks.

‌

Best of luck with your analysis!

Best,

Sara

Many thanks,
Jing

PhD student
The Francis Crick Institute/ University College London
________________________________
From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Monday, April 27, 2026 5:10:04 PM
To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36eogqhtx7do161qiif+092z23fwoh@boards.trello.com <stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36eogqhtx7do161qiif+092z23fwoh@boards.trello.com> Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing,

I managed to obtain similar results to the website by downscaling the image and changing the radius accordingly, or by using a large Gaussian blur (about 10 px) and increasing the radius to a larger number. I worked off a maximum intensity projection on the green channel. 

I'm happy to have a chat! I have some time on Wednesday afternoon after 3 pm or on Friday afternoon. Would you have any preference? 

Best wishes,
Stefania
________________________________
From: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 April 2026 16:56
To: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Stephania,

Thank you so much for your assistance again! Yeah I had the same thought over the weekend and had readjusted the image size to 224x224 pixels… but somehow it still did not resolve the issue. I’ll try a few different gaussian filters too and see if it does anything. May I please ask if the pixel adjustment method has worked for you on the images I sent? Is it possible to meet briefly with you and show you what I did anytime this week when you are available? 

Thank you very much again.

Kind regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student
Gandhi Lab, The Francis Crick Institute

From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Monday, 27 April 2026 at 13:21
To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: stefaniam27+apz14vt0mjvcs1ztyzfj@boards.trello.com <stefaniam27+apz14vt0mjvcs1ztyzfj@boards.trello.com>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing,

I think I know what the problem is! The Cellpose website works by down-sampling the image, reducing its size to 224x224 pixels. It therefore smooths out a lot of the noise/ punctuated behaviour displayed on the green channel, resulting in a better segmentation. 

You can manually down-sample the images (in Fiji, Image>Adjust>Size) before running the segmentation locally in CellPose to obtain the same result, provided you then up-sample the masks you obtain before proceeding to perform the rest of the workflow. 

Alternatively, you can apply a filter (like a Gaussian blur or mean filter in Fiji, Process>Filter) that should get a similar “smoothing effect” before segmentation. You might need to play around a little with filter size to obtain an analogous result to the Cellpose webpage. 

Please let me know if you have any questions or require further assistance! 

Best wishes,
Stefania

Hi Stephania,

I’ve put some images in my folder under light microscopy outputs. The folder is named HLA-dr_CD68_Iba1_Hoescht, in which the green (488) channel for Iba1 is supposed to be the cell marker.  Thank you so much for your kind help again! 

Kind regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng

Thanks!

Best wishes,
Stefania

From: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>

Hi Stephania,

Thank you for your reply and suggestions! I had a try at your method and it seems the error persisted. I’ve also tried to set the image to G=green rather than RGB in the input and it gives the same result nonetheless. Please kindly find the attached image results. Thank you! [image.png]

Kind regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student
Gandhi Lab, The Francis Crick Institute

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Date: Friday, 24 April 2026 at 14:38
To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing!

I believe the problem might be related to your input file format. From the screenshots you are sending, it looks like the local version of Cellpose is picking up information from the wrong channel, or from a mixture of channels. 

Have you tried using a .tif file as input for only the green channel? You can obtain this by importing the .czi file into Fiji with Bio-Formats and saving the green channel separately as .tif. You can then test feeding this as input to the local version of Cellpose. 

Please let me know if it works!

Best wishes,
Stefania

----------------------------------

From: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Thursday, 23 April 2026 at 17:43
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Dear Image Analysis Group,

Hope you’re doing well.

This is Jing and I’m a first-year PhD student at Gandhi Lab. I’m currently running into an issue with Cellpose segmentation and was hoping to get your advice. I’m working with max-projected composite PNG images (derived from .czi files), and I’ve noticed a discrepancy between the Cellpose website output and my local terminal GUI runs. 

Specifically:

  *   The exact same input PNG gives very good segmentation on the Cellpose website (Cellpose-SAM). (Please see attached Image_Cellposeweb). 

  *   However, when I run Cellpose locally (also using Cellpose-SAM), the segmentation appears distorted. (Pleasee see attached Image_GUI) 

I’ve tried to control for the main variables:

  *   Using the same input image

  *   Matching the model (Cellpose-SAM)

  *   Adjusting different channel settings

  *   Trying auto and manual diameters

I also confirmed that the issue is not due to preprocessing differences, as I’m using the same PNG exported. Please also find my cellpose version below: 

cellpose version: 4.1.1

platform:       darwin

python version: 3.14.2

Best wishes,
Stefania

Hi Jing,

I think I know what the problem is! The Cellpose website works by down-sampling the image, reducing its size to 224x224 pixels. It therefore smooths out a lot of the noise/ punctuated behaviour displayed on the green channel, resulting in a better segmentation. 

You can manually down-sample the images (in Fiji, Image>Adjust>Size) before running the segmentation locally in CellPose to obtain the same result, provided you then up-sample the masks you obtain before proceeding to perform the rest of the workflow. 

Alternatively, you can apply a filter (like a Gaussian blur or mean filter in Fiji, Process>Filter) that should get a similar “smoothing effect” before segmentation. You might need to play around a little with filter size to obtain an analogous result to the Cellpose webpage.

To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing!

I believe the problem might be related to your input file format. From the screenshots you are sending, it looks like the local version of Cellpose is picking up information from the wrong channel, or from a mixture of channels. 

Have you tried using a .tif file as input for only the green channel? You can obtain this by importing the .czi file into Fiji with Bio-Formats and saving the green channel separately as .tif. You can then test feeding this as input to the local version of Cellpose. 

Please let me know if it works!

Best wishes,
Stefania

Dear Image Analysis Group,

Hope you’re doing well.

This is Jing and I’m a first-year PhD student at Gandhi Lab. I’m currently running into an issue with Cellpose segmentation and was hoping to get your advice. I’m working with max-projected composite PNG images (derived from .czi files), and I’ve noticed a discrepancy between the Cellpose website output and my local terminal GUI runs. 

Specifically:

  *   The exact same input PNG gives very good segmentation on the Cellpose website (Cellpose-SAM). (Please see attached Image_Cellposeweb). 

  *   However, when I run Cellpose locally (also using Cellpose-SAM), the segmentation appears distorted. (Pleasee see attached Image_GUI) 

I’ve tried to control for the main variables:

  *   Using the same input image

  *   Matching the model (Cellpose-SAM)

  *   Adjusting different channel settings

  *   Trying auto and manual diameters

I also confirmed that the issue is not due to preprocessing differences, as I’m using the same PNG exported. Please also find my cellpose version below: 

cellpose version: 4.1.1

platform:       darwin

python version: 3.14.2

torch version:  2.9.1

May I please ask if you may have encountered the same issue or have any suggestions? I’m happy to discuss or meet at any time you are convenient if needed. 

Thanks very much for your time and help in advance!

Best regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student
Gandhi Lab, The Francis Crick Institute

________________________________
From: Quadri Omotesho <quadri.omotesho@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Thursday, April 23, 2026 7:22:50 AM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Request foe help with image analysis

Good morning,

I am writing to request your guidance with an unbiased quantification of my IF images. The image is attached below and it is a liver tissue stained with CD34 (in white) and CD45 (in red). I would like to quantify the number of cells expressing the CD34 around the blood vessels (circled in black) versus the number of CD34 stained cells on the normal tissue parenchyma. Is there a way I can separate these two quantifications in an objective way. Right now, I am manually annotating the blood vessels and running Num of cell detected but I feel this might be a bit biased and I am wondering if there is a way to do this more efficiently. I am happy to schedule a chat if that is important for more clarity. I look forward to hearing from you. Thank you 

[image.png]
Kind regards,

Quadri Omotesho

The Francis Crick Institute Limited is a registered charity in England and Wales no. 1140062 and a company registered in England and Wales no. 06885462, with its registered office at 1 Midland Road London NW1 1AT

card.desc:
1. _What is the biological question you are trying to answer? --_ My goal is to characterise the liver in the tumour bearing context and to assess if there are changes in some specific markers such as CD34 which stains for cells in proliferative state. My observation is that I see that CD34 stains cells around the blood vessels in the liver from Naive mice while in the tumour-bearing mice, I see that in addition to staining cells around the liver, there are also CD34 cells now appearing in the liver tissue outside of the blood vessels
2. _What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis?-- S_o I want to quantify the number of CD34+ stains blood vessels in both naive and tumour bearing context as well as quantify CD34+ cells outside the blood vessels 
3. _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP_-- I used the Axio Scan EVOS at the EHP to scan the images
4. How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more? --I currently have about 30 images that I aim to quantify, and I will be acquiring more
5. _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?— I have two channels in the image I shared (555 and 647), and I have two conditions - Liver from Naive and Tumour bearing (TB)_
card.name: CD34 localisation to blood vessels in liver tissue – Quadri Omotesho
