**Subject:** Re: Request foe help with image analysis

Hi Quadri,

We can definitely give you some guidance. It’ll be much easier to schedule a meeting to understand your project and images a bit better. Are you available next week for an online chat? You can book a slot in my calendar next week [here](https://outlook.office.com/bookwithme/user/d4b915488d774ba3bb17791e0c9e1b93@crick.ac.uk/meetingtype/Yam2esON2kae3dlTpjO8-A2?bookingcode=74e9a514-72e2-4b40-a872-4499b615e181&anonymous&ismsaljsauthenabled&ep=mlink "‌"). Also happy to meet on a different week if that suits you better.

Ahead of that meeting, could you share a bit more background on your project?

- _What is the biological question you are trying to answer?_ 
- _What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis_ (e.g., _%CD34 nuclei in a tissue sample)? Any reference paper you are following?_
- _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?_ 
- _How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?_ 
- _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?_

Finally, could you also share some representative images with me so I can test a few approaches? If they live in OMERO, you can share the project ID and I can already access them. If they are in your NEMO storage, you can share some images with us in your lab space (stp/light-microscopy/outputs). You can make a folder in that directory with a representative name (e.g., quadri\_for\_sara) and I’ll be able to access any files you add to it.

Best wishes,

Sara

data.text:
Hi Jing,

I think I know what the problem is! The Cellpose website works by down-sampling the image, reducing its size to 224x224 pixels. It therefore smooths out a lot of the noise/ punctuated behaviour displayed on the green channel, resulting in a better segmentation.

You can manually down-sample the images (in Fiji, Image>Adjust>Size) before running the segmentation locally in CellPose to obtain the same result, provided you then up-sample the masks you obtain before proceeding to perform the rest of the workflow.

Alternatively, you can apply a filter (like a Gaussian blur or mean filter in Fiji, Process>Filter) that should get a similar “smoothing effect” before segmentation. You might need to play around a little with filter size to obtain an analogous result to the Cellpose webpage.

Please let me know if you have any questions or require further assistance!

Best wishes,
Stefania

data.text:
Hi Jing!

I believe the problem might be related to your input file format. From the screenshots you are sending, it looks like the local version of Cellpose is picking up information from the wrong channel, or from a mixture of channels. 

Have you tried using a .tif file as input for only the green channel? You can obtain this by importing the .czi file into Fiji with Bio-Formats and saving the green channel separately as .tif. You can then test feeding this as input to the local version of Cellpose.

Please let me know if it works!

Best wishes,

Stefania

data.text:
### Follow-up 22/4/26:

Zoom with Sara, recap:

Hi Lies,

Sending you the usual recap to keep track of what we’ve discussed. Not a long email this time around, a sign you’re basically a fully-fledged image analyst now!

**Using CD31 to exclude endothelial cells**

- When we were tackling your first question (quantifying volume of glial cells), we talked about you being able to exclude endothelial cells (positive for the CD31 marker) from the glial mask. 
- Now you are trying to use this ‘clean’ glial mask to do further analysis with the other new neuronal mask. So the filtering away CD31-positive cells approach is much easier with FIJI's “Image Calculator”, using a binary mask of the CD31 channel and subtracting it from the glial mask.
  - Which is exactly what you’ve done in the macro, and exactly how I would have done it!

**File save error**

- The error pops up in the loop when it reaches the biggest file. It’s good to start by requesting increasing amounts of memory in your OnDemand session (from 500G-2000GB).
- Also check that the FIJI memory its using is also increasing (_Edit > Options > Memory & threads_). Put up to 75% of the available memory for the session.
- Test the loop in a folder with just the heavy image, to save yourself some time (and computer memory).
- If all else fails, you can reach out to the HPC team through slack (#hpc) or going to their Mon/Wed/Fri helpdesk. They manage OnDemand and should have a much better idea on what to look out for.

**Next steps**

- Again, your workflow makes a lot of sense. Your combination of masks, filtering, image calculator, and quantification is the one I would have chosen too.
- Just make sure you visually check each step after creating masks and filtering. You might not need a final filtering step for immune cells.

I’ve sent a calendar invite for our next catch-up meeting on the 21st May.

card.desc:
Jenna came to our “Intro to FIJI” workshops 13/4/26 and booked a follow-up meeting with me for 7/5/26.

#### Project info:

- _What is the biological question you are trying to answer?_ 
  I want to determine what % of my ESC population has 2 X chromosomes. I would like to set up some kind of pipeline/macro to be able to automate this counting. I want to count signal per nucleus and then I can come to a %X, %XX or %>2X per clone.
- _What measurement(s) would you like to get our of your analysis_ (e.g., _%green nuclei in a tissue sample)? Any reference paper you are following?_
  Number of RNA FISH signals for Huwe1 per nucleus
- _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?_ 
  Nikon SoRa
- _How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?_ 
  I usually take 3 images per clone which consist of ~100-150 spotted cells each
- _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?_
  We use either 568, 488 or Cy5 channels. I usually do a probe for Xist RNA: Xist488 and Huwe1 RNA: Huwe1Cy5 but it could be a combination of these.
card.name: RNA FISH – Jenna Hare

data.text:
**Help in image analysis**

Hi Alla,

I had a look at your images. The draft pipeline in Fiji might look something like the following: 

  1.
Import image with Bio-Formats (File > Import > Bio-Formats)
     *
Please note, you might need to install the plugin first if you can't see this command in the menu (instructions here<https://docs.openmicroscopy.org/bio-formats/5.8.2/users/imagej/installing.html>)      *
Bio-Formats takes care of reading the metadata from your file. It performs better than the "drag and drop" option.   2.
Split channels (Image > Color > Split Channels)
     *
This allows you to work on each channel separately
     *
It's often a good idea to duplicate images while testing things in Fiji to avoid having to reopen the raw files multiple times (Image > Duplicate). When duplicating, we can also rename images for clarity (Image > Rename).   3.
[DAPI channel] Gaussian blur 3D, threshold, fill holes, clean to obtain binary mask      *
The Gaussian blur<https://imagej.net/ij/docs/menus/process.html#filters> smooths out some of the fine details you observe in the DAPI channel (Process > Filter > Gaussian Blur 3D). I’ve experimented with some parameters; a sigma of 10.0 in x,y and 5.0 in z seems to do the job in order to obtain a good enough segmentation. It might be worth exploring other parameter combinations, though!      *
Segment the blurred image by using one of the default algorithms<https://imagej.net/ij/docs/menus/image.html#adjust> (Image > Adjust > Threshold). The Triangle algorithm looks quite good on the test data, but again worth exploring other options. This gives a binary mask as output<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/binarization/index.html>, with values of 0 for background and 255 for foreground.      *
Fill holes in the binary mask (Process > Binary > Fill Holes). You can also check other operations that can be run on binary masks<https://imagej.net/ij/docs/menus/process.html#binary>, such as erosion/dilation (Process > Binary > Erode or Process > Binary > Dilate), which might help refine the segmentation.      *
If you want to clean the mask further, you can use Analyze Particles<https://imagej.net/ij/docs/menus/analyze.html#ap> to filter objects based on 2D size or circularity (Analyze > Analyze Particles). By requesting to show Masks as output, you will obtain a new binary image including only the objects passing the size or circularity requirements.   4.
[RED and GREEN, separately] Threshold, clean
     *
Threshold using one of the default algorithms (Image > Adjust > Threshold). Minimum or RenyiEntropy seem to work nicely, but again, some playing around with options might be required.      *
Similar to the considerations above for the DAPI channel, you can filter the signal and clean the binary masks with the same strategies.   5.
[DAPI + RED, DAPI + GREEN, separately] AND, connected components analysis      *
The next step consists of finding the green and red objects that are inside the nuclei. To do this, we can use the Image Calculator tool<https://imagej.net/ij/docs/menus/process.html#calculator> on the binary masks (Process > Image Calculator), working separately on the binary masks obtained for the red and green objects. We want to find the pixels that are considered foreground for both the DAPI mask AND the object mask; in this case, AND is considered as a Boolean operation<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logical_conjunction>. This will output a new binary mask for the red objects in the nuclei, and one for the green objects in the nuclei.      *
We can now analyse the objects in the binary masks. To do so, we need another plugin, MorphoLibJ. Installation instructions and more information here<https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj>. This plugin allows us to go from a binary mask to a label mask via connected component labelling<https://neubias.github.io/training-resources/connected_components/index.html>, where each object is given a separate integer ID and can be queried separately. We first create the label image (Plugins > MorphoLibJ > Binary Images > Connected Components Labeling), and we can then analyse it in 3D (Plugins > MorphoLibJ > Analyze > Analyze Regions 3D). You can choose which metrics you want as output; Volume and Centroid might be a good place to start.      *
The output is a table that can be saved in CSV format and analysed further in other software. The idea would be to find the closest green object to each red object by calculating the Euclidean distance among all pairs and looking at minima, or values lower than a distance threshold. 

Once you find some parameters that work well enough with the images in your dataset, you can automate the pipeline into a macro. Here you can find an example<https://imagej.github.io/tutorials/batch-processing-with-ij-macro> of how to do so. 

I'm aware that you were currently working on a pipeline in Imaris. If you'd like to keep working on that instead of testing the suggestion above, you are, of course, more than welcome to. It might be worth reaching out to whoever in the team assisted you in the first place, as they will be more versed in Imaris than I am. 

Please get in touch should you have any questions!
Best wishes,
Stefania

card.name: Problems with Auto-Segmentation tool in 3D Slicer

data.text:
### Sadiyah reached out to BIA email with issues getting model to work on her samples (17/4/26)

Needs retraining/fine-tuning model so pointed her towards SEAI, but she prob won’t have enough time seeing she’s a sandwich student about to finish..

---

Hi Sadiyah,

I’m glad to hear you were able to get the sample scan working on the HPC setup we worked on. However, the MEMOS extension uses a pre-trained model, which essentially means the software has a “fixed” idea of what a mouse embryo should look like based on their specific training data. These models tend to be very sensitive to staining variations and slight differences in developmental stage. Even if the difference by eye is barely noticeable, it’s probably enough to confuse the algorithm.

In these cases, you would have to fine-tune or retrain the model on your specific type of data, which will take some time to just get the manual annotations needed. It might be worth reaching out to the [SEAI STP](https://intranet.crick.ac.uk/our-crick/software-engineering-and-ai-seai-stp "‌") (software engineering and AI). Their team might have some better suggestions and tools.

Sorry I can’t give you any better news than this, but hopefully SEAI have a different perspective for you to explore.

Best wishes,

Sara

**From:** Sadiyah Naeem <[sadiyah.naeem@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sadiyah.naeem@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Date:** Friday, 17 April 2026 at 16:39
**To:** Crick Image Analysis Group <[bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk](mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>; Sara Salgueiro Torres <[sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Subject:** Problems with Auto-Segmentation tool in 3D Slicer

Hi Image analysis,

Hope you're well, I have some questions regarding 'Mouse Embryo Multi Organ Segmentation' or MEMOS, a mouse embryo auto-segmentation extension on 3D Slicer. I tested MEMOS using the sample scan provided in the paper, with the help of \@Sara Salgueiro Torres, and the segmentation was completely fine, however I've been having problems with the segmentation for an embryo I stained and scanned. There are some differences in the staining protocol due to limited resources, but in both protocols Lugol's Iodine solution was used to stain the embryos.

I am unsure if the incomplete segmentation is due to MEMOS not registering my embryo - my embryo is more developed (E17) compared to the sample scan (E15), or if this due to the scan I acquired. Is there anything you would suggest trying so I can get full segmentation in my embryo? I have attached the sample scan, my scan, the paper as well as the segmentations results, I would really appreciate any help, I look forward to hearing from you.

Kindest regards 

Sadiyah Naeem

Hi Zuzanna,

Thanks for your time earlier!

Here is the QuPath tutorial<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html> I mentioned. It links to other sections of their documentation, so you might need to have a look at those as well. You will want to detect cells first and then classify them in terms of positivity to your 2 markers of interest. 

Information regarding scripting in QuPath can be found at this page<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/scripting/index.html>. It's not too dissimilar from Fiji macros, with the difference that the "recording" is turned on automatically. 

Additionally, you can find instructions on how to run QuPath on OnDemand at this link<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/blob/master/software_instructions/QuPath/access_qupath_v6_nemo.md>.

data.text:
Miriam came to the Helpdesk this morning. We recommended that she acquires more images on the Observer keeping in mind the following:

- if acquiring the whole well, use overlap to facilitate stitching
- set the Z-stack with a 50-100 µm spacing, manually marking the top and the bottom of the sample (absolute settings)
- prepare a negative control where the organoids do not have any red marker to check for autofluorescence/baseline signal
- acquiring about 5-10 images for both samples and get back in touch for analysis.

A quick test showed that Yen thresholding on the red channel should work to count the red-positive organoids. The brightfield channel might not be usable. A negative ctrl is needed to figure out what counts as “red”. Stitching wasn’t tested as the current dataset has no overlap and is not in focus. She wants to image the whole well (3x3 tiles) as the organoids tend to accumulate at the edges and she can’t find a way to prepare the sample differently to avoid this.

**QuPath image analysis**

Hi Zuzanna,

Thanks for your time earlier!

Here is the QuPath tutorial<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html> I mentioned. It links to other sections of their documentation, so you might need to have a look at those as well. You will want to detect cells first and then classify them in terms of positivity to your 2 markers of interest. 

Information regarding scripting in QuPath can be found at this page<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/scripting/index.html>. It's not too dissimilar from Fiji macros, with the difference that the "recording" is turned on automatically. 

Additionally, you can find instructions on how to run QuPath on OnDemand at this link<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/blob/master/software_instructions/QuPath/access_qupath_v6_nemo.md>. 

Please get in touch with any questions!
Best wishes,
Stefania

Many thanks,
Zuzanna

### Meeting 16/4/26 – demo on how to use OC for QuPath:

Met with Joyce through Zoom to:

1. Clarify how to train the same object classifier on different images.

- She kept having some issues following day (detections had missing features and classifier would just detect everything as ‘green’). Troubleshooted and sent [demo video ](https://thefranciscrickinstitute-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/salgues_crick_ac_uk/IQC8a7yTmpBtQrok0xRI0WvhAS2kvb263Gjl9BO-UO3_UHg?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJPbmVEcml2ZUZvckJ1c2luZXNzIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXciLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJNeUZpbGVzTGlua0NvcHkifX0&e=1P8UmE "‌")on how to proceed to avoid problem.

1. Help improve nuclei segmentation by using InstanSeg extension, rather than inbuilt cell detection function.

---

**From:** Sara Salgueiro Torres <[sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Date:** Friday, 17 April 2026 at 11:28
**To:** Joyce Man <[joyce.man@crick.ac.uk](mailto:joyce.man@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>; Stefania Marcotti <[stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk](mailto:stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Subject:** Re: Project discussion

Hi Joyce,

I had the same issue you were having with that image, and I think I finally found the culprit!

It was missing the detections’ (i.e. cells’) measurements, so the classifier didn’t have the necessary information to work with and just defaulted to saying everything was ‘green_positive'. After you detect your cells, with the annotation of interest selected, you need to go to _Analyze > Calculate features > Add intensity features_:

![Screenshot 2026-04-17 at 10.37.26.png](cid:453916581*A32AA082-56A8-4355-9A50-C2C5610B8C49)

These are the parameters I used. When you click Run, another window will pop up asking what you want to apply it to, and you should select ‘Cells’ (or ‘Detections’ if that is not an available option). Now you can proceeed with your Train Object Classifier step, and it should have the information it needs to work.

‌

Another last note, under ’Selected measurements’ you should have a few new lines (‘ROI: 2.00 um per pixel…’), and you can select which one of these you’re interested in. If you’re only interested in the green channel info you can do the same as with the other measurements, and untick anything that’s not relevant to that channel.

![Screenshot 2026-04-17 at 11.23.17.png](cid:453916581*E0B5D946-F98B-4CCC-8B9F-527FB82C9D8E)

‌

Seems to be working fine for me now, and hopefully it does for you! Let me know if it doesn’t and we can figure out where the issue is coming from with a quick call.

Hi Clara,

We can certainly help. For cell segmentation, it might be sufficient to have a nuclear marker in the panel. However, depending on your scientific question, this might not be enough. Would you mind sharing a little bit more information on the project and the current panel design? 

I’m happy to have a quick chat next week if it makes things easier! 

Best wishes,
Stefania

From: Clara Triginer Salvatella <clara.triginer@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Wednesday, 15 April 2026 at 09:30
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Help with image analysis

Good morning,

I'm a 1st year PhD student at the Downward lab and I wanted to request some help for an image analysis project. 

I am currently designing a multiplex antibody panel to use on the Lunaphore COMET (EHP STP) to study the TME in subcut tumour sections. I wanted to know if you would be able to help me tailor the panel to facilitate cell segmentation. 

Please let me know if that is something you could help me with. I would be very happy to provide more information on the project and the current panel design. 

Thank you in advance,

BW
Clara

data.text:
#### Clarification on how to train OC on different images (QuPath) – 14/4/26:

Hi Joyce,

Glad to hear your image analysis project is progressing!

That is exactly what we meant. What works nicely for an image might not work as well for others. So definitely try it on a few different images that are different – conditions, acquisitions dates, look different, etc – and then adjust it on those to refine it if needed.

You will have to train the object classifier as you’ve been doing until now, but ensuring you ‘_Load training’_ and include all the images, so the annotations from your other images are taken into account. You need to save it under the exact same name. QuPath will ask you whether you want to overwrite it. 

![Screenshot 2026-04-14 at 09.51.37.png](cid:488599647*F372A536-55D2-4937-B9D2-BB9BA84776CA)

I am attaching some detailed instructions on how to do so that I wrote for another user that I now generalised so you can follow along for your own images. If it is still a bit confusing, happy to have a quick meeting to go through it and give you a little demo.

Best of luck!

Sara

**From:** Joyce Man <[joyce.man@crick.ac.uk](mailto:joyce.man@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Date:** Tuesday, 14 April 2026 at 08:22
**To:** Stefania Marcotti <[stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk](mailto:stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>, Sara Salgueiro Torres <[sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk](mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>
**Subject:** Re: Project discussion

Hi Stefania, Hi Sara,

I have a quick question about the ML approach to train for red/green positivity.

I have been training images following instructions from Qupath tutorials, and I have established my classifier for a certain image.

In your recap email you mentioned to train different images to account for more variability — do you suggest training the classifier I established for one image, test on other images and adjust the respective classifier accordingly or would you suggest something different?

Thank you.

Best wishes,

Joyce

\*\*\*\*\*\*

**Joyce Man, PhD**
Postdoctoral Project Research Scientist

Heard group | The Francis Crick Institute 

‌

The Francis Crick Institute 

1 Midland Road 

London NW1 1AT 

[www.crick.ac.uk](http://www.crick.ac.uk/ "‌")

From: Crick Image Analysis Group
Sent: 03 March 2026 15:30
To: Chengxuan Xie <chengxuan.xie@crick.ac.uk>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: Automated image analysis pipeline building

Hi Jerry,

Happy to help if we can.

Could you give us a little more detail on what your aims are? 

For example, you say you need support building a pipeline on HPC, but is this something you already have experience with? If so, how much? And what is the purpose of the pipeline? What is the nature of the data being analysed and how big is it? 

Once we know more we can discuss how best to proceed.

Best,

Dave.

From: Chengxuan Xie <chengxuan.xie@crick.ac.uk<mailto:chengxuan.xie@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 02 March 2026 12:47
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Automated image analysis pipeline building

Dear Sir/Madam,

Hope this email finds you well.

I am a new PhD student started October, and I am seeking for some support on building an automated image analysis pipeline on the HPC. Would you be able to inform me how the process goes? E.g. who should I be in contact with and what information would be needed? Thank you very much. 

Best,
Jerry

Please do not feel obligated to respond outside your work hours. From: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Tuesday, 7 April 2026 at 15:45
To: Emily Harders <emily.harders@crick.ac.uk>, Sara Salgueiro Torres <sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk> Cc: stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36bg5y5qeag33a39tpr+2gfkwlp0we@boards.trello.com <stefaniam27+34x66c9at9lcagq8c9a+36bg5y5qeag33a39tpr+2gfkwlp0we@boards.trello.com> Subject: Re: Emily Harders - Image Analysis discussion

Hi Emily,

Thanks for your time earlier!

Please find below some notes from the meeting.

  *
Regarding getting you started on HPC, please go to the HPC helpdesk and get them to set you up (every Mon, Wed and Fri at 10 near the café). You can also Slack them on #hpc or #hpc-beginners. They also have a regular introductory course<https://wd3.myworkday.com/crick/learning/course/445792f3538a10015781a34c54460000?type=9882927d138b100019b6a2df1a46018b> that you can find on Workday. You can refer to their wiki<https://wiki.thecrick.org/display/HPCuserwiki/High+Performance+Computing+%28HPC%29+user+wiki> for more information.   *
Instructions on how to setup QuPath on OnDemand can be found here<https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/Image-Analysis-Group/blob/master/software_instructions/QuPath/access_qupath_v6_nemo.md>.   *
@sara Salgueiro Torres<mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk> can you please send Emily the instructions on how to connect OMERO with QuPath (on OnDemand)? Thanks!   *
Some documentation on scripting with QuPath can be found in their documentation at this link<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/0.6/docs/scripting/index.html>.   *
The Carpentries<https://carpentries.org/> have some good material on git here<https://swcarpentry.github.io/git-novice/>. GitHub Education<https://github.com/education> <https://github.com/education> has more resources on using GitHub for academic purposes. 

Good luck with your image analysis journey!
Best wishes,
Stefania

cards.desc:
Now that we are expanding in size, I’d like us to make more of a contributiopn to the broader bioimage analysis community. We use a lot of tools developed by others and I think we could start doing “our bit” on this front”. We’re not software developers, so I don’t think assisting with “development” is necessarily the way to go, but we could contribute in other ways, even if it’s just logging bug reports, helping to  update documentation, puttting together tutorials, etc.

Maybe we all pick a particular tool that we like and reserve a little time each week to work on it?

cards.desc:
The idea has been floated that we organise a get-together with groups who did similar things elsewhere:

- Jean-Yves' group at Pasteur
- Christian Tischer at EMBL
- Anna Klemm at SciLifeLab
- Damian Dalle Nogare at Human Technopole

We had originally considered organising something in conjunction with CBIAS this year, but never got around to it in the end. But maybe next year. Might be difficult to get funding for all team members to attend, but lets see.

Exchanging individual people between groups is also a possibility - seem to be lots of funding sources for that kind of thing.

From: Quadri Omotesho <quadri.omotesho@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Monday, April 27, 2026 8:56:48 AM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Cc: Sara Salgueiro Torres <sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Request for help with image analysis

Thank you for your response, I have booked a time with Sara for next week. Concerning the background of the project. 

  1.
What is the biological question you are trying to answer? -- My goal is to characterise the liver in the tumour bearing context and to assess if there are changes in some specific markers such as CD34 which stains for cells in proliferative state. My observation is that I see that CD34 stains cells around the blood vessels in the liver from Naive mice while in the tumour-bearing mice, I see that in addition to staining cells around the liver, there are also CD34 cells now appearing in the liver tissue outside of the blood vessels   2.
What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis?-- So I want to quantify the number of CD34+ stains blood vessels in both naive and tumour bearing context as well as quantify CD34+ cells outside the blood vessels   3.
With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP-- I used the Axio Scan EVOS at the EHP to scan the images   4.
How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more? --I currently have about 30 images that I aim to quantify, and I will be acquiring more   5.
What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?— I have two channels in the image I shared (555 and 647), and I have two conditions - Liver from Naive and Tumour bearing (TB) 

I have been having issue uploading the images to the output folder for my lab as it is saying that the disk is full, I will reach out to IT for help, and I will inform you when it works. Thank you Kind regards,

Quadri Omotesho
From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Date: Thursday, 23 April 2026 at 10:56
To: Quadri Omotesho <quadri.omotesho@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: Sara Salgueiro Torres <sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Request foe help with image analysis

Hi Quadri,

We can definitely give you some guidance. It’ll be much easier to schedule a meeting to understand your project and images a bit better. Are you available next week for an online chat? You can book a slot in my calendar next week here<https://outlook.office.com/bookwithme/user/d4b915488d774ba3bb17791e0c9e1b93@crick.ac.uk/meetingtype/Yam2esON2kae3dlTpjO8-A2?bookingcode=74e9a514-72e2-4b40-a872-4499b615e181&anonymous&ismsaljsauthenabled&ep=mlink>. Also happy to meet on a different week if that suits you better. 

Ahead of that meeting, could you share a bit more background on your project? 

  *   What is the biological question you are trying to answer?   *
What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis (e.g., %CD34 nuclei in a tissue sample)? Any reference paper you are following?   *
With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?   *
How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?   *   What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls? 

Finally, could you also share some representative images with me so I can test a few approaches? If they live in OMERO, you can share the project ID and I can already access them. If they are in your NEMO storage, you can share some images with us in your lab space (stp/light-microscopy/outputs). You can make a folder in that directory with a representative name (e.g., quadri_for_sara) and I’ll be able to access any files you add to it. 

Best wishes,
Sara

From: Quadri Omotesho <quadri.omotesho@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Thursday, 23 April 2026 at 09:22
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Request foe help with image analysis

Good morning,

I am writing to request your guidance with an unbiased quantification of my IF images. The image is attached below and it is a liver tissue stained with CD34 (in white) and CD45 (in red). I would like to quantify the number of cells expressing the CD34 around the blood vessels (circled in black) versus the number of CD34 stained cells on the normal tissue parenchyma. Is there a way I can separate these two quantifications in an objective way. Right now, I am manually annotating the blood vessels and running Num of cell detected but I feel this might be a bit biased and I am wondering if there is a way to do this more efficiently. I am happy to schedule a chat if that is important for more clarity. I look forward to hearing from you. Thank you 

[image.png]
Kind regards,

Quadri Omotesho

From: STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk> Sent: 30 January 2026 11:15
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: FW: Image quantification and representation

Sent from Outlook for Mac

From: Amalie Grenov <amalie.grenov@crick.ac.uk<mailto:amalie.grenov@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Thursday, 29 January 2026 at 13:12
To: STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk<mailto:calm@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Image quantification and representation

Hi CALM,

We have a paper under revision using some multiplex (6 colour) immunofluorescence images of human spleen 

The reviewers are asking, which is fair enough, how representative our results are across patients/healthy controls/sections or regions per spleen. 

Some findings are and questions:

  *   Plasma cell aggregates close to vasculature
     *   How common is this pattern (per patient, per area)
     *   Compare patients to healthy control spleens in a systematic way   *   Clusters of CD19+/- CD138+/- Ig+/- cells
     *   How prevalent are these clusters
     *   What proportion are CD19/CD138/Ig positive/negative
  *   CD11c+ CD19+ cells
     *   Quantify number in patient vs healthy control spleen      *   What fraction are ki-67+

Would you be able to help with quantification of some of these findings, or at the minimum how to display the data in a representative transparent way? 

Thank you very much,

Amalie

Amalie Grenov

Postdoctoral researcher

Lab of Carola Vinuesa

Francis Crick institute

London, UK

Hi Stephania,

Thank you for your reply and suggestions! I had a try at your method and it seems the error persisted. I’ve also tried to set the image to G=green rather than RGB in the input and it gives the same result nonetheless. Please kindly find the attached image results. Thank you! [image.png]

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Date: Friday, 24 April 2026 at 14:38
To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing!

I believe the problem might be related to your input file format. From the screenshots you are sending, it looks like the local version of Cellpose is picking up information from the wrong channel, or from a mixture of channels. 

Have you tried using a .tif file as input for only the green channel? You can obtain this by importing the .czi file into Fiji with Bio-Formats and saving the green channel separately as .tif. You can then test feeding this as input to the local version of Cellpose. 

Please let me know if it works!

Best wishes,
Stefania

----------------------------------
From: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Date: Thursday, 23 April 2026 at 17:43
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Dear Image Analysis Group,

Hope you’re doing well.

This is Jing and I’m a first-year PhD student at Gandhi Lab. I’m currently running into an issue with Cellpose segmentation and was hoping to get your advice. I’m working with max-projected composite PNG images (derived from .czi files), and I’ve noticed a discrepancy between the Cellpose website output and my local terminal GUI runs. 

Specifically:

  *   The exact same input PNG gives very good segmentation on the Cellpose website (Cellpose-SAM). (Please see attached Image_Cellposeweb). 

  *   However, when I run Cellpose locally (also using Cellpose-SAM), the segmentation appears distorted. (Pleasee see attached Image_GUI) 

I’ve tried to control for the main variables:

  *   Using the same input image

  *   Matching the model (Cellpose-SAM)

  *   Adjusting different channel settings

  *   Trying auto and manual diameters

I also confirmed that the issue is not due to preprocessing differences, as I’m using the same PNG exported. Please also find my cellpose version below: 

cellpose version: 4.1.1

platform:       darwin

python version: 3.14.2

torch version:  2.9.1

May I please ask if you may have encountered the same issue or have any suggestions? I’m happy to discuss or meet at any time you are convenient if needed. 

Thanks very much for your time and help in advance!

Best regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student
Gandhi Lab, The Francis Crick Institute

To: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Hi Jing!

I believe the problem might be related to your input file format. From the screenshots you are sending, it looks like the local version of Cellpose is picking up information from the wrong channel, or from a mixture of channels. 

Have you tried using a .tif file as input for only the green channel? You can obtain this by importing the .czi file into Fiji with Bio-Formats and saving the green channel separately as .tif. You can then test feeding this as input to the local version of Cellpose. 

Please let me know if it works!

Best wishes,
Stefania

----------------------------------

Dear Image Analysis Group,

Hope you’re doing well.

This is Jing and I’m a first-year PhD student at Gandhi Lab. I’m currently running into an issue with Cellpose segmentation and was hoping to get your advice. I’m working with max-projected composite PNG images (derived from .czi files), and I’ve noticed a discrepancy between the Cellpose website output and my local terminal GUI runs. 

Specifically:

  *   The exact same input PNG gives very good segmentation on the Cellpose website (Cellpose-SAM). (Please see attached Image_Cellposeweb). 

  *   However, when I run Cellpose locally (also using Cellpose-SAM), the segmentation appears distorted. (Pleasee see attached Image_GUI) 

I’ve tried to control for the main variables:

  *   Using the same input image

  *   Matching the model (Cellpose-SAM)

  *   Adjusting different channel settings

  *   Trying auto and manual diameters

I also confirmed that the issue is not due to preprocessing differences, as I’m using the same PNG exported. Please also find my cellpose version below: 

cellpose version: 4.1.1

platform:       darwin

python version: 3.14.2

torch version:  2.9.1

May I please ask if you may have encountered the same issue or have any suggestions? I’m happy to discuss or meet at any time you are convenient if needed. 

Thanks very much for your time and help in advance!

Best regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student

To: STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Image quantification and representation

Hi CALM,

We have a paper under revision using some multiplex (6 colour) immunofluorescence images of human spleen 

The reviewers are asking, which is fair enough, how representative our results are across patients/healthy controls/sections or regions per spleen. 

Some findings are and questions:

  *
Plasma cell aggregates close to vasculature
     *
How common is this pattern (per patient, per area)
     *
Compare patients to healthy control spleens in a systematic way   *
Clusters of CD19+/- CD138+/- Ig+/- cells
     *
How prevalent are these clusters
     *
What proportion are CD19/CD138/Ig positive/negative
  *
CD11c+ CD19+ cells
     *
Quantify number in patient vs healthy control spleen
     *
What fraction are ki-67+

Would you be able to help with quantification of some of these findings, or at the minimum how to display the data in a representative transparent way? 

Thank you very much,

Amalie

Amalie Grenov
Postdoctoral researcher
Lab of Carola Vinuesa
Francis Crick institute

yes, we can help. Certainly in a faster way to render and visualise the data. Regarding quantification, including cell detection and measurements, it will need more time and it might be necessary to set up a project. 

If you wish, we can chat to have a look at the data and see how you would like to proceed.

From: STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk<mailto:calm@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 30 January 2026 11:15

From: Amalie Grenov <amalie.grenov@crick.ac.uk<mailto:amalie.grenov@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Thursday, 29 January 2026 at 13:12
To: STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk<mailto:calm@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Image quantification and representation

Hi CALM,

We have a paper under revision using some multiplex (6 colour) immunofluorescence images of human spleen 

The reviewers are asking, which is fair enough, how representative our results are across patients/healthy controls/sections or regions per spleen. 

Some findings are and questions:

  *   Plasma cell aggregates close to vasculature

     *   How common is this pattern (per patient, per area)
     *   Compare patients to healthy control spleens in a systematic way 

  *   Clusters of CD19+/- CD138+/- Ig+/- cells

     *   How prevalent are these clusters
     *   What proportion are CD19/CD138/Ig positive/negative

  *   CD11c+ CD19+ cells

     *   Quantify number in patient vs healthy control spleen      *   What fraction are ki-67+

Would you be able to help with quantification of some of these findings, or at the minimum how to display the data in a representative transparent way? 

Thank you very much,

Amalie

From: Sara Salgueiro Torres <sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk> Sent: 15 January 2026 3:14 PM
To: Dani Diaper <dani.diaper@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Help with microscopy images

Hi Dani,

I can absolutely help with that! I can easily get you the stills you need, but I can also help you download FIJI on your laptop and show you how to save the stills you want from the files. Is there a specific file format and quality you need it in? 

Here are some instructions on how to do so, in case you want to give it a go yourself. It might be useful for you and your colleagues for future articles: ——————
Downloading FIJI (locally)
What error/issue are you getting when trying to download FIJI? And are you on a Mac or a Windows machine? You can download FIJI from here<https://fiji.sc/> – I’d recommend selecting the Stable version. 

Once FIJI is running…

  1.
Open the image

  *
On the top menu, navigate to Plugins>Bio-Formats>Bio-Formats Importer   *
Select the TIF file you want to open
  *
A window with a lot of options will pop up. Just make sure that you have the following selected:      *
“Hyperstack” (top left)
     *
Unselect the “Split Channels” option (bottom right)
     *
“Composite” in the Colour Options (bottom left)

[Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.33.57.png]
Once the image opens, you will see two sliders to control the channels (colours) and the timepoints: [Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.35.16.png]

  2. Change colours
If the colours of the channels are not the ones you want for the figures, you can select the channel you want to change with the channel slider (red arrow in the image above). The blue arrow points to the window that you have selected – if it’s in red, it’s the red channel. Then you can change the colours by clicking on the LUT button: [Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.41.05.png]

  3. Convert to a still
There’re a few ways you could do this, but the more straightforward way is to go to Image>Duplicate. Then you can select what frame from Slices tab in the window that pops up: [Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.49.38.png]

  4. Flatten the image
You might need to “flatten” the channels, so the colours look right in Photoshop/InDesign: 

  *
Image > Type > RGB Color

  5. Save the new image
Select the newly generated RGB and then navigate to File > Save > Save As and choose the file type you want (maybe PNG?). 

——————

If you’re getting stuck and need them tomorrow afternoon, I can also send you the files. It’s a quick job! Just let me know what format you need them in. 

I will be on long-term leave from next week, so in case there is anything else you need a hand with re images, you can get in touch with our team at bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk . 

I hope that helps!

Best,
Sara

------

Sara Salgueiro Torres (she/her)

Junior Image Analyst

Crick Advanced Light Microscopy

Office: NE316

The Francis Crick Institute

1 Midland Road, London NW1 1AT

Please do not feel obligated to respond outside your work hours.

Hi Tushar,

Thanks for getting in touch!

As far as I am aware, the Crick usually only hosts summer students via its “official” summer student program<https://www.crick.ac.uk/careers-and-study/students/summer-students>, the deadline for which is fast approaching for 2026 placements. But unfortunately, this program is only open to students based at UK universities. 

Do you have your own funding for this placement? I’m afraid we do not have any discretionary funding for such things. There are funding schemes to facilitate visits overseas (such as Imaging4All<https://globalbioimaging.org/i4a>), but they are generally only open to full-time researchers. 

But, there may be other opportunities in the future – it’s probably a good idea to follow the Crick on social media<https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-francis-crick-institute/> and keep an eye out for future announcements. 

Sorry I can’t give you better news, but I hope you find a placement that’s to your liking. 

I wonder if you’ve contacted any imaging facilities closer to home? We have some contacts in India who might offer placements? 

Best,

Dave.

From: Tushar Agarwal <tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in>
Sent: 27 January 2026 21:21
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Regarding Working with your Over the Summer

External Sender: Use caution.

Dear team,

I came across your page through the description of your upcoming one-day course Machine learning-based image analysis with ilastik. In my university's Biophysics lab, I am involved in the study of the collective dynamics and self-organization properties of active matter in confinement (with C. elegans as a model organism). The research involves a lot of image analysis on large sets of images I collect through fluorescence and bright-field microscopy, and recently I have started picking up machine learning to automate the image analysis process. And, given my interest in the work you do and my desire to build the skills in which you have an expertise, I decided to reach out. 

I am looking for a research opportunity abroad for the summer 2026. I am a 3rd-year undergrad physics major at Ashoka University in India. The training I believe I will get working with you over the summer will tremendously help me with my research goals. I have seen the applications of machine learning and image analysis in many, many fields, including diagnosis of diseases like schistosomiasis and malaria, surveillance and taxonomic classification of mosquitoes and other animals, etc. I have been involved in some of these projects myself. And, given my passion for applications of physics in medicine and public health, I believe it is important for me to pick these skills and ideas up. 

If you are willing to host me over the summer, please do let me know. I'd love to contribute to your work and help wherever I can in return. I am attaching my CV and some very basic image analysis work I had done for my project as reference. Looking forward! 

Best,
Tushar

From: Tamara Catrin Klockner <tamara.klockner@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Thursday, January 22, 2026 4:17:33 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Please help with image analysis

Dear CALM Team,

Together with Ania from EHP we are developing a 6-plex IF that I will use to identify a very rare AT2 intermediate in lung repair (CLDN4+, KRT8+) in normal lung FFPE blocks from TRACERx. Additionally, I would like to quantify HE4 levels in AT2 cells. 

I attached you some screenshots of the IF images. We are measuring: 

  *
SFTPA (green, AT2 cells and secreted to lumen)
  *
CLDN4 (yellow, should be on membrane, but here nonspecific nuclear)   *
KRT8 (white)
  *
KRT5 (cyan, basal cells)
  *
HE4 (red)
  *
KI67 (no image attached) and DAPI

Could you help me learn how to analyse these images properly in an automated fashion? I don't know how to do that and there are several challenges: 

  *
I would need to identify AT2 cells based on SFTPA, which is also secreted into the mucus (see attached image).   *
I only assume that cytoplasmic/membrane CLDN4 staining is correct, how to remove the unspecific strong nuclear stain from the analysis?   *
I likely could use KRT8 for the outline of the cell, but it (normally) won't be present in all AT2 cells.   *
CLDN4+ and KRT8+ cells will be very rare.

I would be very grateful if you can find some time to help me. 

Thank you so much!

All the best,
Tamara

Tamara C. Klockner
postdoctoral fellow
Swanton Lab

The Francis Crick Institute

London NW1 1AT

cards.name: RE: Meeting to discuss cytoskeleton analysis

cards.desc:
Hello,


I was hoping to get some help with some image analysis of a spatial dataset and
thought I'd best get the experts in.


I have four cosmx slides that have each been cell segmented by the AtoMx
software using the IF markers PanCK, DAPI, CD68 and CD45.  So far, I've been
concentrating on getting signal from the transcriptomics data but now wish to
see if I can leverage accompanying H&E images to segment out tissue niches.  The
tissue in question is kidney biopsy and I'd be looking to isolate the glomeruli
from each of the 4 H&E and relate those regions back to my transcriptomics data.
Thus, I'm imagining that I'll need to align each H&E to my slide (affinder?) and
then run some glomeruli segmentation.  The dataset isn't massive, so I was
considering using a pre-existing segmentation method - e.g. "Glo-In-One"?


I received my H&E in .svs format, which I converted to ome.tiff using QuPath.


My cosmx images are stitched across the multiple FOVs/slide and saved as zarr
using the method described here:
https://nanostring-biostats.github.io/CosMx-Analysis-Scratch-Space/posts/napari-stitching/napari-cosmx-stitching.html


There are four biopsies / slide but each biopsy may be made of >=1 distinct
tissue region.  I can visually see differences in the IF intensities across the
slide that might be technical but likely reflective of the biology/sample
quality.


If possible, I'd also like to try and segment peri-tubular capillaries as well
but frankly I think that's going to be tough going so the glomeruli would be a
good start.


Any help or advice would be much appreciated.  


Thanks
Richard.
cards.name: Segmentation of H&E and alignment to CosMx images

cards.desc:
Here is the information in preparation for the meeting.

Representative images can be found on OMERO under OK\_20x\_20260303. It is a dataset with 5 channels of some sagittal brain slices.

**What is the biological question you are trying to answer?**
Our lab is interested in autoimmune psychosis. As part of our clinical studies, we receive patient serum and CSF samples, which we screen for autoantibodies using a range of methods. One of our aims is to identify patterns in antibody binding across different patient groups and determine whether there are distinct antibody profiles associated with specific clinical cases.

**Briefly describe the samples you are imaging/have imaged.**
The images are from a tissue-based assay using mouse brain sections. Patient serum or CSF is used as the primary antibody source, followed by immunostaining and whole-slide scanning. We have been sending the slides to Donald for scanning.

**What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis?**
We quantify the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) in specific brain regions. Previously, this has been done by manually drawing ROIs in QuPath and extracting the MFI values. We are now trying to automate this process.

**With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?**
The images were acquired by Donald through the CALM STP as whole-slide scans.

**How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?**
Around 200 available training images from different scanning session.

**What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?**
The images contain the immunofluorescence channel used to detect patient antibody binding, along with the nuclear stain. The dataset includes both patient samples and control samples.

**Is there any reference paper you are following for the analysis?**
No specific analysis paper. Our current workflow has been developed in-house.

To give some additional context, I have already trained a QuPath pixel classifier that can accurately distinguish the hippocampus and surrounding regions. We found that analysing this area alone is sufficient to discriminate between cases and controls, and the MFI values extracted using the classifier correlate strongly with those obtained using manually drawn ROIs.

One problem I have had with the classifier is that both DAPI and AutoFL are necessary for distinguishing regions, but the AutoFL can be very variable between scanning sessions, how to overcome this?

The remaining challenge is that the classifier currently requires me to manually draw a polygon around the hippocampus before running the analysis. We were wondering whether QuPath has a way to automatically identify or generate this polygon so that the entire workflow could be fully automated.

Dear Image Analysis team,


I hope this email finds you well, my name is Anson and I’m a PhD student in the
Fratta Lab. With Auri (cced), a postdoc in the lab, we been investigating the
effects of TDP-43 loss-of-function-induced Golgi fragmentation in ALS-FTD
models. As part of this, we have been looking at the effects of TDP-43 LoF on
the cytoskeleton in neuroblastoma models by IF. However, as we are not
well-versed in how to analyse our results, we would greatly appreciate your

chat about this next week or the week after? We have some images that we can
share for discussion. Thank you so much!


Best wishes,
Anson

Hi Sara,


Sorry to bother you again but I find myself stuck once more and not sure what I
am doing wrong. 


I have used QuPath to generate ROIs and within those have applied a pixel
classifier I trained to make Annotations of Class Tumour based on the intensity
of the FITC channel.


I am then using those annotations to run Stardist in and detect all nuclei.
 Even though I have installed the Stardist extension and I have downloaded the
models, and have added the folder which contains the models in my script, I get
the following error:


ERROR: I couldn't find the model file
/camp/home/nikolak/home/users/nikolak/Histology/QuPath
Directory/dsb2018_heavy_augment.pb in QuPathScript at line number 26
qupath.ext.stardist.StarDist2D$Builder.build(StarDist2D.java:675)

For help interpreting this error, please search the forum at
https://forum.image.sc/tag/qupath
You can also start a new discussion there, including both your script & the

For reference, I have also tried

Do you have any ideas on what I may be missing here? Thank you!


Best wishes,

a mix of acquisition and image analysis people, but the next one is in 2 weeks
(it was on yesterday). 


I've cc'd the image analysis team so this gets onto their radar. 


From an acquisition point of view, this sounds suitable for a widefield
approach. My understanding is that once you have the data, the company's
software reads the barcodes from the brightfield channel, does some processing
to extract each carrier and you then use other software to analyse the
fluorescent channels.


The key step could be data management and getting the images in a format that
the software can read it - this may be a job for OMERO (@kenneth

Matt

Hi Dave,

Apologies for the delay in getting back to you.

Please find attached one of the images we collected. [https://res-2.df.onecdn.static.microsoft/files/fabric-cdn-prod_20251010.003/assets/item-types/16/photo.svg] 20260324_3Zs_9FOVs_n3_plate2_1_MMStack_D2-Site_3.ome.tif<https://thefranciscrickinstitute-my.sharepoint.com/:i:/g/personal/mugabom_crick_ac_uk/IQAx-uiCRhr2TJU3NnmNFagIAd5-Yxptw1lctgZQwXYjT0o> . 

The image is one of 9 sites of a transwell in a 48 well plate. There should be a fluorescent organoid in magenta, while the rest is also small organoids. 

The aim of the experiment was to see if we would get more and/or bigger mutant organoids if we cocultured them with pollution treated macrophages vs control macrophages. The mutant macrophages are fluorescent td tomato. 

I treated the co cultures with different inhibitors to see if they would have any effect on organoid size and number. 

I collected 9 FOVs per transwell (covering almost entire area of the transwell) and 3 planes per FOV. 

Best wishes,

Miriam

Hi Image analysis,

Hope you're well, I have some questions regarding 'Mouse Embryo Multi Organ Segmentation' or MEMOS, a mouse embryo auto-segmentation extension on 3D Slicer. I tested MEMOS using the sample scan provided in the paper, with the help of @sara Salgueiro Torres<mailto:sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk>, and the segmentation was completely fine, however I've been having problems with the segmentation for an embryo I stained and scanned. There are some differences in the staining protocol due to limited resources, but in both protocols Lugol's Iodine solution was used to stain the embryos. 

I am unsure if the incomplete segmentation is due to MEMOS not registering my embryo - my embryo is more developed (E17) compared to the sample scan (E15), or if this due to the scan I acquired. Is there anything you would suggest trying so I can get full segmentation in my embryo? I have attached the sample scan, my scan, the paper as well as the segmentations results, I would really appreciate any help, I look forward to hearing from you. 

Kindest regards
Sadiyah Naeem

Sent: Monday, February 16, 2026 4:07:02 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Cc: Stefania Marcotti <stefania.marcotti@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: Re: Project discussion

Dear Stefania,

Thank you for sending the calendar invite.

To follow up on some information about my project, I want to understand how and why autosomal random monoallelic expression occurs. 

Autosomal random monoallelic expression (aRME) is an epigenetic phenomenon in which only one allele of a gene is expressed in a given cell in a stable, clonal manner independent of parental origin.  Work from the Heard lab has demonstrated that aRME is established during differentiation of pluripotent cells and can be stably propagated as in X-chromosome inactivation. 

In the project, I am trying to understand how aRME is resolved during development using a mouse model in which our gene of interest (Bag3) is coupled to a cleavable, nuclear-localised GPF or mCherry on either allele. EHP has performed IF staining on sections of the brain and the heart, and this was then imaged on the slide scanner. 

With the obtained imaging data, I would like to count/analyse the portion of nuclei showing: 1) GFP+/mCherry+, 2) only GFP+ or 3) only mCherry+ nuclei in the brain and the heart. 

I have shared some example data through NEMO and I am happy to discuss further about the project this Friday. Please let me know if you have any questions before or have issues opening the files. 

Apologies for the long project explanation, but I hope this will be helpful ahead of the meeting. 

See you then!

Best wishes,
Joyce

******
Joyce Man, PhD
Postdoctoral Project Research Scientist
Heard group | The Francis Crick Institute

The Francis Crick Institute

London NW1 1AT

[https://res-2.df.onecdn.static.microsoft/files/fabric-cdn-prod_20251010.003/assets/item-types/16/photo.svg] 20260324_3Zs_9FOVs_n3_plate2_1_MMStack_D2-Site_3.ome.tif<https://thefranciscrickinstitute-my.sharepoint.com/:i:/g/personal/mugabom_crick_ac_uk/IQAx-uiCRhr2TJU3NnmNFagIAd5-Yxptw1lctgZQwXYjT0o> . 

The image is one of 9 sites of a transwell in a 48 well plate. There should be a fluorescent organoid in magenta, while the rest is also small organoids. 

The aim of the experiment was to see if we would get more and/or bigger mutant organoids if we cocultured them with pollution treated macrophages vs control macrophages. The mutant macrophages are fluorescent td tomato. 

I treated the co cultures with different inhibitors to see if they would have any effect on organoid size and number. 

I collected 9 FOVs per transwell (covering almost entire area of the transwell) and 3 planes per FOV. 

Best wishes,
Miriam

Sent: Thursday, February 5, 2026 9:02:38 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Siddhartha Sharma <siddhartha.sharma@crick.ac.uk>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>; STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk> Cc: roccodantuono+2rx760dnreqtb7v11l3+35z1g3iq2u19ayw1ouy+1teeygpsh1@boards.trello.com <roccodantuono+2rx760dnreqtb7v11l3+35z1g3iq2u19ayw1ouy+1teeygpsh1@boards.trello.com> Subject: RE: ImageStream Analysis

Hi Siddhartha,
just to fix in my memory the brief conversation on your experimental plan before my memory fades over the weekend, and for my colleagues to be aware. 

At the moment there are no images yet, the user is interested in imaging single molecule with ImageStream. My past as flow operator says the cytometer most probably has dry objective (low NA), so single molecule imaging might be more difficult that it would be on a TIRF microscope or where oil objectives can be used and imaging condition be optimised with longer exposure. 

To clarify this and  keep my knowledge at pace, Siddartha will ask Phil Hobson and/or colleagues in flow if they have example of single molecule experiments done with ImageStream and what is the intensity of signals. 

In case, we are happy to explore ImageStream imaging or rely on our veteran of single molecule microscopy Gregory Mashanov. 

Thank you for the chat.
Best regards,
Rocco

From: Siddhartha Sharma <siddhartha.sharma@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 04 February 2026 14:26
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: ImageStream Analysis

Hello,

I am interested in assessing the IRF5 phosphorylation state, and translocation to the nucleus. 

To assess this, I am going to use ImageStream cytometry. I know that it’s relatively straightforward to assess translocation of transcription factors by ImageStream. 

However, IRF5 has many phosphorylation states and previous papers looked at them in totality using the phos-tag system. This system binds all phosphorylation sites in the total proteome and can come with a biotin tag. 

I have a hunch that I could combine a pan anti-IRF5 antibody with this phosphotag system. I think it might be informative to know the percent of translocated IRF5, and the number of phosphorylated sites on IRF5. 

I know image analysis can handle the translocation of IRF5 into the nucleus. However, can it also discern the phosphorylation number of single molecules? 

If you’d like to meet in person to discuss, I’d be happy to meet with you. 

All the best,
Sidd

Hi,

I hope you’re doing well.

I am currently running a Nextflow pipeline relying on Singularity containers for cell phenotyping in tumour samples. The pipeline completes successfully without any apparent errors, and I obtain outputs such as the summary maps and phenotype maps. However, I have noticed that the tissue segmentation and composite generation steps are not executed. The corresponding output folders are empty, and consequently the cell object tables are also empty. 

This workflow previously worked on an earlier dataset, but the issue started when I reran the pipeline after adding new images. 

I have already discussed this with Mihaela who created the pipeline and then with the HPC helpdesk, since the problem appears to be related to Fiji in my nemo environment. We were not able to identify the cause so far. As a next step, I wanted to check with you in case this is a known issue related to Fiji. 

When running the pipeline, I encounter the following error message: 

“File is not in a supported format, a reader plugin is not available, or it was not found.” 

I was wondering whether you have encountered a similar issue before, or if you might have suggestions on what to investigate next. 

Thank you very much for your help.

Kind regards,
Alexandrine

From: Siddhartha Sharma <siddhartha.sharma@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Wednesday, February 4, 2026 2:26:15 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: ImageStream Analysis

Hello,

I am interested in assessing the IRF5 phosphorylation state, and translocation to the nucleus. 

To assess this, I am going to use ImageStream cytometry. I know that it’s relatively straightforward to assess translocation of transcription factors by ImageStream. 

However, IRF5 has many phosphorylation states and previous papers looked at them in totality using the phos-tag system. This system binds all phosphorylation sites in the total proteome and can come with a biotin tag. 

I have a hunch that I could combine a pan anti-IRF5 antibody with this phosphotag system. I think it might be informative to know the percent of translocated IRF5, and the number of phosphorylated sites on IRF5. 

I know image analysis can handle the translocation of IRF5 into the nucleus. However, can it also discern the phosphorylation number of single molecules? 

If you’d like to meet in person to discuss, I’d be happy to meet with you. 

All the best,
Sidd

- Yasemin Baris is seeking support for image analysis related to single-molecule TIRF experiments.
- The focus is on automating data analysis for molecule tracking, stoichiometry, and dwell-time analysis.
- Yasemin has experience in biochemistry but seeks guidance in data analysis.
- David Barry suggests using Python packages, mentioning PySTACHIO as a potential tool.
- A meeting is scheduled for Tuesday, 9 June 2026, from 11:00 to 12:00 GMT with David Barry.
- Yasemin is waitlisted for an Introduction to Image Analysis course next week.
cards.name: Image analysis support for Yasemin Baris
cards.originalDesc:
From: David Barry
Sent: 28 May 2026 09:40
To: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>; Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>; Gregory Mashanov <Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi Yasemin,

I'm copying in @Todd Fallesen<mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk> and @Gregory Mashanov<mailto:Gregory.Mashanov@crick.ac.uk> who probably have more experience with this kind of analysis than me. 

Can you give us  a little bit more background and a bit more detail on what you've done so far and what you're planning to do? 

Have you already begun imaging and acquiring data? If so, could you possibly share some image data with us? 

Have you already tried to analyse the results in any way? If so, what software have you used? What approaches do other people in your lab use? Are there any previously published methods that you are trying to implement? 

There are some general approaches to tracking we use quite a lot, but the kind of trajectory analysis you're looking for is a bit more niche. The tracking bit is, in a sense, the "easy" bit and there are a few different options for that, but analysing the trajectories to extract "molecule tracking, stoichiometry, and dwell-time analysis" is definitely not "standard" - it's probably doable with a mix of different python packages (for example), but I doubt there's a single tool out there that can do all this. 

For example, there's PySTACHIO from Mark Leake's group in York: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2001037021002944. Mark's a very nice guy and he's well known in the UK microscopy community, so that might be a good place to start? But I haven't used it myself, so can't comment on the specifics of the software. It doesn't seem to be terribly well documented, but it does seem like it's being actively developed, which is a good sign. 

In the past, people would have generally written their own code and scripts to do this kind of analysis (as myself, Greg and probably Todd can attest to), but let's see if we can't reuse what's already out there before heading down that route (or at least keep the amount of new code to a minimum - no sense reinventing the wheel). 

Greg/Todd - anything to add?

Dave.

From: Yasemin Baris <yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk<mailto:yasemin.baris@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 27 May 2026 16:06
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: New booking: Yasemin Baris for Yasemin Baris - Image Analysis Support 

Hi David,

Many thanks for your email.

Yes, we haven't met yet. I am currently a visiting scientist and incoming postdoctoral fellow in the Yardimci Group. I have been working on single-molecule TIRF experiments and hope to extract and automate data analysis for molecule tracking, stoichiometry, and dwell-time analysis, which I understand are fairly standard approaches in single-molecule analysis. 

Since I am new to the data analysis side of things (my background is in biochemistry), I thought it would be very useful to have a chat with you. 

I have also been waitlisted for the Introduction to Image Analysis course next week and have not yet been enrolled. I was wondering whether there would be any update on the enrolment. 

Best wishes,
Yasemin

cards.desc:
Christine Dambietz reached out on Slack after the MDIA 2025 course regarding planning some image analysis pipeline. She was assisted by Rocco on the scope. She’s trying to quantify co-localisation between mytotracker (magenta) and an autophagy reporter (green or red depending on activation state). Note that mytotracker bleeds through to the red channel. She has both low and high mag confocal images and acquiring more (with Cami) in the near future, probably without mytotracker.

Suggested analysis

1. on low mag images, measure co-localisation with CellProfiler between mytotracker + activated autophagy marker, mytotracker + native autophagy marker, mytotracker + nuclei (internal control)
2. on high res images, measure area and signal intensity on green and red channels in Fiji (`Analyze particles` on binary mask redirected to original channel)

For now she is playing around to find a good pipeline, she will be in touch when she acquires more images.
cards.name: Co-localisation of autophagy reporter

From: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Thursday, 23 October 2025 at 12:59
To: Sarah Ma <sarah.ma@crick.ac.uk<mailto:sarah.ma@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: TWOMBLI / ECM analysis help
There are a whole bunch of softwares you could use to do this sort of thing (FIJI, Imaris, Python, etc.) - which you choose is really up to you. But we tend to recommend people use freely available, non-commercial software in general (in the interests of reproducibility). 

I'd probably recommend having a go with MorphoLibJ<https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj> and seeing how far you get with that - it's pretty easy to use and well-documented. It's also scriptable<https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj#scripting-morpholibj>, meaning everything can be automated, which might be useful if this is something you might be doing a lot of (even if not, the ability to script things is again useful in the interests of reproducibility). 

Best,

Dave.

From: Sarah Ma <sarah.ma@crick.ac.uk<mailto:sarah.ma@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 21 October 2025 09:21
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: TWOMBLI / ECM analysis help

Great! The sample is one segment of the abdomen of a Drosophila melanogaster pupa, one of them at 14.5 hours after pupariation (hAPF, with "-1" at the end of the file name), the other 17.5hAPF ("-2" at the end of the file name). 

No, they're not the original data, we acquire overnight movies, so I've only taken 2 timepoints from my movie as examples. I also use E-cadherin::mKate2 as a membrane marker, so I've split the channels, hence you only have the GFP channel, which is collagen IV alpha2 chain tagged with GFP near the N-terminal. I acquired it on a Zeiss 880, two tiles of 1024x1024 pixels with 10% overlap and z slices taken every 2um. 

In regards to the projections, this makes sense, so I'd do 3D segmentation? Sorry for the very basic questions, but would you suggest staying in Fiji to 3D segment, or a different software, such as IMARIS? 

Thanks again!

Best wishes,
Sarah

From: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk<mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>> Date: Monday, 20 October 2025 at 15:22
To: Sarah Ma <sarah.ma@crick.ac.uk<mailto:sarah.ma@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: TWOMBLI / ECM analysis help
Yes, thanks, I can access them now.

Ok, can you give me a little more detail on how these images were generated? What exactly is the sample, what is the marker and how was the image acquired? Have these images been manipulated in some way or is this the raw data? The TIF file extension suggests this is not the original data? 

To answer your analysis-specific questions, I'm not sure TWOMBLI is quite what you need here. I think all you need to do is a simple segmentation of all the "objects" in the image and measure their volume. You can express the result as a ratio of  the total volume of all the punctate versus the volume of the "intact" collagen network. 

I would advise against doing projections, as this is going to (a) make it more difficult to detect punctae as they are likely to be obscured by matrix above/below and (b) prevents you from measuring the volume and getting an accurate ratiometric measurement. 

Let me know what you think.

Dave.

Hi Dave,

So sorry for my late response!

Thanks for pointing this out, I was using the older version! I've tried out the new version and I'm no longer encountering an issue I was getting from time to time with the older version. 

As an alternative to TWOMBLI, I was wondering whether there would be a way to determine a puncta vs "line/intact/"fibrillar" ratio? Or is there a way I could get the same results through the metrics given by TWOMBLI? I've attached examples here<https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fo/yjvstt41blfi3qudksuqn/AJfmj1cae-aDJ8X01SZS5M8?rlkey=vg1zan7l18btzh9kjrxebsomc&st=7nsemryb&dl=0> of some images I would want to quantify using their max intensity projections (unless you'd advise a different projection method). For getting a ratio, I'm not too sure thresholding will work as I believe the punctae are just as bright as some of the more intact network. 

I've booked a meeting, so not expecting a reply to the above paragraph if it would be too long! Look forward to talking on the 4th. 

Thanks for your help!

Best wishes,
Sarah

Hi CALM,

We have a paper under revision using some multiplex (6 colour) immunofluorescence images of human spleen 

The reviewers are asking, which is fair enough, how representative our results are across patients/healthy controls/sections or regions per spleen. 

Some findings are and questions:

  *   Plasma cell aggregates close to vasculature

     *   How common is this pattern (per patient, per area)
     *   Compare patients to healthy control spleens in a systematic way 

  *   Clusters of CD19+/- CD138+/- Ig+/- cells

     *   How prevalent are these clusters
     *   What proportion are CD19/CD138/Ig positive/negative

  *   CD11c+ CD19+ cells

     *   Quantify number in patient vs healthy control spleen      *   What fraction are ki-67+

Would you be able to help with quantification of some of these findings, or at the minimum how to display the data in a representative transparent way? 

Thank you very much,

Amalie

From: Ania Mikolajczak <anna.mikolajczak@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 28 October 2025 12:44
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: Mary Green <Mary.Green@crick.ac.uk>
Subject: COMET Lunaphore data

Hi David,

Thanks so much for meeting last week.

We have discussed about our first staining that we have done in EHP with COMET and how we can analyse those images using HORIZON supplier software and compare it to open-source pipelines that are available and you've tested in CALM. 

You should have received the email from Lunaphore explaining how to install the HORIZON software and the licence will last 6 months. I have used it a little bit before, and I am very happy to sit down with you to go through all the features that are available. 

I am uploading two folders into the STP/lm/outputs/Dave folder. One of them is a training human tonsil slide, the other one is human thymus (Bonfanti lab). You will find two files in each folder, one raw and one with background removed (.ome) - that is done on HORIOZON software. 

Both slides were stained with 14 proteins from their antibody kit that they sell/provide for training. On the thymus, we have added additionally 4 antibodies that the lab is interested in. Some antibodies did not work optimally from their kit, as this was just a first test and the concentrations will have to be adjusted, and some clones will need to be changed. The thymus project will be the first one that we will be working on, and it will be purely protein panel with some additional antibodies that we will still need to test. 

Unfortunately, the slide with RNAScope has not worked very well and we are currently awaiting their response to understand why the staining is suboptimal so at the moment we only have protein data. 

We are mainly interested if the features that the HORIZON offers can be easily reproduced using open-source software. And if those could be shared with us so we know what options are available when discussing the projects with researchers. 

We would really appreciate your advice and expertise on this. 

I will do some analysis myself on HORIZON and once you have a bit more time, we could meet and go through it together. 

Thank you

Ania

Hi,

I hope you’re doing well.

I am currently running a Nextflow pipeline relying on Singularity containers for cell phenotyping in tumour samples. The pipeline completes successfully without any apparent errors, and I obtain outputs such as the summary maps and phenotype maps. However, I have noticed that the tissue segmentation and composite generation steps are not executed. The corresponding output folders are empty, and consequently the cell object tables are also empty. 

This workflow previously worked on an earlier dataset, but the issue started when I reran the pipeline after adding new images. 

I have already discussed this with Mihaela who created the pipeline and then with the HPC helpdesk, since the problem appears to be related to Fiji in my nemo environment. We were not able to identify the cause so far. As a next step, I wanted to check with you in case this is a known issue related to Fiji. 

When running the pipeline, I encounter the following error message: 

“File is not in a supported format, a reader plugin is not available, or it was not found.” 

I was wondering whether you have encountered a similar issue before, or if you might have suggestions on what to investigate next. 

Thank you very much for your help.

Kind regards,
Alexandrine
