Gina wants to analyse her DNA FISH images that have been acquired by Donald (Olympus slide scanner) using [ecSeg](https://github.com/UCRajkumar/ecSeg/tree/master "‌") (python script).

- Drop of cells on a slide – most of the image is empty
- Huge images (XY dim: 213622 x 103050 px)
- 3 Z-slices – try capturing most cells in focus
- 3 channels – DAPI + x2 DNA FISH probes (TRITC, FITC)

‌

ecSeg expects TIFs, so she wanted to convert the original .vsi into TIFs, max project them and use the script on them.

‌

After a chat with Donald, the best way forwards might be to detect the nuclei (QuPath/Python) and save a folder in OMERO with individual cells as TIFs. Then these could be fed through the pipeline (would have to adapt it or find other analysis).

- Everything through OMERO Python API?

---

More FISH packages worth considering?

- [RS-FISH](https://github.com/PreibischLab/RS-FISH "‌")
cards.name: DNA FISH slide analysis with ecSeg via OMERO – Gina (Swanton lab)
cards.originalDesc:
Hi Georgia,

It seems you are asking two slightly different, but related questions here: 

  1.  Is there a higher throughput way to download images from OMERO?      *   Yes, there is a Python API which allows you to download images programmatically via scripting: https://omero-guides.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python/docs/index.html   2.  Is there an easy way to convert Olympus files (vsi/ets) to TIFF?      *   Yes, the easiest approach is probably to use Bio-Formats command line tools: https://bio-formats.readthedocs.io/en/latest/users/comlinetools/conversion.html 

Both of the above could be run directly on NEMO.

However, it's probably worth pointing out that a single TIF file representing a high-magnification scan of a whole slide would be quite unwieldy, to say the least. 

Is there a reason why you're using the particular analysis pipeline you referred to? Is this something you've used before? Something that has been recommended to you by someone else? I ask because there may be better alternatives. 

Happy to arrange a chat to discuss further.

Best,

Dave.

From: Georgia Stavrou <georgia.stavrou@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 10 November 2025 09:13
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Obtaining .tif from .vsi images
Importance: High

Good morning,

I am new to OMERO and wanted to cross-check.
I have had some slides scanned using the Olympus scanner and saved as .vsi. I wanted to see what would be the best way of obtaining .tif files from the stitched images? 

I have recently used the export function from OmeroPathviewer but this is quite low throughput as I have approximately 30 slides and loading cells at 40x. Is there a way of either annotating on Pathviewer (marking positions) and then directly exporting these on NEMO? 

My downstream analysis uses ecSeg and requires .tif files (https://github.com/UCRajkumar/ecSeg/tree/master, which is already on NEMO). My understanding is that trying to directly converting .vsi to tiff would require a lot of memory. These are cultured interphase FISH cells, they are scattered throughout the slide, not tissue sections 

Please let me know if you require any additional information. I am unable to come to the Crick for a 1 on 1 but happy to meet through Zoom/Teams. 

Many thanks and best wishes,
Gina

cards.desc:
Alisa contacted us after the Fiji training in Dec 2025. Info below.

I have now uploaded 4 sample movies (6 pupae total) to Nemo under the folder “Alisa\_for\_Sara”.

The images are all live time-lapse images of wound healing in the pupal abdomen of _Drosophila_. I induce wounds of fixed sizes using laser ablations and then image the healing process for about 4 hours (all imaged on the Zeiss 880 NLO).

For the timelapse, the images are 5-10 min apart and the z stacks are 1 um apart. Ecad is imaged with a laser power of 9 and gain of 900, as the signal is relatively weak. For the two later images, two pupae are imaged at once using positions. Additionally, the two earlier timelapses (14/11 and 21/11 are 8-bit images, I have now switched to taking 16-bit images).

For the analysis, I want to be able to segment the images to produce masks of the histoblasts (the small cells surrounded by larval LECs). I then want to use these segments to quantify things like proliferation rates (preferably comparing proliferation near and far from the wound site), orientation of division, cell size, and cell shape (eg. aspect ratio). The main issue I am facing currently is finding an automated way to quantify proliferation rate and direction.

For segmentation, I haven’t had any great success with standard thresholding and “count particles”, but Cellpose seems to work well (I use the command line GUI to calibrate cell size and then use the Cellpose plugin in Fiji for the actual segmentation). This is usually pretty good but it would be great to have a way to manually correct errors made here. The Cellpose mask allows me to quantify cell area and aspect ratio, but I don’t know if it is possible to use this mask to measure things like proliferation. I know some people in the lab use track mate for this, but they have had issues with consistently detecting divisions. Therefore, any suggestions for a better pipeline to quantify divisions would be great.
cards.name: wound healing fly abdomen - Alisa Kinaret (Tapon lab)

From: Jing Zheng <jing.zheng@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: Thursday, April 23, 2026 4:43:30 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Advice on Cellpose-SAM segmentation discrepancies (web vs local) 

Dear Image Analysis Group,

Hope you’re doing well.

This is Jing and I’m a first-year PhD student at Gandhi Lab. I’m currently running into an issue with Cellpose segmentation and was hoping to get your advice. I’m working with max-projected composite PNG images (derived from .czi files), and I’ve noticed a discrepancy between the Cellpose website output and my local terminal GUI runs. 

Specifically:

  *   The exact same input PNG gives very good segmentation on the Cellpose website (Cellpose-SAM). (Please see attached Image_Cellposeweb). 

  *   However, when I run Cellpose locally (also using Cellpose-SAM), the segmentation appears distorted. (Pleasee see attached Image_GUI) 

I’ve tried to control for the main variables:

  *   Using the same input image

  *   Matching the model (Cellpose-SAM)

  *   Adjusting different channel settings

  *   Trying auto and manual diameters

I also confirmed that the issue is not due to preprocessing differences, as I’m using the same PNG exported. Please also find my cellpose version below: 

cellpose version: 4.1.1

platform:       darwin

python version: 3.14.2

torch version:  2.9.1

May I please ask if you may have encountered the same issue or have any suggestions? I’m happy to discuss or meet at any time you are convenient if needed. 

Thanks very much for your time and help in advance!

Best regards,
Jing

Yongjing Zheng
PhD Student
Gandhi Lab, The Francis Crick Institute

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Sent: Monday, April 20, 2026 3:42:42 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Konstantina Angoura <konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures

Best,

From: Konstantina Angoura <konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 16 April 2026 14:20
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures 

Hi Dave,

Thank you for this! I booked a slot but just to mention the overall goal :) . These images are of the samples we did spatial transcriptomics (Xenium) on. We have 6 images in total. Our goal is (a) to get some morphometric measurements of the samples including the longest axis length to then group samples by morphology for the analysis of the sequencing. (b) We also want the coordinates of the longest axis to then position each cell of each sample we sequenced along the long axis for each sample. We know from imaging that cell types in our samples are dependent on their position along the axis, and we want to prove that by our transcriptomics and group cells sequenced by axis position. So, it is quite important that the axis is accurate, if possible. Naomi wanted this macro/method for consistency with our collaborators but if we have a way that works better then we are happy the change it! I hope that makes sense! Happy to explain further when we meet. Thank you! 

Best wishes,
Konstantina

________________________________
From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: Wednesday, April 15, 2026 3:30:02 PM
To: Konstantina Angoura <konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk<mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures 

Hi Konstantina,

I know you’re on leave right now but just sending this while I think of it. 

Thanks for the additional information.

The macro you’re using is almost certainly over-complicating things. I’ve attached a very rough result I’ve obtained using a much simpler method (it’s obviously not perfect, it’s just for the purposes of illustration). 

But in order to determine the best way forward, it would be useful to know what the overall aim is? You mentioned in your initial email that you “want to make a macro to draw the longest axis of each sample and save the length and the position of the line”, but what do you then plan to do with this information? 

You can book a slot to chat here<https://outlook.office.com/bookwithme/user/6a2f989c9bc54d02a22b5797e647f357@crick.ac.uk?anonymous&ismsaljsauthenabled&ep=plink> to discuss further. 

Best,

Dave.

From: Konstantina Angoura <konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk<mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: 13 April 2026 10:54
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Re: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures 

Hi Dave!!

Thank you for the reply! Yes, sorry I was confusing. I am attaching the pdf file with the code from our collaborators and here is the dropbox link to one of the images I want to analyse: https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/ozn9liiltaihxah0wc0fp/multi_block2_slice1.tif?rlkey=lwjk0a31gzf0in8mvz9kehzll&st=cx5lj3c2&dl=0 

Our collaborators work with brightfield images containing 1 sample at a time. The images I want to analyse are dapi staining of multiple samples. So ideally, we would have a code that did preprocessing and then made each sample in the images into a separate mask (manually in Fiji I did the following functions to get the masks Threshold/Median/Fill Holes/Connected Component Labelling) to then apply the elongation index code in the pdf to each mask in each image individually. Each sample looks a bit like a banana, and the goal is the get the length and coordinates of the midline of the long axis of each sample (even if the sample is curved/bent). The methods I used previously were ok with measuring the length of the long axis but the line they drew did not tracethe midline of the structure.. 

I am A/L this week, but would it be useful to quickly meet up next Monday (20/4) so I can explain? Let me know what your availability is! Thank you so much in advance and apologies for explaining this badly 😅. 

Best wishes,

Konstantina

________________________________

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: Monday, April 13, 2026 10:33:16 AM
To: Konstantina Angoura <konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk<mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk>> Cc: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: RE: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures 

Hi Konstantina,

Happy to help if we can.

If you could share some example image data and the script you’re using, we can get back to you with some recommendations. 

I can’t really make out what is in the schematic you’ve shared – is this from a paper/presentation? Could you share the original? 

Best,

Dave.

From: Konstantina Angoura <konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk<mailto:konstantina.angoura@crick.ac.uk>> Sent: Thursday, April 9, 2026 5:43 pm
To: STP (Crick) Advanced Light Microscopy <calm@crick.ac.uk<mailto:calm@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Help with Macro to measure midline of structures

Hello!

I hope this finds you well. I have a macro question 😊 . I have images of some sections with multiple samples in each image. We want to make a macro to draw the longest axis of each sample and save the length and the position of the line. The issue with that some samples are curved, so drawing the longest axis often does not trace the midline we would manually draw. We have a macro from another lab, but it works on different format images, so I was wondering if you can help me adapt it (I am bad with macro code). This macro works with the Max Inscribed Circles plugin, but it is still a bit clunky and I am not sure it is the best way (but it is what it is used in the field so it would be great to have tried it).. Also, super super open to suggestions of better ways of doing this! So yes, let me know if you have some time to discuss this. Thank you! 

[Screenshot 2026-04-09 at 17.38.24.png]

Best wishes,

Konstantina

cards.name: Myotube Quantification

cards.desc:
Coordinate with Dave to discuss using a pixel classifier in QuPath for analysing histological images. Access files on OMERO as needed. Tissue samples include excised lymph node biopsies with Ziehl-Neelsen staining, focus on identifying caseation, fibrosis, Mtb presence, and HIV co-infection markers.

Link for pixel classification guidance: [QuPath Pixel Classification Tutorial](https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/pixel_classification.html)

Agenda:
1. Permissions check on OMERO for file access.
2. Discuss analysis objectives: 
   - Quantify caseation or fibrosis.
   - Identify Mtb presence in relation to lymph node architecture.
   - Quantify p24 immunoperoxidase staining as an HIV marker.
cards.name: Identification and quantification of different regions in lymph node biopsies

cards.desc:
Omar Bouricha is using immunofluorescence to identify cells with genetic mutations that can initiate kidney cancer. The task is to segment tubules in kidney samples and count those with mutant cells for more accurate analysis. 23 images are currently available, with an expectation of 60-70 in the future. Additionally, plans include multiplex IF images with six markers for all kidney tubular types for approximately 20 samples. Consider using models like Tubule-U-Net that work with tubule-like structures from existing literature. Relevant papers:
- https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-27331-3
- https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10479587/pdf/fneph-02-1007002.pdf.
cards.name: Develop Model for Segmentation of Kidney Tubules

cards.desc: Explore options for manual tracking of cells using TrackMate in FIJI. The tool can help assign unique IDs to different cell types and track them across frames. Consider additional capabilities like color-coding IDs and marking cells in all frames, understanding it's a manual process. Reference link for TrackMate: https://imagej.net/plugins/trackmate/tutorials/manual-tracking.
cards.name: Manual Tracking of Cells in FIJI

cards.desc: Coordinate with Dave to meet in person for discussing the handover and potential updates of the 'Mito Synapto to spines' script. Decide on script maintenance and whether to continue using FIJI or switch to Python for greater flexibility. Dave is available tomorrow, Thursday, or next week. Refer to the Github repository for the script: https://github.com/FrancisCrickInstitute/mito-synapto-spine-analysis/blob/main/blob_detector.ijm.
cards.name: Synaptic Spine Detection - Sandra Dubes

Adapt the existing code to wait for the files to fully write, queue them, and run the processing function in parallel. Review the code and example dataset. Code repository: [iss-sync-and-crunch](https://github.com/znamlab/iss-sync-and-crunch/)

cards.desc:
Explore the use of TrackMate to analyze cell proliferation by focusing on cells that remain within the imaging frame throughout the entire time-lapse. Consider creating a "no border ROI" to accurately count these cells and identify the stop of proliferation.

Review the sample movie provided on OneDrive: [Sample Movie](https://thefranciscrickinstitute-my.sharepoint.com/:i:/g/personal/sekara_crick_ac_uk/Eaf55WG6hW1IqTk4tjgSAQIBZyhWJ97NOmhHNdP3fvHi_w?e=N2hbx0).

Evaluate other potential software solutions for the issue presented in the email and discuss these options with Dave.
cards.name: Analyze cell proliferation using TrackMate - Aashika Sekar

cards.desc:
Hi Katherine,

The workstations are under light microscopy.

It might be a good idea to have a chat about what you’re trying to do.

I’d be inclined to think that QuPath should be more suitable for scanned slides than CellProfiler, so if you’re finding QuPath is not doing what it should, there could be an issue that needs addressing. Maybe as a starting point you could share some images with us and we can take a look?

Best,

Dave.

From: Todd Fallesen <[Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk](mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Date: Monday, 19 May 2025 at 16:29
To: Katherine Stafford <[katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk](mailto:katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Cc: Camille Charoy <[camille.charoy@crick.ac.uk](mailto:camille.charoy@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:camille.charoy@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:camille.charoy@crick.ac.uk "‌")\>, David Barry <[David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:David.Barry@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Subject: RE: HPC training for imaging analysis
Hi Katherine,
Thanks for the email! Normally, I’d be happy to give you a hand, but I’m actually going on paternity leave in a couple weeks, so I’m drawing down all the projects I take on right now.

For CellProfiler, we have a virtual image analysis workstation you can book on ppms, which has cellprofiler installed.  It’s pretty cheap to use (a couple £ per hour, but I can’t remember exactly).  That has 256gb of ram and 24gb gpu. It’s like getting a small node from HPC, but still running windows.  I’d recommend starting with that and seeing how it works. As it’s windows based, it’s not really a steep learning curve, and a lot of people have good luck with it.

We are also offering a cellprofiler course on May 30th, which you can sign up for on workday.  It’s being taught by Camille Charoy

Dave Barry was also recommended to you by Martin, so I’ll cc him on here.

Cheers,
todd

From: Katherine Stafford <[katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk](mailto:katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:katherine.stafford@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Sent: 19 May 2025 16:19
To: Todd Fallesen <[Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk](mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk "‌")[http://mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk](http://mailto:Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk "‌")\> Subject: HPC training for imaging analysis

Hi Todd!
I’m trying to analyse some slide scanner images and am struggling with my laptop’s RAM ( & I would say, also struggling generally- very few cells of interest and lots of background fluorescence).

So far I’ve been using QuPath, but I definitely don’t love it. I started trying to make a pipeline on CellProfiler but was also having memory issues and having to subsegment my images just to work on the pipeline.

I’ve been told I can use HPC to overcome my RAM issues and am looking for training. Martin Jones replied to my slack message and said you would be the person to contact. (Also saw your PhD is from Wake Forest on Cricknet!! I grew up in Chapel Hill 😊)

Thanks!
Katherine

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Sent: Friday, January 23, 2026 2:24:28 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Tamara Catrin Klockner <tamara.klockner@crick.ac.uk>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: Please help with image analysis

Hi Tamara,

I always recommend QuPath as a starting point for this kind of data. It’s very well documented and supported and there is even a tutorial specifically for exploring multiplex data: https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/0.6/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html 

You will probably find it can do a lot of what you need to do, but possibly not everything. There are likely to be ways around those limitations, but maybe just begin with detecting cells and attempting to classify them based on your markers. 

We’re obviously happy to support and discuss further if needed – you can book a meeting with me here<https://outlook.office.com/bookwithme/user/6a2f989c9bc54d02a22b5797e647f357@crick.ac.uk?anonymous&ismsaljsauthenabled&ep=plink>, but I recommend having a go with QuPath first. 

Best,

Dave.

From: Tamara Catrin Klockner <tamara.klockner@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 22 January 2026 16:18
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Please help with image analysis

Dear CALM Team,

Together with Ania from EHP we are developing a 6-plex IF that I will use to identify a very rare AT2 intermediate in lung repair (CLDN4+, KRT8+) in normal lung FFPE blocks from TRACERx. Additionally, I would like to quantify HE4 levels in AT2 cells. 

I attached you some screenshots of the IF images. We are measuring: 

  *   SFTPA (green, AT2 cells and secreted to lumen)

  *   CLDN4 (yellow, should be on membrane, but here nonspecific nuclear) 

  *   KRT8 (white)

  *   KRT5 (cyan, basal cells)

  *   HE4 (red)

  *   KI67 (no image attached) and DAPI

Could you help me learn how to analyse these images properly in an automated fashion? I don't know how to do that and there are several challenges: 

  *   I would need to identify AT2 cells based on SFTPA, which is also secreted into the mucus (see attached image). 

  *   I only assume that cytoplasmic/membrane CLDN4 staining is correct, how to remove the unspecific strong nuclear stain from the analysis? 

  *   I likely could use KRT8 for the outline of the cell, but it (normally) won't be present in all AT2 cells. 

  *   CLDN4+ and KRT8+ cells will be very rare.

I would be very grateful if you can find some time to help me. 

Thank you so much!

All the best,
Tamara

Tamara C. Klockner
postdoctoral fellow
Swanton Lab

London NW1 1AT

From: Sara Salgueiro Torres <sara.salgueirotorres@crick.ac.uk> Sent: Thursday, January 15, 2026 3:14:26 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Dani Diaper <dani.diaper@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Help with microscopy images

Hi Dani,

I can absolutely help with that! I can easily get you the stills you need, but I can also help you download FIJI on your laptop and show you how to save the stills you want from the files. Is there a specific file format and quality you need it in? 

Here are some instructions on how to do so, in case you want to give it a go yourself. It might be useful for you and your colleagues for future articles: ——————
Downloading FIJI (locally)
What error/issue are you getting when trying to download FIJI? And are you on a Mac or a Windows machine? You can download FIJI from here<https://fiji.sc/> – I’d recommend selecting the Stable version. 

Once FIJI is running…

  1.
Open the image

  *
On the top menu, navigate to Plugins>Bio-Formats>Bio-Formats Importer   *
Select the TIF file you want to open
  *
A window with a lot of options will pop up. Just make sure that you have the following selected:      *
“Hyperstack” (top left)
     *
Unselect the “Split Channels” option (bottom right)
     *
“Composite” in the Colour Options (bottom left)

[Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.33.57.png]
Once the image opens, you will see two sliders to control the channels (colours) and the timepoints: [Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.35.16.png]

  2. Change colours
If the colours of the channels are not the ones you want for the figures, you can select the channel you want to change with the channel slider (red arrow in the image above). The blue arrow points to the window that you have selected – if it’s in red, it’s the red channel. Then you can change the colours by clicking on the LUT button: [Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.41.05.png]

  3. Convert to a still
There’re a few ways you could do this, but the more straightforward way is to go to Image>Duplicate. Then you can select what frame from Slices tab in the window that pops up: [Screenshot 2026-01-15 at 14.49.38.png]

  4. Flatten the image
You might need to “flatten” the channels, so the colours look right in Photoshop/InDesign: 

  *
Image > Type > RGB Color

  5. Save the new image
Select the newly generated RGB and then navigate to File > Save > Save As and choose the file type you want (maybe PNG?). 

——————

If you’re getting stuck and need them tomorrow afternoon, I can also send you the files. It’s a quick job! Just let me know what format you need them in. 

I will be on long-term leave from next week, so in case there is anything else you need a hand with re images, you can get in touch with our team at bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk . 

I hope that helps!

Best,
Sara

------

Sara Salgueiro Torres (she/her)

Junior Image Analyst

Crick Advanced Light Microscopy

Office: NE316

The Francis Crick Institute

1 Midland Road, London NW1 1AT

Please do not feel obligated to respond outside your work hours.

Hi Sara,

I'm from the Crick comms team and I've got an issue that I was hoping you might be able to help with. I saw on CrickNet that you run the intro to Fiji courses so thought you'd be a good person to approach. 

We've recently launched a Crick magazine and are currently working on the second edition. I've written a feature on a recent publication and the designers would like to use some microscopy images with it. The researcher has supplied some files that we need to get stills from - many years ago when I was in the lab I used to use Fiji, but it's been quite some time and I think I've probably forgotten almost everything. Also, I've been unable to download Fiji onto my Crick laptop and I don't have access to Nemo, so I've fallen at the first hurdle! 

I don't suppose you'd be able to help obtain the stills from the montage files would you? I've no idea if this is something you can help with, so please don't worry if it's not something you can do! I've attached what I've been sent as well as a word doc with screen grabs of the still frames that we're after in case you are able to help. The design team need the images by end of Friday, if this is not possible, please do let me know. 

Thanks,
Dani

Aurora got in touch (Slack, 8th January) asking for help to **segment multilobed nuclei**. Todd has worked with her (+lab) for a few years. He has created a very robust segmentation script in FIJI using StarDist, but it doesn’t work well for multilobed nuclei → try **cellpose**.

→ 1st FIJI macro – segments cells + creates ROIs into output folder for downstream analysis

→ 2nd FIJI macro – uses 1st macro’s ROIs to run downstream analysis (colocalisation, intensity analysis)

… ideally, get Cellpose running in FIJI to just change 1st script (add cellpose rather than stardist). If not, put together a jupyter notebook to run it on Python.

- CALM Nikon spinning disk
- Z-stacks → MIP
- 3 channels

**NOTE – she has very pronounced vignetting in these images!** Chatted to Matt, said probably dirt? Will look into it. Probably will have to either:

- Create a separate script to run a **flatfield correction** on the images (they will not be compared to other image sets)
- OR crop images and to use only cells in centre

‌

**Images:**

- Zeiss AxioImager widefield (CALM)
- Starting to acquire some more on Leica SP8 confocal (CALM)
- Some 2D, some Z-stacks (analysing MIPs sufficient for now)
- 3 channels:
  - 1: DAPI
  - 2: ER (anti-klc3). Some images where Golgi instead → probably a slightly different macro
  - 3: collagen (anti-collagen)

‌

**AIM**

**Track collagen ER export to Golgi**:

- Expect that in resting state, collagen is more spread out in cytoplasm in ER (+some in Golgi)
- After activation (ascorbate), collagen moved from ER to Golgi
- Quantify this **change in location at different timepoints** (different samples fixed at different timepoints)

External Sender: Use caution.

Dear Dave,

Thanks for getting back to me!

Right, since the program was only open to UK students, I thought I could try reaching out via email for some other opportunity. 

Funding is quite an issue, to be honest. Given it's going to be my last summer in undergrad, I was hoping to get an internship somewhere outside India for a much broader exposure to academia (since I plan to do a PhD in Europe after my bachelors). But, I have found over the last couple months that funding is, in fact, a common problem across labs, which does make sense given I am still in college. While I can fund my travels and some day-to-day expenses, I see that accommodation and food over three months gets quite expensive. I have sent out dozens of emails to departments in my university and across programs but found no success, unfortunately. 

Regardless, yes, I'd really appreciate if you could share some contacts for anywhere. And, I will keep an eye on the social media pages, thanks! 

Best,
Tushar

On Fri, Jan 30, 2026 at 12:26 AM Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> wrote: Hi Tushar,

Thanks for getting in touch!

As far as I am aware, the Crick usually only hosts summer students via its “official” summer student program<https://www.crick.ac.uk/careers-and-study/students/summer-students>, the deadline for which is fast approaching for 2026 placements. But unfortunately, this program is only open to students based at UK universities. 

Do you have your own funding for this placement? I’m afraid we do not have any discretionary funding for such things. There are funding schemes to facilitate visits overseas (such as Imaging4All<https://globalbioimaging.org/i4a>), but they are generally only open to full-time researchers. 

But, there may be other opportunities in the future – it’s probably a good idea to follow the Crick on social media<https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-francis-crick-institute/> and keep an eye out for future announcements. 

Sorry I can’t give you better news, but I hope you find a placement that’s to your liking. 

I wonder if you’ve contacted any imaging facilities closer to home? We have some contacts in India who might offer placements? 

Best,

Dave.

From: Tushar Agarwal <tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in<mailto:tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in>> Sent: 27 January 2026 21:21
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Regarding Working with your Over the Summer

External Sender: Use caution.

Dear team,

I came across your page through the description of your upcoming one-day course Machine learning-based image analysis with ilastik. In my university's Biophysics lab, I am involved in the study of the collective dynamics and self-organization properties of active matter in confinement (with C. elegans as a model organism). The research involves a lot of image analysis on large sets of images I collect through fluorescence and bright-field microscopy, and recently I have started picking up machine learning to automate the image analysis process. And, given my interest in the work you do and my desire to build the skills in which you have an expertise, I decided to reach out. 

I am looking for a research opportunity abroad for the summer 2026. I am a 3rd-year undergrad physics major at Ashoka University in India. The training I believe I will get working with you over the summer will tremendously help me with my research goals. I have seen the applications of machine learning and image analysis in many, many fields, including diagnosis of diseases like schistosomiasis and malaria, surveillance and taxonomic classification of mosquitoes and other animals, etc. I have been involved in some of these projects myself. And, given my passion for applications of physics in medicine and public health, I believe it is important for me to pick these skills and ideas up. 

If you are willing to host me over the summer, please do let me know. I'd love to contribute to your work and help wherever I can in return. I am attaching my CV and some very basic image analysis work I had done for my project as reference. Looking forward! 

Best,
Tushar

cards.desc:
Jenna came to our “Intro to FIJI” workshops 13/4/26 and booked a follow-up meeting with me for 7/5/26.

#### Project info:

- _What is the biological question you are trying to answer?_ 
  I want to determine what % of my ESC population has 2 X chromosomes. I would like to set up some kind of pipeline/macro to be able to automate this counting. I want to count signal per nucleus and then I can come to a %X, %XX or %>2X per clone.
- _What measurement(s) would you like to get our of your analysis_ (e.g., _%green nuclei in a tissue sample)? Any reference paper you are following?_
  Number of RNA FISH signals for Huwe1 per nucleus
- _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP?_ 
  Nikon SoRa
- _How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more?_ 
  I usually take 3 images per clone which consist of ~100-150 spotted cells each
- _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?_
  We use either 568, 488 or Cy5 channels. I usually do a probe for Xist RNA: Xist488 and Huwe1 RNA: Huwe1Cy5 but it could be a combination of these.
cards.name: Chromosome counting (ESC) – Jenna Hare

cards.desc:
- We are imaging fixed and stained zebrafish embryonic hearts - either extracted from the fish or in the fish.
  This could lead to images of 4 to 6 tiles and, dependently of the sample, between 150 and 500 Z-stacks (but I used a very small stack of 0.1µM at 40X with SORA = 128X).
  I had 4 channels in total. 
- I'm looking at chromatin organisation into the nuclei. The biological question is then: how are chromatin and histone marks organised into the nuclei? I would like to quantify the global intensity of my signal over my sample, quantify intensity and number of foci I can observe depending on heart regions, signal localisation into the nuclei themselves - more central or accumulating at the nuclear periphery.

Shared images: '\data\STPs\light_microscopy\outputs\Alexia\_June\_2026'.

It's an example of an isolated zebrafish heart at 48hpf. It contains 4 channels (taken from far-red to blue to reduce photobleaching):

- NHS-ester-ATTO-647N: labelling all the proteins
- H3K9me3-Alexa-594: my signal of interest
- Fli1a:H2B-EGFP-Alexa-488: which labels my cell type of interest = endocardium
- DAPI

# Initial enquiry (email):

Dear image analysis group, 

I hope this email finds you well!

I acquired .vsi files from the Olympus Inverted Sora from the microscopy
facility. However, I was struggling to open it on my FIJI through the VPN
because of their sizes (from 30GB to roughly 100GB for one single image). I
talked to the microscopy team, and they suggested me to contact you. They
mentioned the possibility to reach via VPN a FIJI installed on the computer
containing the data (data located on [http:////data2.thecrick.org/lab-vermotj](http:////data2.thecrick.org/lab-vermotj "‌")).

In addition, we are using a homemade interactive tool in the lab for several
quantifications and segmentations that runs locally with python. Do you think
there will be any way to 'download' the data in order to use our tool? The
microscopy team mentioned OMERO, if I remember well. 

Many thanks in advance for your help, 

Kind regards, 
Alexia PIGEOT

- Zeiss Axio Scan slide scanner (EHP)
- Faint signal from bacteria + fluorescence coming from erythocytes within tissue, which prevents him from automating his analysis
- He’s tried sunning filters, image calculation, background subtraction in FIJI. No success so far

SEE ATTACHED NOTES (docx) FOR MORE INFO

- essentially came to the conclusion he needs to improve his image acquisition before he can do automated and robust analysis
cards.name: Mtb bacterial quantification in lung tissue slices – Baptise (Gutierrez lab)

cards.desc: David Barry has requested further information regarding the data acquisition process of the 32-bit files to understand if they are processed or deconvolved. He is inquiring about the experimental setup, specifically the number of conditions, datasets per condition, and the hypothesis being tested. Furthermore, reasons for acquiring 3D stacks instead of 2D for cells on a coverslip were questioned, as well as the definition of 'co-localized' foci. These aspects will influence the approach to automating the analysis. Current suggestions include using CellProfiler for 2D analysis and software like napari, FIJI with MorphoLibJ, or GIANI for 3D analysis. Links: [GIANI for 3D Analysis](https://journals.biologists.com/jcs/article/135/10/jcs259511/275435/GIANI-open-source-software-for-automated-analysis), [MorphoLibJ](https://imagej.net/plugins/morpholibj). Decide on the preferred approach and schedule a meeting in the new year to discuss further. Sample image link: [Google Drive Image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Tr-ECHd97TP7nGpiHF2evZyD2c6nP4Su/view?usp=drive_link)

cards.desc:
Hi,


Hope this finds you well!
I'm Magnus Jhaveri, a PhD student in the Serio lab. I've collected some 2D
timelapse footage of a photoconvertible fluorophore in growing axons and am
hoping to segment the axons as they grow using SNT in FIJI. The aim would be to
compare the ratio of photoconverted/unphotoconverted populations within the
segmented timelapse of growing axons. I tried briefly in ilastik, but feel SNT
may offer more consistent neurite segmentation across frames, particularly as my
signal-to-noise is not great.


The struggle I'm having is refining my paths on SNT, as axon width varies along
the length. Would it be possible to book a slot with someone later this week to
walkthrough SNT with some of my data as an example?


All the best,
Magnus

cards.desc:
Gisela: I am looking to automate the characterization of extracellular mitochondria for properties such as 1. percentage of colocalization with vesicle markers (CD9, integrin b1) and 2. the size of such vesicles. Immunofluorescent images are acquired from fixed primary mouse astrocytes and astrocytes + neurons co-culture, on iSiM at 150x.

---

I'm looking at extracellular mitochondria released by astrocytes, which seem to be predominantly enclosed within extracellular vesicles (EVs). The current problem with visualizing EVs is the lack of a universal marker, and in my pilot experiments CD9 and integrin beta-1 (ITGB1), compared to other common EV markers, seem to co-localize with mitochondria more readily. Therefore I am looking to quantify the extent of these two EV markers representing extracellular mitochondria (for other future experiments that involve these surface markers). 

I have uploaded a few examples of images that I have on hand currently, with the ones labeled "CC" being astrocyte + neuron co-culture and without "CC" being astrocyte-only mono-culture. Astrocytes are transduced with Halotag\_P2A\_mito-StayGold while neurons are transduced with mTagBFP2.

Images were acquired in 4 channels:

far red (cyan) — Halotag (astrocyte cell fill);

red — EV markers (CD9/ITGB1 by immunostaining);

green — mito-StayGold (astrocyte mitochondria)

blue — DAPI in mono-culture; mTagBFP2 (neuron cell fill) in co-culture

Initially I was also planning to distinguish non-enclosed mitochondria and EV-enclosed mitochondria using Halotag (because EVs tend to carry cytoplasmic content) but the Halotag protein expression is suboptimal and pretty much unusable in current state (my previous viral construct with good cytoplasmic expression unfortunately had non-specific expression for mitochondrial tag). Therefore at this stage I am just aiming to quantify the extend of co-localization of EV marker and extracellular mitochondria in general. As for the co-localization, the EV markers and mitochondria marker usually do not overlap completely (as seen in the examples), so the criteria would be the two being in proximity (in contact / partial overlapping). I also have EV marker secondary-antibody-only sample as control so these can be used for thresholding.

cards.desc:
Summarise and explore the shared data related to Xenium image alignment, focusing on raw data for registration/alignment.

- The data comprises NF control and d30 Xenium data, along with QuPath analysis and alignment matrices. NF control images remain unaligned.
- The dataset is approximately 17GB and is organised in six directories.
- The meeting is tentatively scheduled for 2pm on 12th December 2025.
- Consider long-term objectives for what the analysis aims to reveal and anticipate future data requirements.

Ensure to follow up on issues with permissions for certain folders. Source: email from Cameron Burnett and David Barry, dated 14th November 2025.
cards.name: Xenium image alignment overview
cards.originalDesc:
Thanks Cam,

I seem to be able to access most of it - I'm getting permission errors on one of the folders, but I'll contact HPC about that. Doesn't seem like the data is huge (about 17GB in total?), so should be manageable. 

I'm pretty swamped over the next few weeks, but I should be able to find some time to test something prior to us meeting next month. 

In the meantime, if you could provide a summary of what all the data you've shared represents (I'm seeing six directories in the parent folder with multiple files and subdirectories in each), that would be helpful. In particular, to avoid any confusion, if you point me specifically toward the raw data to be registered/aligned, that would be great. 

It would also be really useful to know what the long-term plan is here? Ultimately, what is it that you want the analysis to show? And how much more of this data do you expect to have? Is this a one-off, or are other people in your lab doing similar work? 

Best,

Dave.

From: Cameron Burnett <cameron.burnett@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 14 November 2025 14:28
To: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: Amy Strange <amy.strange@crick.ac.uk>; Cameron Shand <cameron.shand@crick.ac.uk>; Ania Mikolajczak <anna.mikolajczak@crick.ac.uk>; Emma Connick <emma.connick@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Re: Xenium image alignment advice

Hi Dave,

I have copied over NF control and d30 Xenium data plus all of my QuPath analysis and alignment matrices. NF control has not yet been aligned to serial sections.

I have tentatively requested a meeting at 2pm on the 12th December. 

Best wishes,
Cam

Hi Dave,

It's good to hear from you. It's just transcript reads in the Xenium panel, so I expect it will not be easy. It is the 5000plex panel with 100 custom probes. They also did the standard morphology panel for image alignment and cell segmentation. 

I have actually managed to align most of the images manually in Xenium Explorer using the built in linear alignment algorithm. I cropped IF images to one spleen section and aligned images by painstakingly finding shared nuclei across serial sections of the same tissue. Each Xenium Image contains 3 spleen sections, but the software only allows me to overlay 2 additional IF images so I had to create a cropped Xenium file and overlay the 3rd IF image on that, which is not ideal. 

Other alignment methods that allow me to align with the whole IF image would be better. 

Best wishes,
Cam

Cameron Burnett

Postdoctoral Fellow

Immunity and Cancer Laboratory

The Francis Crick Institute

1 Midland Road

London NW1 1AT

United Kingdom

Hi Cameron,

Genomics STP recently ran Xenium on some of my mouse spleens, which seems to have worked well. EHP then did multiplex IF on post-xenium slides and with a serial section staining for: DAPI, GFP, mCherry, CD19, Bcl6 and CD138. The serial section staining worked well, the post-xenium slide had some issues: the most important signal in the image that I need to overlay to the Xenium data is the mCherry staining, however this was weaker post-xenium yet it still looked good in the serial section. Would it be possible to align the serial section image to the Xenium image while using the post-xenium image as a guide so that I can get a better approximation of the mCherry signal? Could we discuss this?

Many thanks,
Cam

cards.desc:
Discussion between Sarah Ma and David Barry about ECM degradation analysis using TWOMBLI.

- Sarah is exploring ECM degradation in Drosophila metamorphosis and using TWOMBLI for data analysis.
- She has issues with an older version of a tool and looks for alternative methods for analysis.
- The Dropbox link was initially not working; Sarah now uses a new version of TWOMBLI without issues.
- Sarah seeks advice on obtaining a puncta vs. "line/intact/fibrillar" ratio.
- A meeting is scheduled for the 4th for further discussion.

**Resources:**
- Example images for analysis: [Dropbox link](https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fo/yjvstt41blfi3qudksuqn/AJfmj1cae-aDJ8X01SZS5M8?rlkey=vg1zan7l18btzh9kjrxebsomc&st=7nsemryb&dl=0)

cards.desc: Request form Foteini (Hill lab) to get helped setting up the acquisition and analysis for quantify nuclear relocation of protein during mitosis. At the moment it was an afternoon conversation, where we explored different ways to do it in FIJI and CellProfiler. No project should be required as user happy to do the analysis per se. Just leaving the card here for a bit to signpost the conversation.

cards.desc:
Neuroblast 3D segmentation - Elisabeth Kamper (Gould Lab)

Have explored with Elisabeth how to segment neuroblast in 3D with napari. We started working on similar data sets with FIJI macro some years ago, before I develop my plugin fit for the purpose. Manage to install napari on user computer (success) and she seems quite happy to try the plugin, which seems to work for her data: [Frontiers | ZELDA: A 3D Image Segmentation and Parent-Child Relation Plugin for Microscopy Image Analysis in napari](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2021.796117/full "‌")

Waiting for user feedback
cards.name: Neuroblast 3D segmentation - Elisabeth Kamper (Gould Lab)

cards.desc:
Automate the counting of total and GFP-positive cells (His_GFP) using Cellpose as segmentation tool, as her images are brightfield and cells have irregular shapes.

### **28th June Update (SARA):**

- Put together a quick analysis using FIJI and sent her some of the results to see what she thinks. + scheduling a meeting 2nd half July to discuss more detail on her images and building a macro to automate it
- Preliminary pipeline:
  - FIJI – cellpose on BF (cells) > morpholibj to extract mean GFP int
  - Jupyter – show hist GFP mean int distribution, run thresholding algorithm, calculate %GFP positive cells
- Tried CellProfiler to extract GFP intensities but much more of a hassle considering it can be _easily?_ done all under FIJI and then post-process results in Python
cards.name: Emma Kinloch's GFP cell counting, cellpose (Vousden Lab)

cards.name: UK-BIAS
cards.originalDesc:
From: UK Euro-BioImaging discussion list <UK-EUROBIOIMAGING-PROJECT@JISCMAIL.AC.UK> On Behalf Of Georgina Fletcher Sent: 16 October 2025 13:43
To: UK-EUROBIOIMAGING-PROJECT@JISCMAIL.AC.UK
Subject: Join UK-BIAS: Help Shape the UK BioImage Analysis Network – Apply by Nov 5th 

External Sender: Use caution.

***Circulated on behalf of David Barry (Crick), Stephen Cross (Bristol), Marie Held (Liverpool), Martin Jones (Crick), Stefania Marcotti (Crick), Laura Murphy (Edinburgh), Daniel Rolfe (CLF Octopus)*** 

________________________________

Dear Colleagues,

We're excited to announce the first steps towards the creation of the UK BioImage Analysis Network (UK-BIAS) — a new national initiative to connect image analysts across the UK, expand access to bioimage analysis expertise, and strengthen our research community. 

Inspired by successful models like F-BIAS<https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1013058> in France, we hope a UK-BIAS would support the UK Node of Euro-BioImaging's Biological Image Data Analysis services while building a collaborative network that benefits everyone involved. 

Why get involved?

  *   For analysts: Collaborate with peers, share skills, reduce professional isolation, and increase your visibility   *   For institutions: Generate external revenue opportunities and enhance your reputation   *   For researchers: Broaden access to critical bioimage analysis support nationwide 

We need your input and involvement to make this happen.

If you're an image analyst, facility manager, or researcher interested in shaping this network, please complete our Expression of Interest form by Wednesday, November 5th: 

→ Complete the form here ←<https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdgCUDt9nq5bRPK5fhK5xIP1-OLBAbnmRc9ONUsVWsdW0d9kg/viewform> 

Options include either applying to be part of the UK Node of Euro-BioImaging or being an “affiliate member” of the group. 

All applicants will be informed of the outcome by Friday, November 14th. 

Please share with your colleagues too so they don't miss this opportunity to be part of something that could transform bioimage analysis support across the UK. Kind regards,
Georgina

Georgina Fletcher
BioImagingUK Project Officer and UK Node Manager for Euro-BioImaging  [BioImagingUK logo]

cards.desc:
**Intro to Python for IA (Nov '25) course follow-up**

- Cryo sections of zebrafish heart tissue
- Imaged with **Andor (**:warning: **) BC43** at 20X
  - Small Z-stack → MIP (not intetersted in 3D)
  - **Saved as .ims → converts to TIFs in FIJI to do analysis**
- 4 channels
  - DAPI
  - Green – epicardial layer marker (“outside” cells)
  - White – protein of interest, WT1
- All images have been acquired for this specific project already, but will be acquiring other datasets in the future.

‌

**Main questions:**

- _How many DAPI objects are double positive (both green and white)?_
- _How many cells are in the “compact layer” and how many in the “trabecular layer” of the tissue?_

‌

**Current pipeline:**

- CellProfiler – seems much more complicated than needed
- IQ is suboptimal (very low signal in all channels, inc DAPI), **and** :warning: **she’s clipped her images to “see better signal” using the Andor software before analysing her images!!!** :warning:
cards.name: Cell counting in zebrafish heart tissue sections – Mariana Valenzuela

cards.desc:
Came to the [Helpdesk](https://trello.com/c/Sdmg5FvG "‌") on 13th October 2025 asking for some help on tackling her IA. Currently just has some pilot images – testing IA before acquiring more.

- Using LTTL in the radiation room downstairs
- 2D images of cells tracked over tiime (every 10min). Length varies, currently doing overnight
- Only channel is a DNA damage marker (foci) – radioligand targeting CAF-specific protein (which have YFP-53BP1 reporter).
  - By looking at images, seems like it’s good enough to get nuclear segmentation from it too

‌

She’s tried Trackmate to track individual spots… Suggested she segments the nuclei first, and then track number of foci in the labelled nuclei over time:

- Start by exporting intensity measurements before going into spot counting

‌

Asked for her to share some example images with me (Sara) so I can test some options for her (14th Oct 2025).
cards.name: DNA damage (spot) tracking over time – Alexandrine Carminati

cards.desc: Discuss using a multiplex system for characterizing lymphocytes in postmortem tissue and plan data analysis for Omero. Explore MACSima system for potential optimization. Images uploaded at: //nemo/lab/schmackk/data/STPs/light_microscopy/outputs/feldmah/. Coordinate with Kenneth Ho for OMERO setup and Sara Salgueiro Torres for analysis support. Relevant details: Laminin and CD45 for HPO stains, dual immunofluorescence for B cell markers, and triple immunofluorescence for T cell markers.
cards.name: Lymphocyte Counting Project – Harriet

cards.desc: Resume the vasculature project by testing the dye again and preparing more samples for the BrainSaw if further data is needed. Coordinate with Dan Gunton for acquisition support as initial experiments by Andres and Maxwell weren't successfully repeated. Investigate if there were changes to setting up the BrainSaw.
cards.name: Vasculature Project: Dye Testing and BrainSaw Setup

cards.desc:
Met Sofia (Pachnis Lab) end of 2024 to discuss Glia Quantification Inside Ganglia Project, and she brought up a 2nd project = quantifying RNascope signal inside vs outside ganglia in murine Enteric Nervous System (something the lab does extensively).

Todd has worked on this extensively in the past and published a paper ([https://www.nature.com/articles/s41596-022-00697-4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41596-022-00697-4 "smartCard-inline") )
cards.name: RNAscope Quantification in Ganglia Project (Sofia)

cards.desc: Coordinate with David Barry to discuss the potential project involving image analysis on FFPE mouse lung tissue. A specific challenge is detecting structures (tumour, TLS, non-tumour) using DAPI staining and existing software has multiple issues. Consider alternative solutions such as QuPath or another image analysis platform. Evaluate potential collaboration opportunities with Image Analysis working group.
cards.name: TLS QuPath analysis - Alicia Alonso De la Vega

cards.desc:
Coordinate with Sara to begin analyzing the cell images using CellProfiler. Alicja will shadow.

Try repurposing Todd’s 2019 FIJI macro first.

**AIMS**:

- Count how many cells have 1 or 2 GFP dots
- In cells with 2 spots, measure distances between them

Kim barely has any experience with FIJI, none with CellProfiler. Will teach her how to use the pipeline(s)
cards.name: GFP spot counting in yeast cells – Kim (Uhlmann lab)

cards.desc:
Came to the Image Analysis Helpdesk on 3/2/25 looking for some help to analyse whole slide images of brain tissue. Just started using QuPath and was struggling to get anything to work. Donald and I (Sara) got QuPath set up for her on NEMO (as it was very slow and crashing locally).

Gave her an overview on QuPath and how to quantify and classify her cells. It was a bit too much information, so she got in touch with me (Sara) via Slack a few times with small queries.

Now needs a bit more help to analyse her images. She is keen to learn how to do it herself, so I met up with her (12/3/25) and I’m having a look at her images to then teach her how to automate her image analysis.

MAIN AIMS:

In her manually annotated ROI (a specific region of the brain):

1. Count number of microglia
2. Quantify area covered by the microglia – DONE
3. Quantify cell body area (= whole cell – branches) → so probably DAPI of microglia cells is enough
cards.name: Microglia Quantification – Micaela S (Tybulewicz lab)

cards.desc: Arrange a meeting to discuss the issues with MACSima data analysis involving big differences in background fluorescence intensity in the PE channel. Specific focus on classifying cells as positive or negative for a marker, and potential normalization based on the local background average. Review the attached screenshot for further context.
cards.name: Discuss MACSima data analysis issues

cards.desc: Assist Gina with analysing Zebrafish retina timelapse data using Imaris/Fiji. Prioritize scheduling a session over the next week to ensure she receives the needed guidance.
cards.name: Support with Zebrafish retina timelapse analysis

cards.desc:
Consider the feedback from JCB's reviewers for manuscript #202503009. Address the Major Concerns of Reviewer #1 by restructuring the analysis to focus on sample means to counter pseudoreplication and developing a more adaptable metric for data subsampling. Address the Major Concerns of Reviewer #2 by debugging the Binder integration and refining Figure 1B. Explore options for revising the manuscript: 1) Rewrite using the existing dataset, 2) Use a new publicly available dataset with more replicates [dataset link](https://www.nature.com/articles/sdata201718), 3) Combine analyses of both datasets to strengthen arguments.

Links: 
- Existing source dataset: [eLife Sciences](https://elifesciences.org/articles/79283)

cards.desc:
Got in touch with me first time 6th March after my Intro to IA seminar talk asking about IA courses available. Asking about her needs, I gave her some advice on where to start (she has no IA experience) and offered to meet up with her to give her a hand.

- Main question – how many SOX17+ve (nuclear marker) cells are there in the image?
- Just has a pilot so far
- Fluorescent images taken with CALM’s Observer with 3 channels:
  - DAPI
  - SOX17 (green)
  - OCT4 (red) – control, not expecting to see a signal, definitely not from SOX17 positive cells

#### **16th April, 1st face-to-face meeting**

- She has tried running some analysis and making some rudimentary FIJI macros
- She needs to optimise her images, especially SOX17. DAPI is okay, but SOX17 has a very narrow dynamic range and max intensity is very low (hence quite a lot of background and noisy images). Suggested trying to do a quick optimisation test for her antibodies/staining protocol overall and chatting to someone in CALM that can help her acquire the images (at least a few min to help her spot what she needs to look out for)
- Showed her my pilot tests on those noisy images (hard to distinguish what’s +ve from -ve)
  - Segmented cells with both Stardist and Cellpose in FIJI
  - Measured mean intensity of SOX17 channel with MorphoLibJ
  - Processed results in a little jupyter script (sox17-classification-test.ipynb) → shared with her for reference
    - Plots all mean intensities in a histograms and then runs 3 different thresholding algos (Otsu, Median, Triangle) to determine what’s a positive object in a more unbiased way
- She’s going to try improve her SOX17 signal and implement the analysis steps I taught her on FIJI (potentially putting them into a script). Once she has better images, I’ll help her some more with the analysis and trying to set up a macro to batch analyse her images
cards.name: Isabel Levy (Rouhani lab) – SOX17+ counting

Laura came to a [helpdesk](https://trello.com/c/xCmdKKjm "‌") on 29th September asking for help quanitfying the morphology of the ER structures in her images:

- Fixed cells
- Z-stacks (~5 slices per stack)
- Stained for:
  - DAPI (ch1)
  - F-actin (ch2, phalloidin)
  - YAP (ch3)
  - ER (ch4)
- Example data in NEMO LM inputs (_garcia-manyess>downiel_)

She has managed to automatically segment the ER, but needs help getting the following ER measurements:

- Volume
- Continuity
- Tubularity
- Branching/Number junctions

(not sure if resolution is good enough to measure all of them)

‌

Laura’s suggested papers (ER signal measured along lines drawn from nucleus to edge of cell):

- English, A.R. and Voeltz, G.K. (2013) “Rab10 GTPase regulates ER dynamics and morphology,” _Nature Cell Biology_, 15(2), pp. 169–178. Available at: [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncb2647](https://doi.org/10.1038/ncb2647 "smartCard-inline") .
- Chen, Z. _et al._ (2025) “External strain on the plasma membrane is relayed to the endoplasmic reticulum by membrane contact sites and alters cellular energetics,” _Science Advances_, 11(26), p. eads6132. Available at: [https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.ads6132](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.ads6132 "smartCard-inline")

cards.name: Spatial Transcriptomics Project Advice - SP25361

cards.desc:
Review the QuPath tutorial for multiplex data analysis to start exploring image analysis for identifying and quantifying AT2 cells and their markers.

Key references:
- QuPath multiplex analysis tutorial: https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/0.6/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html
- Consider meeting with Dave for further guidance: [Booking link](https://outlook.office.com/bookwithme/user/6a2f989c9bc54d02a22b5797e647f357@crick.ac.uk?anonymous&ismsaljsauthenabled&ep=plink)

Challenges to address:
- Identify AT2 cells with SFTPA while excluding its secretion.
- Remove non-specific nuclear CLDN4 stain.
- Utilize KRT8 for outlining cells, considering its limited presence.
- Detect rare CLDN4+ and KRT8+ cell markers.
cards.name: Explore image analysis for AT2 cells using QuPath
cards.originalDesc:
Hi Tamara,

I always recommend QuPath as a starting point for this kind of data. It's very well documented and supported and there is even a tutorial specifically for exploring multiplex data: https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/0.6/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html 

You will probably find it can do a lot of what you need to do, but possibly not everything. There are likely to be ways around those limitations, but maybe just begin with detecting cells and attempting to classify them based on your markers. 

We're obviously happy to support and discuss further if needed - you can book a meeting with me here<https://outlook.office.com/bookwithme/user/6a2f989c9bc54d02a22b5797e647f357@crick.ac.uk?anonymous&ismsaljsauthenabled&ep=plink>, but I recommend having a go with QuPath first. 

Best,

Dave.

From: Tamara Catrin Klockner <tamara.klockner@crick.ac.uk>
Sent: 22 January 2026 16:18
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Please help with image analysis

Dear CALM Team,

Together with Ania from EHP we are developing a 6-plex IF that I will use to identify a very rare AT2 intermediate in lung repair (CLDN4+, KRT8+) in normal lung FFPE blocks from TRACERx. Additionally, I would like to quantify HE4 levels in AT2 cells. 

I attached you some screenshots of the IF images. We are measuring: 

  *   SFTPA (green, AT2 cells and secreted to lumen)

  *   CLDN4 (yellow, should be on membrane, but here nonspecific nuclear) 

  *   KRT8 (white)

  *   KRT5 (cyan, basal cells)

  *   HE4 (red)

  *   KI67 (no image attached) and DAPI

Could you help me learn how to analyse these images properly in an automated fashion? I don't know how to do that and there are several challenges: 

  *   I would need to identify AT2 cells based on SFTPA, which is also secreted into the mucus (see attached image). 

  *   I only assume that cytoplasmic/membrane CLDN4 staining is correct, how to remove the unspecific strong nuclear stain from the analysis? 

  *   I likely could use KRT8 for the outline of the cell, but it (normally) won't be present in all AT2 cells. 

  *   CLDN4+ and KRT8+ cells will be very rare.

I would be very grateful if you can find some time to help me. 

Thank you so much!

All the best,
Tamara

Tamara C. Klockner
postdoctoral fellow
Swanton Lab

The Francis Crick Institute
SW307
1 Midland Road
London NW1 1AT

On Fri, Jan 30, 2026 at 12:50 AM Tushar Agarwal <tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in<mailto:tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in>> wrote: Dear Dave,

Thanks for getting back to me!

Right, since the program was only open to UK students, I thought I could try reaching out via email for some other opportunity. 

Funding is quite an issue, to be honest. Given it's going to be my last summer in undergrad, I was hoping to get an internship somewhere outside India for a much broader exposure to academia (since I plan to do a PhD in Europe after my bachelors). But, I have found over the last couple months that funding is, in fact, a common problem across labs, which does make sense given I am still in college. While I can fund my travels and some day-to-day expenses, I see that accommodation and food over three months gets quite expensive. I have sent out dozens of emails to departments in my university and across programs but found no success, unfortunately. 

Regardless, yes, I'd really appreciate if you could share some contacts for anywhere. And, I will keep an eye on the social media pages, thanks! 

Best,
Tushar

On Fri, Jan 30, 2026 at 12:26 AM Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> wrote: Hi Tushar,

Thanks for getting in touch!

As far as I am aware, the Crick usually only hosts summer students via its “official” summer student program<https://www.crick.ac.uk/careers-and-study/students/summer-students>, the deadline for which is fast approaching for 2026 placements. But unfortunately, this program is only open to students based at UK universities. 

Do you have your own funding for this placement? I’m afraid we do not have any discretionary funding for such things. There are funding schemes to facilitate visits overseas (such as Imaging4All<https://globalbioimaging.org/i4a>), but they are generally only open to full-time researchers. 

But, there may be other opportunities in the future – it’s probably a good idea to follow the Crick on social media<https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-francis-crick-institute/> and keep an eye out for future announcements. 

Sorry I can’t give you better news, but I hope you find a placement that’s to your liking. 

I wonder if you’ve contacted any imaging facilities closer to home? We have some contacts in India who might offer placements? 

Best,

Dave.

From: Tushar Agarwal <tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in<mailto:tushar.agarwal_ug2023@ashoka.edu.in>> Sent: 27 January 2026 21:21
To: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk<mailto:bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk>> Subject: Regarding Working with your Over the Summer

External Sender: Use caution.

Dear team,

I came across your page through the description of your upcoming one-day course Machine learning-based image analysis with ilastik. In my university's Biophysics lab, I am involved in the study of the collective dynamics and self-organization properties of active matter in confinement (with C. elegans as a model organism). The research involves a lot of image analysis on large sets of images I collect through fluorescence and bright-field microscopy, and recently I have started picking up machine learning to automate the image analysis process. And, given my interest in the work you do and my desire to build the skills in which you have an expertise, I decided to reach out. 

I am looking for a research opportunity abroad for the summer 2026. I am a 3rd-year undergrad physics major at Ashoka University in India. The training I believe I will get working with you over the summer will tremendously help me with my research goals. I have seen the applications of machine learning and image analysis in many, many fields, including diagnosis of diseases like schistosomiasis and malaria, surveillance and taxonomic classification of mosquitoes and other animals, etc. I have been involved in some of these projects myself. And, given my passion for applications of physics in medicine and public health, I believe it is important for me to pick these skills and ideas up. 

If you are willing to host me over the summer, please do let me know. I'd love to contribute to your work and help wherever I can in return. I am attaching my CV and some very basic image analysis work I had done for my project as reference. Looking forward! 

Best,
Tushar

cards.desc:
Micheal-Bogdan Margineanu contacted Dave upon leaving the Crick to put him in touch with Chris Tsantoulas, principal research scientist, and Konstantina Nikolatou from the Elosegui-Artola lab, who are interested to use his pipeline for segmentation and calcium transients analysis (Fiji TrackMate+CellPose followed by MATLAB). Micheal had received support from Todd and Chris Hadjigeorgiou fore NEMO/OnDemand setup previously.

Micheal reckons Chris and Konstantina will need need help to set up the conda environment for cellpose, and then link it to TrackMate in Fiji. He will have access in the next year to Nemo and OnDemand as he will maintain an Affiliated Researcher status, but they will need to have their own installation to perform this independently as well.

Sara and Stefania to meet with Konstantina and Chris on 08/10 to get more info/ help them to setup

---

- [**Paper**](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-08575-7 "‌") that describes the methods

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Sent: Tuesday, March 10, 2026 2:19:05 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Maryam Pearson <maryam.pearson@crick.ac.uk>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: QuPath help

Hi Maryam,

It depends on what you mean by “double positive”, but I suspect combining classifiers in QuPath will probably achieve your goal. 

If you have a look at the documentation under “Multiplexed analysis”<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html>, there’s a section detailing how to first detect and measure cells<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html#detect-measure-cells> (which I suspect you’re already somewhat familiar with), followed by instructions on how to create “classifiers” for different markers<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html#create-a-classifier-for-each-marker>. You can then combine these classifiers<https://qupath.readthedocs.io/en/stable/docs/tutorials/multiplex_analysis.html#combine-the-classifiers> for different markers, such that you can classify cells according to expression levels in different channels. 

Let me know if any of this does not make sense.

Best,

Dave.

cards.desc:
1. _What is the biological question you are trying to answer? --_ My goal is to characterise the liver in the tumour bearing context and to assess if there are changes in some specific markers such as CD34 which stains for cells in proliferative state. My observation is that I see that CD34 stains cells around the blood vessels in the liver from Naive mice while in the tumour-bearing mice, I see that in addition to staining cells around the liver, there are also CD34 cells now appearing in the liver tissue outside of the blood vessels
2. _What measurement(s) would you like to get out of your analysis?-- S_o I want to quantify the number of CD34+ stains blood vessels in both naive and tumour bearing context as well as quantify CD34+ cells outside the blood vessels 
3. _With what microscope did you acquire your images? Was it with someone from the CALM STP_-- I used the Axio Scan EVOS at the EHP to scan the images
4. How many images do you have? Are you planning to acquire more? --I currently have about 30 images that I aim to quantify, and I will be acquiring more
5. _What channel(s) do you have in the shared images? Conditions/Controls?— I have two channels in the image I shared (555 and 647), and I have two conditions - Liver from Naive and Tumour bearing (TB)_
cards.name: CD34 localisation to blood vessels in liver tissue – Quadri Omotesho

dissertation focused on machine learning for biological image segmentation,

cards.desc:
Hi Noel,


This is exactly how Bioformats works.


Each multi-dimensional acquisition is a single dataset and Bioformats reads the
metadata and knows how many timepoints, channels and positions there are.
Bioformats is unfortunately slow... They updated ImageJ a while ago and it
defaults to Bioformats for all tiff files now (it didn't use to, and is also OS
dependent), which can be quite annoying.


I've cc'd the image analysis team - they may know if there is a way to use the
tiff reader rather than Bioformats. However, you may be better off working out
how to open the files with bioformats macro extensions if you have an analysis
script (minimise the number of times it reads the metadata) or maybe consider
importing the data into OMERO to simplify opening them.


As an aside, on the new systems there is a 'multi-MDA' acquisition mode that
will allow you to separate the wells from each other (i.e. acquire them as
individual datasets), this may be useful for you.


Best,
Matt




--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

From: Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Sent: Friday, April 24, 2026 4:47:35 PM (UTC+00:00) Monrovia, Reykjavik To: Isabel Levy <isabel.levy@crick.ac.uk>; Crick Image Analysis Group <bioimage-analysis@crick.ac.uk> Subject: RE: Visiopharm to quantify Liver Steatosis

Hi Isabel,

As mentioned previously, I believe at least one group in the building has trialled Visiopharm, but I don’t believe anyone has purchased a license. 

We do sometimes arrange demos of commercial software, but it does take a bit of effort and organisation, so we tend not to do so unless there’s a clear demand for a particular product. More generally, we tend not to support a whole lot of commercial softwares because (a) they tend to be expensive and (b) it’s very rare that they perform any better than freely-available, open source alternatives, although there are some exceptions. 

But if there’s something unique this software can do that cannot be done with existing methods, we can investigate the feasibility of a demo. 

Best,

Dave.

Happy to set up a meeting (perhaps in the new year?) to discuss further. 

Dave.

I’m thinking that CALM would be better placed than BABS to help with this, at least the initial steps, and it might end up being a nice collaboration between us. 

We’re due to meet with the scientist to discuss the project on Monday morning next week (10:30 via zoom).  Would one of your team be available to come in on the call do you think?  - apologies for the late notice.  If no one is available so soon, then perhaps a subsequent meeting in the new year would be a good fit.  It would be great to have your input. 

Best wishes,
Richard.

cards.name: 2026-08-28 Ilastik

cards.desc:
_Recent advancements in microscopy technology have revolutionised our ability to routinely acquire complex, multi-dimensional image datasets. However, gaps remain in the effective analysis of this data, primarily due to frequent reliance on qualitative, manual image assessments, which lack the precision, reproducibility, and scalability that automated, quantitative analysis can provide._
_To bridge this gap, this workshop is designed to empower researchers with the necessary foundational skills to start confidently analysing their data. Participants will be introduced to open-source tools such as FIJI and Ilastik, and to methodologies for automating their image analyses, thus enhancing research precision, efficiency, and reproducibility._

cards.name: 2026-02-04/05 - Imaris
cards.originalDesc:
Hi Stefania/Sara

Just forwarding this on to you as Todd is away and I'm not entirely sure what the plan is regarding the below. I think Georgia mentioned it to me briefly during CBIAS and I'm not entirely sure what I said in response. 

From: Georgia Golfis <georgia.golfis@oxinst.com>
Sent: 08 December 2025 09:30
To: Todd Fallesen <Todd.Fallesen@crick.ac.uk>
Cc: David Barry <David.Barry@crick.ac.uk>; Camille Charoy <camille.charoy@crick.ac.uk> Subject: Imaris training 02.2026 organisation

External Sender: Use caution.

Hello,

I hope this message finds you well.

I am writing to you regarding the Imaris training scheduled for February. I've booked my travel and accommodation. 

I would suggest that we proceed with a fully customised training. Please find below a proposed agenda for your consideration. Naturally, feel free to adjust it as you see fit. 

Kindly let me know:

  *   The final agenda and timing
  *   The expected number of participants
  *
Proposed training plan
Thursday, 5 February
13:00-14:00: General presentation
14:00-14:30: Break - during this time, participants should provide me their images in .ims format via USB drive, or alternatively, you may gather all images in advance so that I can copy them onto my device 14:30-16:30: Review and discussion of all images to agree on appropriate image analysis workflows 

Friday, 6 February
10:00-12:30: Demonstration of the tools required for each participant's image analysis 12:30-13:30: Lunch break
13:30-16:30: Hands-on image analysis by participants, with my support available as needed 

I look forward to hearing from you.
Kind regards,
Georgia
